title: Duke braces for impact of federal loan caps on graduate students
subtitle: Starting July 1, the Trump administration will eliminate an uncapped borrowing program for graduate students. The new caps often come nowhere near close to the cost of attendance for Duke programs.
author: Dylan Halper
text: Starting next month, the Trump administration will impose new limitations on graduate federal loans, leaving Duke’s graduate schools and prospective students in uncertain financial territory. The new regulations , coupled with provisions from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act , radically upend the current borrowing system for graduate students. On July 1, new borrowers will no longer be able to take out loans through the Graduate PLUS program, which permits current graduate students to borrow up to their programs’ cost of attendance. Now, for all but three Duke graduate degrees, new borrowers will only be able to take out up to $20,500 in federal loans per year. The Trump administration raised that cap to $50,000 for a small set of “professional” degrees, with the only Duke programs qualifying being law, medicine and the Master of Divinity. The changes will “lead to a lot of questions about where students should look for funding next,” Miranda McCall, associate vice provost and director of the Karsh Office of Undergraduate Financial Support, wrote in a May 27 email to The Chronicle. According to McCall, Duke graduate and professional students borrowed over $133 million in federal loans during the 2024-25 academic year. Once the new regulations take full effect and existing students can no longer take advantage of Grad PLUS, McCall expects almost half of that money will shift to the private lending market. For students admitted to most Duke programs, the $20,500 cap is far from the cost of attendance. For instance, the estimated cost of attendance for the doctor of physical therapy (DPT) program in Duke’s School of Medicine is over $85,000 a year. Kyle Covington, president of the American Physical Therapy Association and director of educational innovation and of postprofessional education in Duke’s DPT program, worried that the caps might restrict access to physical therapy programs at a time when physical therapists are needed most. In 2022, researchers projected a shortfall of over 12,000 physical therapists in the United States. “We are concerned what this means for the future of our workforce in physical therapy,” Covington said. “... Our students and future students will either turn to higher-cost private loans or choose not to enter the field at all.” These worries are not limited to physical therapy. Michael Relf, dean of the Duke School of Nursing, shared that nurse practitioner and nurse anesthetist programs could be severely impacted, with the new cap coming “nowhere near to the cost of attendance.” Last year, Duke’s estimated tuition for first-year nurse anesthetists was $99,000. Although Relf was unsure whether fewer students will start to enroll in Duke’s nursing programs or whether more will shift to the private market, he said the caps could not come at a worse time. Nurse anesthetists will be particularly impacted, Relf noted, as around 80% of anesthesia care in the United States is delivered by nurses, and there is already a shortage in these roles. “We were concerned both from the educational perspective as to what [the caps] would mean for students coming in, but from a health system perspective, what that means for our future workforce,” said Brian Lee, director of government relations for Duke Health. Not only might underserved communities receive fewer providers under the new regulations, but students from these backgrounds may struggle to access a Duke education. Although supplementing federal loans for private ones can lead to lower interest rates for some students, McCall wrote, this is only true for borrowers with a good credit score. She noted that many students do not have long credit histories or a co-signer, potentially leading to similar or higher interest rates than before. Jeff Harris, executive director of the Office of Government Relations and Duke in DC, explained that those from a lower socioeconomic status might face more challenges obtaining reasonable private loans, as they frequently have less favorable credit histories. However, the exact impact still remains to be seen. “Will we see the same number enter but have to use those higher-cost loans, or will we see a drop in those that enter?” Covington asked. Depending on the answer, Duke could face revenue struggles. If enrollment were to decline, the University’s revenue would likely take a hit as well. The Trump administration justified the elimination of Grad PLUS and the new caps — which were estimated to save the federal government $44 million — by claiming that unfettered borrowing access permitted universities to perpetually raise their cost of attendance. “This final rule will help ensure students can access higher education without racking up excessive loan debt, offer repayment options that better serve borrowers and force institutions to reduce costs,” said Under Secretary of Education Nicholas Kent. Duke officials were skeptical. “It’s not as easy as if you get rid of federal financial aid, the cost of tuition is going to go down,” Relf said, noting that training healthcare providers is often costly as a multiyear, time-intensive experience. Covington agreed, noting that while his organization supports decreasing students’ financial burden, “restricting access to lower-cost federal loans without addressing the underlying reasons ... just shifts the burdens on the students and communities.” Apart from funding concerns, Relf and Covington also said that removing the “professional” label from their occupations sends an inaccurate message about what nurses and physical therapists do. “It’s been confusing to a lot of people ... because we are universally trained at the doctoral level,” Covington said. Although the changes’ exact consequences on Duke are still unclear, the University is working to prepare graduate students for the new financial landscape. McCall wrote that her office is “stepping up” its financial counseling and building it into the pathway for students applying for private loans. It is also holding information sessions and created an online page dedicated to answering questions about the new regulations. Federal rules prevent the Office of Student Loans & Personal Finance from recommending a single private lender, but the office does maintain a recommended lender list to provide students with options that “meet high standards for service, rates and terms,” McCall wrote. For the School of Medicine and the School of Nursing, next steps are still uncertain, depending on how enrollment and financial aid trends play out the next few semesters. Since current students can still access Grad PLUS loans for a maximum of three academic years, it might take time to feel the impact of the changes. “Our focus is on supporting students by providing financial aid, counseling and guidance on available funding options. We will continue to evaluate how best to support students as these changes take effect,” Duke School of Medicine officials wrote in a May 26 statement to The Chronicle. Relf noted that fundraising for additional scholarships will continue to be a priority for the School of Nursing. As a first-generation college graduate himself, he said the issue is personal. “We want students to have a world-class education, regardless of their socioeconomic status,” Relf said. Although the changes are intended to take effect in just a few weeks, Harris and Lee explained that their office will continue to engage with congressional representatives to share the potential impact of the regulations on Duke and prospective students. They said that although a full reversal is unlikely, there has been bipartisan pushback in Congress and a number of lawsuits filed. In particular, North Carolina joined a coalition of 24 other Democratic-led states and the District of Columbia to sue over the rules in May, challenging the government’s restriction of the higher $50,000 cap to a select number of programs. Last week, a U.S. House of Representatives committee advanced a bipartisan amendment to a funding bill that would put nursing programs under the $50,000 cap. “While the final rule has been published ... our advocacy is continuing on this issue,” Lee said.

title: Duke finalizes student insurance after initial draft criticized for limited abortion coverage, provider options
subtitle: A petition signed by over 800 Duke community members expressed concern that Duke’s new student health insurance plan, to be managed by Aetna instead of Blue Cross Blue Shield, would limit student healthcare options.
author: Sarah Diaz
text: When Duke announced last month that it would switch student health insurance plan carriers from Blue Cross Blue Shield to Aetna, some students were caught off guard. Alongside the announcement, an initial draft of the Aetna plan was published, which raised concerns for both graduate and undergraduate students about whether their healthcare options would now be more limited. In particular, students took issue with provisions favoring a small set of healthcare providers and excluding coverage for most abortion care. In response, Duke Graduate Student Union drafted a petition demanding changes to the insurance plan. The petition was signed by over 800 Duke community members, including graduate and undergraduate students, alumni and faculty, and co-endorsed by several additional organizations, including the Duke chapter of Planned Parenthood, Sunrise Duke and the Duke Faculty Union, said Riley Wolfe, a doctoral student in history and an organizing co-chair of DGSU. “Many aspects of this new plan present serious inequities for our University, especially to those workers who are the most vulnerable: those with chronic illnesses, those with children and dependents, and those in need of critical reproductive care,” the petition said . Then, on June 8, the same day the petition was delivered to administrators, Duke released a finalized version of the Aetna plan. Although the new plan seemed to directly address some of the concerns laid out by students — for example, it now provides coverage for abortion care — it left other worries unresolved. Rezin Davis, Student Affairs’ director of marketing and communications, wrote in a June 8 email to The Chronicle that the shift from Blue Cross Blue Shield to Aetna was part of the University’s annual review of student medical insurance plans, a process intended “to help manage rising healthcare costs while continuing to provide strong coverage for students.” The announcement described Aetna as bringing “a wealth of student insurance experience,” citing more than 40 years of experience serving colleges and universities. Aetna had already been the carrier for Duke students’ dental coverage and Davis added that the insurance company “offers resources and support specifically designed for student populations.” However, this didn’t stop students’ concerns. For one, compared to the Blue Cross Blue Shield plan, the new Aetna plan incentivizes students to use certain in-network providers, known as Select Care services. These Select Care providers are Duke Health, WakeMed and THN-Cone Health Network, although the initial plan only included Duke Health and WakeMed in the Select Care category. Practically, this means higher costs for in-network providers that are not one of those three. With Blue Cross Blue Shield, there were no individual or family deductibles for any in-network providers. But, under the new Aetna plan, while deductibles remain $0 for Select Care providers, other in-network providers now carry a deductible of $250 for individuals and $500 for families. The deductible is waived for in-network providers for certain healthcare services such as a routine physical exam. The difference between Select Care and in-network providers extends to copay costs as well. A physician’s office visit at a Select Care facility only requires a $25 copay, but this price jumps to $35 at other in-network providers. Likewise, an urgent care visit costs $45 with Select Care, but rises to $90 for other in-network providers. For students with chronic illnesses, the distinction between Select Care and other in-network providers might make it more costly to access specialist care unique to specific facilities. Abi Human, a graduate student in the Nicholas School of Environment who lives with a chronic condition, described the difficulties of finding and receiving appropriate treatment. “A lot of times, there is really only a couple doctors who can handle your issue, and they may not be in the Duke healthcare system,” Human said. She shared that the doctor she regularly sees will likely not qualify for Select Care coverage pricing. “I kind of have to choose between what I can afford and what I need for my health,” Human said. The provision could also impact those studying at the Duke Marine Lab in Beaufort, 180 miles away from the University’s main campus. According to Human, these students lack easy access to Duke or WakeMed hospitals and as a result could either pay higher premiums for nearby services, or travel several hours for lower-priced coverage. Even though the final draft added THN-Cone Health to the list of Select Care providers, which Wolfe said was an improvement and helped mitigate student concerns, the options are still limited for Marine Lab students in particular, he added. The petition also took issue with what students saw as missing areas of coverage in the initial plan, primarily a restriction on when abortion care would receive student insurance coverage. Under the Blue Cross Blue Shield plan, benefits for abortion were provided “as allowed by state law for all members.” However, the initial draft of the new Aetna plan excluded both abortion pills and procedures as eligible health services, except in cases of rape, incest or “if it places the woman’s life in serious danger.” In the finalized draft released last week, abortion care will now be covered for students beyond those exceptions. At Select Care providers, Aetna will cover 80% of the cost, dropping to 70% at other in-network providers and 60% for out-of-network ones. The petition also expressed concern about other healthcare areas that received less significant coverage in the new plan than under Blue Cross Blue Shield. Most of these, including vision benefits and mental healthcare, were subsequently included in the final version. For Human, the draft’s initial exclusion of most abortion coverage was worrying, claiming it would make abortion care “virtually inaccessible.” Davis attributed the initial exclusion of abortion care to the draft’s incomplete nature. “Some concerns raised in recent weeks were based on an incomplete draft document that was shared as part of a required review process before benefits were finalized,” Davis wrote. Wolfe was skeptical that the initial exclusion of most abortion coverage was temporary. He believes that the union’s intervention and student outcry prompted the changes seen in the final version. “If you really look at what has changed between those plans, they are things that were in the petition,” Wolfe said. “... Elective abortion coverage, the vision coverage, the expanded network, the mental health copay, these are really specific things that were in our petition.” After the release of the finalized draft, the union met with University administrators June 12 to discuss the new Aetna plan and address their remaining concerns, which include the possibility of a transition period amid the switch to Aetna, removing or expanding visit limits for services like physical therapy and gynecology, and working to ensure accessible coverage for Marine Lab students. Wolfe expressed gratitude for the union’s opportunity to have “a seat at the table,” saying it was not something Duke was obligated to offer them. He said that administrators promised to further communicate with Aetna and student representatives to provide further clarification on the plan’s provisions.

title: Student Affairs announces major restructuring, brings back dean of students position
subtitle: Mary Pat McMahon, vice provost and vice president of student affairs, announced the restructure. With several key Student Affairs roles vacant amid the University’s strategic realignment and cost-cutting initiatives, McMahon wrote that the time was right to consider changes.
author: Dylan Halper, Ella Moore
text: Duke Student Affairs senior leadership will undergo a significant restructuring, with the dean of students position reconsolidated into a single role after nearly two years of being split into three. Mary Pat McMahon, vice provost and vice president of student affairs, announced the changes Wednesday and Thursday in emails to Student Affairs partners and students. With several key Student Affairs roles vacant amid the University’s strategic realignment and cost-cutting initiatives, McMahon wrote that the time was right to consider changes. “The Division remains guided by our goal of ensuring every Duke student feels they truly belong here at Duke,” McMahon wrote. “... As the world around us changes in many ways, the Division of Student Affairs needs to iterate with you all while also sustaining the distinctively impactful, signature elements of what it means to be a Blue Devil,” McMahon wrote. McMahon also told The Chronicle that the restructuring offers a chance for Student Affairs to serve a more “comprehensive” student body from undergraduates to graduate and professional students, considering different “generational needs” and an increased focus on building “healthy habits.” The biggest change is to the dean of students role, which will transition from three positions to one. McMahon wrote that Student Affairs is currently conducting a national search for an associate vice president and dean of students, with a name expected by early fall. After former Dean of Students John Blackshear left Duke in January 2024, Stacey Harris was announced to replace him that summer. However, for unspecified reasons, Harris did not assume the role , and Student Affairs divided the position’s responsibilities among a three-person leadership team. The new, singular dean of students role will be responsible for “a more focused portfolio” than in the past but will still oversee a vast array of programs. These include QuadEx, DukeReach, New Student & Family Programs, the undergraduate Time Away Office and the Office of Student Conduct and Community Standards, among others. The position will also have a new responsibility overseeing Student Involvement and Leadership, which supervises student organizations and fraternity and sorority life, and the Duke International Student Center. McMahon wrote that these were added to the position’s purview due to strategic realignment efforts initiated last summer. The current Dean of Students team — Clay Adams, Jeanna McCullers and Libby Skiles — will transition to new roles. Adams will now serve as assistant vice president for student affairs’ external relations. His position differs slightly from McCullers and Skiles in that he will work under the new dean of students as well as directly with McMahon on development and fundraising. “He has the institutional knowledge, policy, familiarity, process, experience that the next dean is going to need, but he’s also going to have this fundraising piece, external relations ... that’s beyond the dean of students' scope,” McMahon said. Adams will supervise fundraising and development for Student Affairs and manage external projects that involve students, such as the new HomeGrown partnership with Durham, as well as Student Affairs’ partnerships with other offices on campus. He also will focus on expanding the University's support for veteran programs, military affiliates and students with military experience. McCullers will serve as the assistant vice president for strategic initiative and policy. In this position, she will maintain her focus on student policy, which she largely handled as assistant vice president in the Dean of Students' office. Although she will work on broader risk management matters, the new dean of students will be responsible for individual student conduct cases. Skiles will maintain her current title as assistant vice president of student wellbeing, continuing to supervise Student Health, student insurance and Counseling and Psychological Services. She will also co-lead a new team focused on evaluating “student flourishing” on campus. McMahon described the concept of “flourishing” versus “just getting by” as emerging nationwide in the realm of higher education, with institutions such as Harvard University focused on the idea. The University hopes to take a more community-driven approach to this concept, with Skiles’ task force working to “take AI, take belonging, take how people spend time face to face,” and use data-driven practices to create a thriving Duke community, according to McMahon. Beyond the dean of students position, Student Affairs is also conducting an ongoing national search for an associate vice president for resource administration. That role — held by Chris Rossi until the end of May — will remain largely the same: supervising Student Affairs’ finances, budgeting, capital planning and similar domains. McMahon said that she was “pretty confident” that her team would fill these two positions during the summer. Once those searches are complete, Student Affairs will recruit to fill the position of assistant vice president of student affairs and Fannie Mitchell executive director of the Career Center, following Greg Victory’s departure in May. Rachel Coleman is serving in the role on an interim basis during the transition period. McMahon wrote that Student Affairs expects to have a job posting by Sept. 1, and told The Chronicle she has just begun discussions with stakeholders regarding the position. Finally, Student Affairs is also looking for a new chief of staff after Margot Cardamone left in May to join Duke Global as global initiatives lead. The chief of staff position, which oversees operations and communications for Student Affairs, has been posted and applications are being reviewed on an ongoing basis. McMahon wrote that Student Affairs welcomes feedback and encouraged community members to share thoughts about the transition with Senior Advisor Debbie Heida.

title: ‘A leader in health AI’: School of Medicine Dean talks integration of artificial intelligence and healthcare
subtitle: At the annual State of the School Address on Wednesday, Dr. Mary Klotman overall took an optimistic tone, expressing excitement about the School of Medicine’s efforts to integrate AI throughout much of what they do. However, she also recognized the challenges AI presents for the medical field specifically.
author: Luca Gragnoli
text: Dr. Mary Klotman, dean of the Duke School of Medicine and executive vice president for health affairs at the University, discussed the future of health discovery, education and care amid the rise of artificial intelligence at the annual State of the School Address on Wednesday. The address, delivered in the Great Hall at the Trent Semans Center for Health Education, took an optimistic tone overall, expressing excitement about the School's efforts to integrate AI throughout much of what they do. However, Klotman, School of Medicine ‘80, also recognized the challenges AI presents for the medical field specifically. “We have an opportunity to be a leader in health AI, but the leader in responsible, ethical and safe AI,” Klotman said. She pointed to examples of Duke’s own practitioners already employing AI in their research, leading to discoveries such as advanced medical imaging, enhanced drug effectiveness and interpreting data to predict mental illness in young patients. “I think the potential for discovery and implementation is just enormous,” Klotman said. She also acknowledged the role of Chris Lindsell, vice dean of the School of Medicine for data science and AI, in strategizing the implementation of AI across the School. Lindsell assumed the role in April as its inaugural appointee. Part of AI’s integration into the School will be curriculum “reforms” that will teach students how to “lead in a world where AI will be fully integrated” into their professions. “We are all students in AI,” Klotman said. A vision for integrating AI and education connects back to the School’s mission surrounding patient care, Klotman said. The goal is ultimately to support clinicians in delivering better outcomes through improved efficiency and precision. “This is about enabling people to focus more of their time on the work that matters, whether it's research, teaching, patient care,” she said. In a video recording, Dr. Aditee Narayan, vice dean of the School of Medicine for medical and health professions education, shared a plan for implementing “ethical” and “professional” AI usage into the medical curriculum, including the development of large language models to process information from students’ patient notes to inform their learning. However, alongside the potential for implementation in medical practice and research, Klotman recognized concerns about how AI might minimize personal connections in medical care and education. “Our profession is human-to-human,” she said, “[We must] make sure we don't risk that fundamental connection that is so important.” Klotman also contextualized these advancements within the broader landscape of the School of Medicine, which has faced a difficult year and a half amid major federal funding cuts and Duke’s strategic realignment effort . “We have not lost sight of keeping very vigilant in watching what is coming out of our federal funders.” Klotman said. But, “it would be a huge mistake to be distracted and lose sight of strategy ... where the opportunities, particularly with technology and AI, to advance our research mission [are] enormous.” Klotman identified community partnerships as a potential way to alleviate concerns about the adoption of AI within the School. “There is a tremendous amount of mistrust in what these tools might do harm to in terms of patient care, a certain level of fear about what these tools might mean for them, so working with our community partners is probably more important than ever,” Klotman said. She offered examples of a number of collaborative successes, such as the Duke Clinical and Translational Science Institute’s Seed Health Atlas , an AI-powered tool that helps community members and researchers understand health factors across the nation. Klotman closed by sharing a compilation of Duke Health’s recent accomplishments, including consequential innovations in the fields of cancer research and medical education. “I'm excited about what we'll accomplish together and confident in our ability to lead with purpose, integrity and impact,” Klotman said. “What we touch today is the tip of a very large iceberg.”

title: DukeHub requires VPN for off-campus access after IT Security Office alerted to potential ‘active attack’
subtitle: The move to require a VPN connection to access the platform comes “out of an abundance of caution.” Camille Jackson, director of communications for the Office of Information and Technology, wrote that Duke has seen no indications of a risk to student, faculty or staff’s data or accounts.
author: Dylan Halper
text: Update June 13: As of June 12, users no longer require a Duke VPN to access DukeHub or DKUHub. Below is the original story. Duke recently started requiring off-campus users seeking to access DukeHub or DKUHub to use a VPN — or virtual private network — after the Information Technology Security Office received indications of an “active attack targeting certain systems.” According to Camille Jackson, director of communications for the Office of Information and Technology, Duke has seen no indications of a risk to student, faculty or staff’s data or accounts. “Out of an abundance of caution, we have taken the proactive and precautionary steps necessary to enhance security protections for our systems and data while we continue to assess the situation,” Jackson wrote in a Wednesday email to The Chronicle. The VPN requirement went into effect Tuesday at 1:56 p.m. Jackson wrote that a “trusted partner” had alerted the IT Security Office to the potential attack. Jackson explained that a VPN “provides an additional layer of protection for both DukeHub and the individuals who use it.” For those that do not have Duke’s VPN, the OIT website maintains instructions to help download, install and set up the service. Although a Wednesday email from the Office of the University Registrar said that if possible, one should download and install the VPN client while still on campus, Jackson wrote that those off campus can still do so, but must first log in to a separate service with their NetID. Over the summer, the primary use for DukeHub among students is for course registration. Shopping carts for first-years opened last week for fall registration, which begins July 29. All other grades can currently use DukeHub for drop/add for both the fall and Summer Session II. Duke most recently experienced a cyber threat last month when it was one of 9,000 schools impacted by a cyberattack on Instructure, the parent company of the online learning platform Canvas. After Instructure struck a deal with the hackers to remove them from the system, Chief Information Security Officer Nick Tripp wrote in a May 12 email that his office had “credible evidence” that affected data included names, contact information, Canvas internal usernames, enrollment information, course-related data and academic term information. At the time, Tripp wrote that Duke had “reinforced its digital security” for Canvas integration with other services and was continuing to monitor the situation closely. Users can access Canvas through DukeHub, though there has been no public indication that the current attack is related to the May incident. Jackson wrote that since this is a developing situation, OIT is still assessing details. She added that if appropriate, more information will be shared with the Duke community, but their office has to be cautious as publicly disclosing information while investigations are underway can sometimes “provide useful information to malicious actors.” OIT said they will provide an update if a VPN connection is no longer required to access DukeHub.

title: Durham County passes $1B budget, hikes property taxes, increases public school funding by $14 million
subtitle: Board chair Mike Lee described the approval of the fiscal year 2026-27 budget as “not easy,” adding that this year’s budget discussions were “passionate, sometimes uncomfortable and always necessary.”
author: Ella Moore
text: The Durham Board of County Commissioners unanimously approved a $1.05 billion operating budget Monday, including a property tax hike of 2.5 cents and a $14.55 million funding increase for Durham Public Schools. Board chair Mike Lee described the approval of the fiscal year 2026-27 budget as “not easy,” adding that this year’s budget discussions were “passionate, sometimes uncomfortable and always necessary.” Although Durham County has annually increased funding for the school district by over $10 million the last six years, DPS has contended with faltering government support , dropping enrollment, the rise of charter school competition and educator demands for better pay. Without a new state budget , the county has struggled to fill education funding gaps. “We are balancing our residents’ ability to pay [property taxes] also with pressures that we’re having to absorb, because our federal and state partners have shifted how they support the organization,” County Manager Claudia Hager said at Monday’s meeting. In late March, the DPS Board of Education requested $252 million from the county — a $28 million increase from the previous budget. The Durham Association of Educators, the main union representing DPS teachers and staff, asked the county to raise property taxes by 4 cents to fulfill the funding request, according to INDY Week. Vice Chair Nida Allam and Commissioner Michelle Burton proposed an additional 0.25-cent increase to the 2.5-cent raise the county commissioners planned to vote on. But, Lee, alongside commissioners Wendy Jacobs and Stephen Valentine, firmly opposed a tax hike exceeding 2.5 cents. Property taxes support around 60% of the county’s operating budget, and previous tax increases have led to a rise in commercial property funding appeals that made a dent in the City of Durham’s finances. The 2.5 cents property tax increase, sets the county rate at 57.92 cents per $100 of assessed value. According to real estate platform Zillow, the average home value in the Durham municipal area was around $400,000, which would mean a county property tax of $2,316, or an increase of around $100. City of Durham residents also have to pay property taxes to the city government, but the City Council does not plan to raise property taxes in its budget which it's set to vote on next week. Although DAE requested a minimum wage increase from $17.15 to $19.22 for classified workers, or non-teaching, support staff, Lee said that the $14.55 million funding increase for DPS would amount to an $18.22 wage. Commissioners said that the county would try to meet the full demand next year. Though Jacobs acknowledged the “heartbreaking stories” and financial need of DPS classified workers, “the problem is that we have other heartbreaking needs happening,” she said. DPS faces a tough year ahead. North Carolina recently adopted a federal school choice tax credit program that supports enrollment in charter and private schools, meaning DPS will likely lose more funding at the state level as it is increasingly allocated toward charter and private school enrollment. Based on December 2025 data from the Education Law Center, North Carolina spends around $12,000 per student in combined local and state funding, ranking it last in the United States. According to WRAL, the county’s increase brings the local per-pupil funding to $5,998, and with additional state funding stalled, the financial support remains far below the national average. Besides navigating DPS funding gaps, Durham County is tasked with overseeing the county jail, emergency services and various social programs. The county raised its budget by 1.09% overall, building on the county manager’s proposed $1.045 billion budget by $4.33 million. The additional funding will include contributions to a 2% cost of living increase in Durham County employee salaries, a $1.5 million increase in county health insurance costs and several new staff positions in public safety departments. The board also allocated funding increases for Durham Technical Community College — which launched a new admissions partnership with Duke this year — and continued funding for the Hayti Reborn Justice Movement, which runs several programs to reduce recidivism for Durham residents. The budget will take effect July 1. The county plans to provide more detailed documentation of the complete budget in the coming weeks.

title: DukeEngage students were ready to spend the summer in Uganda. Then Ebola broke out
subtitle: After Duke’s Global Travel Advisory Committee barred students from traveling to Uganda, DukeEngage participants relocated to Durham for long-distance service.
author: Mia Taubenblat
text: DukeEngage Uganda was relocated to Durham following an outbreak of a rare type of Ebola in the country and neighboring Congo. The program — set to start June 6 — had to shift course after the University’s Global Travel Advisory Committee officially restricted travel to Uganda on May 18. Students were given less than 48 hours to decide whether or not to continue with the program in Durham, with seven of nine electing to do so. Megan Madonna, DukeEngage Uganda’s program leader and an assistant research professor in the department of biomedical engineering, worked to adjust the program to ensure that student participants could still collaborate with engineering students from Makerere University in Kampala, Uganda, like they had done in years past. The only difference is that the students in each country will now be thousands of miles apart. “This program is all about design with context and constraints, and we are all experiencing this in real time as we pivot [the program],” Madonna said. “There’s a bit of metacognition going on.” According to Associate Director of DukeEngage Inga Peterson, DukeEngage has never had to cancel a program before for public health reasons. Although programs have previously been canceled due to regional conflict, it’s extremely rare that programs have the opportunity to pivot to a new location. Madonna first heard about the Ebola outbreak in an alert from International SOS, a health and security service firm, but hoped it was an isolated incident. She realized that the program might be in jeopardy when the World Health Organization declared a public health emergency of international concern May 17 after two Ebola cases were documented in Uganda. The outbreak is the result of the rare Bundibugyo virus, which does not have an approved vaccine or treatment like the virus responsible for previous Ebola outbreaks. As of June 6, Uganda has reported 19 confirmed cases, including two deaths. The day after the declaration, Duke’s Office of Global Health and Safety notified Madonna that they would not allow students to travel to Uganda. “I’m sad for our collaboration. This is something that’s really special to Duke engineering, and it’s gone on for over 10 years at this point. My biggest hope was to keep this collaboration strong through this,” Madonna said. The fastest and most efficient way for DukeEngage to relocate the program was to rely on a place known to students, faculty and families. Duke’s campus is home to multiple makerspaces, Madonna said, and undergraduates and their families are already familiar with Durham. For sophomore Ana Meza, the news that the program would not be in Uganda less than a month before its start date was frustrating, given that she had put her energy into applying for DukeEngage and committed to the program in November. Yet, Meza still agreed to participate in the program in Durham, saying it felt like a “cool opportunity” for her to work on a project under Madonna, whom she described as a great professor. Although she did not know exactly what the program would look like, since the program directors needed to gauge interest before planning details, Meza heard from other students that they were sticking with the program and she did not want to lose the opportunity to bond with them. “That’s pretty amazing that [the students] are willing to do this, because I know it’s so far from ideal,” Peterson said. “But they’re making this commitment, and we will support them as much as we can.” In past summers, student participants have worked alongside Makerere students to identify unmet needs in local hospitals through visits and designing solutions together in a local makerspace. Peterson noted the tangible difference that students’ innovations make in Ugandan healthcare and the relationship that Duke and Makerere students build with each other. However, the difference in time zones present a new challenge for the cross-university collaboration, with Kampala currently seven hours ahead of Durham. As a result, the program’s model of joint work during the day and team bonding in the evenings had to be flipped. While Duke students are asleep, Makerere students will begin the design process in Kampala and then break for lunch. Then, all students will come together for a four-hour session of co-building and team bonding so students can get to know each other personally. After that, Duke students will have lunch and get back to design work in the afternoon. Zoom will be used for some of the synchronous sessions, and troubleshooting will occur as the program progresses, Madonna added. During a typical summer in Uganda, students live together in one house in Kampala, cooking meals and sharing quality time as a cohort. But with Duke students in Durham, Madonna hopes to exchange recipes between the groups to keep that friendly spirit alive. “It'll be interesting to see what collaboration looks like when you're not physically together,” Meza said. Both Madonna and Peterson lauded DukeEngage Uganda for being able to pivot the program so quickly. “The fact that we are able to continue this program, in a way that we’re not usually able to, I think speaks volumes on how people are trying to commit to working on these challenges,” Peterson said.

title: Professor Emeritus Cecil Borel remembered as pioneer, inaugural leader of Duke neuroanesthesiology
subtitle: During his nearly 20 years at the University, Borel, 75, helped found Duke's neurosurgical intensive care unit and established one of the first acute care nurse practitioner programs in the country.
author: Luca Gragnoli
text: Professor Emeritus Dr. Cecil Borel III is remembered as a key figure in the field of neurocritical care who helped establish Duke’s world-renowned neuroanesthesiology department and neurosurgical intensive care unit. Borel, 75, died May 2. “His legacy of innovation and collaboration continues to influence the standard of care today,” wrote Stacey Hilton, director of communications for the department of anesthesiology at the School of Medicine, in a statement announcing Borel’s passing. Borel earned his medical degree from Drexel University in 1977 and achieved a dual board certification in internal medicine from the Commonwealth University of Pennsylvania and in anesthesiology from Johns Hopkins University. He also completed a fellowship in critical care medicine at the University of Maryland. In a 2020 issue of the BluePrint, Duke anesthesiology’s annual publication, Borel recounted that the challenge of anesthesiology is what drew him to the field. “Ultimately, I enjoyed anesthesiology for what it is itself, the ability to make very difficult procedures possible; anesthetizing patients and bringing them back safely — safely being the key,” Borel said. Borel began his career at Johns Hopkins, where he helped establish the university’s neurosciences critical care unit — one of the first of its kind in the world. In 1993, Borel joined Duke as an associate professor of anesthesiology, establishing the neuroanesthesiology program and a neurosurgical ICU. When neuroanesthesia became an independent division in 1997, Borel became its inaugural leader. He retired in 2012, after nearly 20 years at Duke, becoming a professor emeritus. During his tenure, Borel’s most significant achievements were his expansion of the Neuro-ICU through the strategic recruitment and training of specialists and his multidisciplinary approach to building an ICU team. To make up his unit, Borel gathered experts from internal medicine, neurology, anesthesiology and neurosurgery. Professor of Anesthesiology Nicole Guinn, who is now chief of the neuroanesthesiology division, emphasized Borel’s seminal contributions to the field through his work in the ICU unit. She described in an email to The Chronicle how important Borel was in founding Duke’s Neuro-ICU, a specialized facility dedicated to patients with severe brain and nervous system conditions. His vision helped establish a structured, expert-driven approach to neurological critical care where none had previously existed. Yet, Borel’s impact extends beyond the ICU. In an email to The Chronicle, Professor of Anesthesiology Richard Moon praised Borel’s development of an anesthesia method for sedating patients undergoing awake craniotomies , which allows patients to remain conscious during brain surgery so that surgeons can monitor neurological function in real time and avoid damage to the brain. Moon also lauded Borel's realization of the Duke Neurocritical Care team. Today, the Division of Neurocritical Care includes 14 critical care doctors, many of whom also hold additional areas of specialization in areas like vascular neurology, neuroanesthesiology and neurophysiology. Borel also contributed to the establishment of one of the first acute care nurse practitioner programs in the world. He wanted nurse practitioners to be at the heart of patient care and developed a model to realize that goal. According to Guinn, Borel’s passion for neurocritical care was equal to his commitment to building the field’s future workforce, through the development of important training for nurse practitioners and physician assistants. For his own research in anesthesiology, Borel’s interests centered around critical care for patients with life-threatening neurological illnesses, particularly those that resulted in respiratory dysfunctions. Beyond his medical work, Borel is remembered as an outstanding sailor and boat builder. In 1999, Borel started building the Janetess, a vessel named after his wife Janet. BluePrint described how Borel found “peace in the power of the wind.” His life, as Borel himself told the magazine, was best described by the “marriage between two disparate ideas that fulfilled two of his life’s passions”: his world-class neurocritical care program and his sailboat. “Work can be stressful and building a boat requires exercise, so there was a balance between the two,” Borel told the magazine in 2020. “I’ve learned a lot from the boat, but mainly to persevere.” Borel was also a jazz aficionado, a talented flutist, a former member of a college blues band and an eager painter. “I wouldn’t advise everybody to start building a boat, but don’t forget to take time to have personal interests; allow yourselves to recover, relax and take time for things that make life sweeter,” Borel said in 2020. Duke flags were lowered June 5 in honor of Borel’s life and legacy.

title: Duke HR launches AI chatbot to answer employee questions
subtitle: The chatbot, officially named Ask CHRIS, short for Chat with HR Information Systems, is designed to cohere complex information about HR policies, benefits, programs and more. It comes as part of a broader Duke effort to integrate AI throughout campus life.
author: Dylan Halper
text: Duke Human Resources recently debuted a new “employee,” named CHRIS. However, CHRIS is not a human, but an artificial intelligence chatbot. The chatbot — officially named Ask CHRIS , short for Chat with HR Information Systems — is designed to organize complex information about HR policies, benefits, programs and more, so that University employees can more easily receive answers or be directed to the right office for any work-related questions. “As technology continues to progress, we're always looking at ways to figure out how we can find a way to incorporate it into our normal personal lives as well as our working life,” said Antwan Lofton, vice president of human resources and chief human resources officer. Paul Grantham, assistant vice president for work culture and communication services, explained that the idea for Ask CHRIS emerged around a year ago, in line with the Provost’s Initiative on AI . Since then, Duke has moved to integrate AI throughout campus life, granting undergraduates free, unlimited access to ChatGPT-4o, launching the DukeGPT chatbot and beginning construction on a small data center to enhance research. In the spring, the AI at Duke steering committee released a report calling for the University to cultivate a “unified institutional identity” around AI and seek innovative pathways to remain a nationwide leader on the issue. Ask CHRIS was developed in Microsoft Copilot by Duke Human Resources and the Office of Information Technology. The chatbot’s knowledge base primarily contains information aggregated from the HR website, along with frequently asked questions the HR office receives, since it can be difficult for employees to navigate the HR website themselves to find answers. “There’s so many offices that sit within human resources ... it could take a normal employee anywhere from five to 10 minutes to try to go through the website to find where these areas are,” Lofton said. Ask CHRIS also provides more personalized responses than the HR website by requiring users to log in with their Duke NetID. For example, Grantham said that if an employee wanted to know about their benefit accrual rating, the chatbot could use the general policy and combine it with information about the employee’s department and how long they have worked at Duke to provide the correct answer. “That’s a really helpful piece that I think makes this chatbot different than something like Gemini,” Grantham said. Grantham also noted that there are privacy protections in place to prevent improper use of personal data. He said that the chatbot does not have access to certain information, such as employee salaries, and that Duke’s agreement with Microsoft Copilot requires that any information put into the chatbot does not enter the public domain. Lofton and Grantham said that the introduction of Ask CHRIS is not about taking away existing HR resources but rather should be seen as an “enhancement to all of our jobs.” “This tool allows us to do more of the human relations side of human resources,” Grantham said, adding that most of the time HR employees are responding to “transactional questions” rather than engaging in the “relational” work of consulting, mentoring or providing guidance and support. “This was to add to [what we have], not to take away,” Lofton said. To see how Ask CHRIS worked, The Chronicle conversed with the chatbot. In general, the chatbot responded to HR questions by summarizing the relevant policy, frequently listing steps an employee should take to address their concerns. Most answers also concluded with a link to the policy and contact information for the HR office. By contrast to DukeGPT, which can respond to questions on topics ranging from politics to writing class essays, Ask CHRIS has a much more limited knowledge domain, frequently responding to non-HR-related questions by saying, “I have run into a restriction, can you rephrase the question?” However, like DukeGPT, Ask CHRIS is still susceptible to “ hallucinations ,” the term for when generative AI models produce false results that are either incorrect or simply made up. When asked to name the eateries in the Brodhead Center, it returned names such as “Twisted Taco,” “Tend” and “La Creperie” alongside correct entries. Grantham emphasized that although the tool has gone through months of internal testing, it is still in its pilot phase. He noted that users can provide feedback directly into the chatbot by providing a “thumbs-up” or “thumbs-down” on specific answers. According to Grantham, Ask CHRIS received over 900 queries in the first two weeks of its pilot, and only 2% of responses received a “thumbs-down”. He said that in a “phase two” of the chatbot, it would ideally be integrated with more University systems so that it could answer more complicated questions like how many vacation days an employee has remaining. “This is not the end of [Ask CHRIS], this is just the beginning,” Lofton said.

title: Summer construction at Duke: What to know about closures, delays, major projects
subtitle: Sidewalk restrictions, parking impacts and major renovations are underway across Duke’s campus. Here’s where construction projects stand a month into the summer.
author: Abby DiSalvo
text: Duke Facilities Management is midway through several major construction projects across campus. The work, which ranges from sewer upgrades to complete building overhauls, may impact building access throughout the summer and fall. The Reuben-Cooke Building is roughly halfway through a $78.5 million overhaul , which includes modern offices, new classrooms, the restoration of 513 historic windows and updated utility systems. The 76,500-square-foot building has seen roughly 75 to 120 workers on site each day. The renovation project was funded in part by a $25 million gift from The Duke Endowment, with construction slated to wrap up May 2027. The building has been closed since last June, as have 60 parking spaces in the upper Allen Building lot and sidewalks between Reuben-Cooke and the Social Sciences Building. Evan Heisman, communications and support services manager for Duke Facilities, said a “dedicated green space” will replace roughly 10 parking spaces at the rear of the building. Heisman said that utility enabling and a new penthouse for the building’s mechanical systems had essentially been completed by mid-May. The remainder of the project will include the installation of roof tiles, new HVAC systems and restored original windows, as well as connection to permanent power. The revamp will also add a new lobby display recognizing Wilhelmina Reuben-Cooke, the building’s namesake and one of the first five Black undergraduates admitted to Duke. Reuben-Cooke served as a trustee of Duke and the Duke Endowment after a career as an attorney, professor and senior administrator at Syracuse University and the University of the District of Columbia. Underground utility systems around Cameron Indoor Stadium and surrounding recreational facilities including Card Gym, Wilson Recreation Center and Taishoff Aquatics Center are receiving updates over the summer. According to project manager Ed Sinnott, the work includes replacing the stadium’s original sanitary sewer lines, modernizing stormwater infrastructure and updating connections to power and backup generators. Construction will limit access to pedestrian sidewalks around Krzyzewskiville and Cameron Indoor through September. Stormwater infrastructure around the adjacent buildings will also be modernized, with “limited, targeted work in Krzyzewskiville,” according to Sinnott. Ground-level maintenance and proper drainage are especially important to the K-Ville lawn during basketball season, when students camp out in tents for rivalry basketball tickets. Duke Facilities Management will coordinate with Duke Landscaping Services to restore affected areas upon completion of the project. Cameron Indoor saw expansions and HVAC upgrades in 2001 and 2016, but its utility systems have not been updated since the stadium’s original construction in 1939. As part of a three-phase renovation project , Duke’s baseball program will receive a new 8,000-square-foot facility along the third-base side of Jack Coombs Field. The David Koch Jr. Player Performance Center will feature multiple state of the art pitching, hitting and training labs. Inside the performance center, coaches will be able to monitor a pitcher’s ground force production, biomechanical data and other metrics in real time on a single dashboard. Work on the performance center began in February. According to a May update, current work includes excavation, stormwater improvements, utility connections and new visitor parking. Visitors should expect one-lane traffic and flaggers on Whitford Drive until construction wraps up in November. The move-in phase of the project is scheduled to conclude in December. The final phase of the facility renovation, which does not yet have a timeline, includes an expansion of the performance center for new locker rooms, coaches’ offices, a team lounge and student-athlete workspaces. The Krzyzewski Center for Athletic Excellence — also known as the “K Center” — will soon receive a three-story expansion. New hydrotherapy and training rooms will be placed on the first level as more space is added for the men’s and women’s basketball programs. Work on the building’s stormwater and utility systems has been underway since February in preparation for the project. To make room for the new addition, Duke will demolish Scharf Hall, a 3,746-square-foot event space located in the K Center, beginning in August. Parking spaces in the Sanford School of Public Policy Lot may be limited or unavailable during the Scharf Hall demolition. Parking disruptions are expected to end fall 2027 with construction wrapping up January 2028. Duke recently began construction on a 13,000-square-foot data center near Central Campus. The two-story building, which is forecasted to cost around $40 million and open in the 2027-28 academic year, will be located on the east side of Anderson Street and connect to one of the University’s chilled water plants. Toddi Steelman, vice president and vice provost for climate and sustainability, emphasized that this facility will be significantly smaller than many hyperscale projects making headlines. The design was intentionally aligned with the University’s sustainability goals and Climate Commitment, according to administrators. Tracey Futhey, vice president of information technology and chief information officer, said the facility’s 1.5 megawatt capacity would constitute around a 2% to 3% increase in Duke’s energy usage. Although the site for the data center site is directly next to Central Campus, Vice President of Facilities Tom Morrison said that the project will not interfere with any current or potential future uses for the land, including the Central Campus tent, which has hosted social events by student organizations and off-campus groups over the past two years. A $64 million upgrade to Duke’s East Campus library began in May 2024. Highlights of the renovation include two new entrances, an Innovation CoLab space, a 75-seat auditorium and a cafe with an outdoor terrace. An expansion on the library’s west side has increased the building’s square footage by roughly 75%, and the project is expected to add nearly 200 seats to the building. Although construction on the building’s exterior and built-out shell has been completed, plenty of work remains to be done inside. Before the University can receive a Certificate of Occupancy for the library, it must paint the ceilings, finish the floors, set up shelving, and install the HVAC system and audio/visual wiring. Kelly Lawton, head of East Campus libraries, floated a potential unofficial opening for the upcoming December reading period. Lilly’s official reopening, originally scheduled for summer 2026, has been delayed to spring 2027. While work continues, first-years can access library resources through the Bishop’s House and Music Library on East Campus. Over the summer, a variety of other construction projects will occur on East Campus. Grading – the process of reshaping and leveling ground – will begin on the field between Brodie Recreation Center and Jack Katz Stadium in preparation for the installation of new tennis courts. Brodie will also be connected to Duke’s underground hot water heating system. While those projects are underway, Brodie Gym Drive will occasionally be restricted to one-way traffic. According to a Duke Today update , detours around Brodie and Lilly will be “constant” for both vehicles and pedestrians. The construction that spanned East Campus Quad for much of the last year will wrap up in August. Grading and sodding of central lawns will occur over summer break, but the replacement of stormwater drainage systems, sidewalks, granite curbs and irrigation systems concluded earlier in the academic year. A portion of the south side of Duke University Road will be redone with a new sidewalk and trees, upgraded lighting and stone pillars. New pedestrian signals will also be added to the crosswalk at Anderson Street. The project is scheduled for completion in September 2026. While work is underway, pedestrian access to the site will be limited and temporary lane closures for vehicular traffic may be imposed. Editor's note: This story was updated Friday to reflect that the Reuben-Cooke renovations were never slated to wrap up in fall 2026.

title: How to celebrate 2026 Pride Month in the Bull City
subtitle: From roller rinks to remembrance marches, Durham has a full month of events honoring its LGBTQ+ community. The Chronicle has put together a list of events if you’re looking for ways to celebrate Pride in the Bull City this June.
author: Claire Cranford
text: From roller rinks to remembrance marches, the Bull City has a full calendar of events in honor of Pride Month and the LGBTQ+ community’s hard-won history. Durham’s Pride theme this year is “Always Forward, Never Straight: Preserving History, Coming Home,” which aims to center community and progress while honoring the history of Pride in the Triangle. The Chronicle has put together a list of events if you’re looking for ways to celebrate the month in Durham. Durham’s Pride history runs deep, and this year seeks to honor that. The Museum of Durham History is hosting “To Love and Live Free,” a free exhibit running through Oct. 11. It commemorates the 40th anniversary of the 1986 "Out Today, Out To Stay" Pride march and the grassroots campaign to defend former Mayor Wib Gulley after he signed a proclamation condemning discrimination against gay and lesbian residents. Using archival materials and 18 new oral histories, the exhibit traces LGBTQ+ history from North Carolina's first march for lesbian and gay rights to the organizing battles that shaped today's inclusive Durham. On the morning of June 5, Jewish for Good is hosting a talk featuring Rachel Gelfand, former Duke gender, sexuality and feminist studies instructor, on Jewish LGBTQ+ activists in the South, weaving oral histories from the 1970s and '80s together with contemporary voices. The discussion will touch on a range of topics including the origin of Durham Pride, the role of Jewish activists in LGBTQ+ movements and what makes the South a unique space for queer organizing. Attendance is free for members and $3 for guests. The month closes with the “Our Day Out” Remembrance March & Resource Fair on June 27, commemorating North Carolina's first LGBTQ+ march on June 27, 1981. The march begins at CCB Plaza at 10 a.m. and ends at the LGBTQ Center of Durham, followed by speakers and a resource fair running until 2 p.m. On June 7, head to Kotuku Surf Club for the PINK Triangle PRIDE Kickoff , a free outdoor event featuring live music, DJ sets and performances by drag kings, queens and burlesque artists. The Pinhook has upcoming queer-centered nights to mark on your calendar: the STREET FRUIT Drag + Dance Party on June 20, an evening of monochromatic outfits and prizes, and Queer Icons on June 26, a multi-performer Pride show. The STREET FRUIT event is 21+ and Queer Icons is 18+. For an 18+ night of drag, ALT-PRIDE FEST at the Durty Bull Brewing Company on June 20 features a drag/burlesque competition, featured entertainers and a “Rage Rave” after party. The Durham Bulls will host a Pride Night on June 4 as they play against the Charlotte Knights at Durham Bulls Athletic Park. First pitch is at 6:45 p.m., with tickets starting at $13 and hot dogs for $1. If sports aren’t your thing, head to the Nasher Museum that same evening at 6 p.m. for a gallery guide tour of artworks featuring LGBTQ+ perspectives. Durham's most beloved quirky tradition returns June 6: the Beaver Queen Pageant in Duke Park. Pre-pageant activities including music and skits begin at 4 p.m., with the pageant itself kicking off at 5 p.m. This year's theme is “Beaver Rivalry,” in reference to Crave’s hit queer-show “Heated Rivalry.” A portion of proceeds benefit Keep Durham Beautiful and Friends of Ellerbe Creek, but the event itself is free to attend. On June 13, the LGBTQ Center of Durham hosts OUT On The Block , a casual, family-friendly celebration. The same day, catch the Pride Market at The Glass Jug Beer Lab (RTP location), featuring LGBTQ+ arts and crafts vendors. Over at Pickleback on June 14, Pride Fest runs from 2 to 8 p.m. with local vendors from 2 to 5 p.m. and DJs spinning from 5 to 8 p.m. Tickets start at around $14 and attendees must be 21+. On June 20, Bennett Place hosts OUT & FREE African Dance , a free outdoor class from 10:30 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. in partnership with Baba McDaniel Roberts and The Harambee Collective. Offered in honor of both Pride and Juneteenth, the event features live drumming on the grounds of a North Carolina Historic Site from the Civil War. No dance experience needed, and you can also just watch. Also check out Rollin’ with Pride at Wheels Durham on June 25. The $40 tickets include admission, skate rental, a slice of pizza and a small drink. All ticket revenue supports the local LGBTQ+ community. Cap off the month with the Pride Silent Disco from 7:30 to 11:30 p.m. on June 27 at Hi-Wire Brewing, after the “Our Day Out March.”

title: Duke sued for trademark infringement of ‘Put Up Your Dukes’ phrase
subtitle: The lawsuit, filed by a boxing equipment company in a Florida federal court, claims the University unfairly profited off the usage of the phrase. The suit seeks to permanently bar Duke from using it going forward and pay any damages for profits earned through the alleged infringement.
author: Seyun Park
text: A boxing equipment company filed a lawsuit against Duke last Friday, alleging that the University's use of the phrase "Put Up Your Dukes" constituted trademark infringement and counterfeiting of the company’s trademark. In the lawsuit, KDukesBoxing, which says it is the first premier Black-owned boxing gloves and equipment company, contends that Duke violated federal and state trademark law by using the phrase and wordmark in its marketing. Founded in 2016 in New York by former Marine Kevin Dukes, the company says it has used “Put Up Your Dukes” trademarks for more than nine years. The lawsuit was filed May 29 in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida and seeks a jury trial, as well as a permanent injunction barring Duke from using the phrase, the destruction of all goods Duke currently possesses bearing the phrase and monetary damages for profits earned through the alleged infringement. “Because of [Duke’s] substantial reputation and national market presence, consumers are likely to believe that [KDukesBoxing's] goods and services ... are sponsored, approved or produced by [Duke], rather than the other way around,” the complaint says. “This, in turn, threatens to deprive [KDukesBoxing] of the recognition, distinctiveness and commercial benefit that its incontestable marks are entitled to protect." KDukesBoxing owns two trademarks associated with “Put Up Your Dukes.” The first is a logo trademark that is used on the company’s boxing equipment, featuring two boxing gloves in an uppercut position above the phrase. The lawsuit alleges that Duke’s branding is “confusingly similar” to that mark. The second trademark is a standard-character trademark on the phrase “Put Up Your Dukes,” which the company filed in 2017 and received in 2019. According to the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office, a standard-character trademark “protects the words themselves and is not limited to a particular font style, size or color.” Duke introduced its trademarked Bull City logo in August 2024, describing it in a press release as a symbol that “connects Duke and its home city of Durham.” Although the logo itself does not feature the words “Put Up Your Dukes,” the slogan appeared in promotional materials accompanying the launch. Since then, the University has frequently used the phrase alongside the logo, including on Durham billboards, social media posts, a large sign inside Wallace Wade Stadium and T-shirts sold at Duke University Stores. Duke's Bull City logo can be seen on the Devils Deck in Wallace Wade Stadium. According to the complaint, Duke “has made and will continue to make substantial profits and/or gains to which it is not entitled,” and that continued use of the phrase would cause “irreparable damage to the public and to [KDukesBoxing's] business reputation and goodwill.” Unless the court issues an injunction, the suit claims this includes lost customers, sales and profits. The lawsuit also alleges that Duke was aware of the trademark, saying the University received “express notice” of KDukesBoxing’s rights to the phrase as early as October 2017, but continued using the phrase regardless. Duke has until June 19 to file a response brief with the court. The University did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment on the lawsuit. Usually, Duke is the one suing over its own trademarks. Last year Duke filed 26 complaints with the Patent and Trademark Office and has filed an additional 24 so far in 2026. Trademark infringement claims against the University itself, however, are relatively less common. Duke previously settled a trademark infringement case in 2016 that alleged the cafe in the Divinity School, then called the “Divinity Refectory,” was infringing on Bon Vivant Catering’s trademark “Refectory Cafe.” Duke ended up changing the name to “Divinity Cafe,” and it is now "Zweli's Cafe at Duke Divinity."

title: Durham residents pack City Hall amid budget debate, tensions over housing, policing
subtitle: Dozens of Durham residents filled City Hall Monday to weigh in on the city’s proposed budget, urging investments in violence prevention, housing and community programs while debating public safety priorities. The City Council also approved a $16 million expansion of its contract with Axon Enterprise after a heated discussion over surveillance technology.
author: Lila Cohen
text: Durham City Council faced pressure from dozens of community members on the city’s proposed budget, homelessness plan and renewal of a contract including contentious surveillance technology during its Monday meeting. Residents participated in the second public hearing on the city manager's proposed Fiscal Year 2026-27 budget and Fiscal Year 2027-32 Capital Improvement Plan, which will guide the city through what has been described by council members and City Manager Bo Ferguson as an “unprecedented” year. During the May 18 council meeting, Ferguson proposed a $766.1 million budget, an increase from $722.2 million last year. However, the budget contained “tradeoffs,” with cost cuts alongside increased investments from community partners and direct financial commitments to social problems such as homelessness. Debate over the city’s budget reignited longstanding tensions in Durham, with criminal justice representing a central theme. Residents expressed concerns about violent crime but also highlighted the risks of overpolicing. Community members urged the council to allocate funding toward a variety of causes, from specific requests — like $250,000 for nonprofit group Violence Interrupters — to broader goals such as limiting gun violence and investing in Durham’s youth. Several Durham residents also spoke in support of Legal Aid, sharing how the program helped them navigate the criminal justice system. The City Council is scheduled to adopt the final budget June 15. The council also unanimously passed a five-year plan to make homelessness “rare and brief,” alongside the budget deliberations. According to an impact report by Urban Ministries of Durham, there has been a 300% increase in unsheltered homelessness in the city between 2018 and 2024. As of April 2026, the city’s Homelessness Data Snapshot reported over 1,400 people experiencing homelessness in Durham. The resolution to adopt the strategic framework calls for $13 million in its first year and a goal of reducing unsheltered homelessness by 30% by next June. Should the council also follow Ferguson’s budget proposal, the city will contribute $4.55 million over the next fiscal year toward the goal, while calling on other Durham community partners, including Duke, to pledge additional funds toward the cause. “Our long-term goal is simple,” Ferguson said in his May 18 presentation. “When someone becomes homeless in Durham, they obtain housing within 30 days.” Not all Durham residents were on board. Leslie St Dre worried that the strategic framework prioritized subsidies for private housing development over public investment. “I appreciate that y’all want to focus on a strategy to curb homelessness. I am very concerned that we are talking about $13 million with most of it to subsidize landlords, instead of solutions that go back and stay within the city like more public housing or social housing,” St Dre said. In a 6-1 vote, the council also renewed Durham’s contract with Axon Enterprise, a public safety technology company that supplies the city with body-worn cameras and tasers. The eight-year renewal would cost the city about $16 million. The contract would expand Durham’s partnership with Axon Enterprise to additional services, including a product called Axon Fusus, a platform designed to create a “Real Time Crime Center.” The platform describes the service as a tool that unifies live video, alerts and other surveillance data into one view to allow teams to “act faster and coordinate with clarity.” The council had previously rejected a similar “real time” surveillance technology offered by Peregrine Technologies in February, with some residents voicing concerns over the service’s use of artificial intelligence. Residents voiced similar concerns at Monday’s meeting about pervasive surveillance. Omar Gomez, an organizer with the Nuestro Barrio Liberation Center located in East Durham, said he was “troubled” with the multimillion-dollar contract “to further take away tax dollars from the working class people here in Durham.” Gomez said the contract would lock Durham into a “multi-year savings opportunity agreement” comparable to a “Black Friday sale to increase the power of state surveillance.” The money could instead “go towards hundreds of thousands of families here in Durham who are struggling to afford food, to afford their rent, their groceries for their children” and other social issues, Gomez added. Interim Chief of Police Walter Tate described the contract as “one of the largest contracts [he’s] seen for the police department in 30 years.” However, he said the technology would not be used for “predictive policing,” the practice of using AI algorithms to speculate where and by whom a future crime might occur, but rather would send back a “live feed” that the department can use. “This is not predictive policing. This is not surveillance that we’re using it for. This is to keep citizens safe,” Tate said. In 2023, Durham ranked in the top 10 cities nationally with high homicide rates based on a variety of data. Durham recorded 1,529 violent crimes in 2025, but saw decreases in rape, robbery and aggravated assault from the previous year. The homicide rate in both 2024 and 2025 was unchanged, with 39 total recorded. Durham Mayor Leonardo Williams, who supported the renewed contract, emphasized that the focus should be on those who have lost loved ones to violence. “There are people who are dying on a daily basis,” Williams said. “This is not a freaking joke; this is not another policy to organize around.” “I have no idea how we can pretend that we don’t need these resources,” he added.

title: Proctoring, protests, presidents: 6 stories that shaped higher ed in May
subtitle: The Chronicle recapped six major updates in the world of higher education from the month of May.
author: Lila Cohen, Seyun Park
text: Many colleges and universities wrapped their academic year in May, but the news cycle for higher education did not stop. Although The Chronicle has already covered Harvard University’s decision to cap the number of A’s students can receive and a rare halt in National Science Foundation funding, we are here to recap six other May stories in higher education. For 133 years, proctoring was explicitly banned in Princeton University’s Rules and Procedures of the Faculty. Starting July 1, that will no longer be the case. The previous rules relied on the Honor Code, a system that trusted students to act with integrity and report others in violation. However, with the growing presence of artificial intelligence in college classrooms, that trust has declined. Only one faculty member opposed the initiative, which passed in a faculty meeting May 11. The policy was prepared by Dean of the College Michael Gordin who cited perceptions of widespread cheating as evidence for the demand for proctoring. Now, instructors will remain present for exams to serve “as a witness to what happens.” Isaac Bernstein, a Princeton junior and undergraduate student government academics committee chair, told Princeton Alumni Weekly that a survey he conducted of 806 Princeton students showed that 50.1% were in favor of proctored examinations, while 44.9% were opposed. Graduating students across the country displayed increasingly negative responses to commencement speakers who praised artificial intelligence. At the University of Arizona, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt told graduates that “whatever path you choose, AI will become part of how work is done,” only to be met with a chorus of boos. Schmidt’s response to the jeers was to say, “I know it. Deal with it. Like I said, it's a tool.” Similar incidents occurred at the University of Central Florida and Middle Tennessee State University. “The rise of artificial intelligence is the next industrial revolution,” UCF commencement speaker Gloria Caulfield said before being booed. An April poll conducted by Gallup reported that just 18% of Gen Zers, ages 14 to 29, feel “hopeful” about AI, while 42% reported feeling “anxious” about the technology. According to a fall 2025 Harvard Institute of Politics poll, 59% of 18 to 29-year-olds see AI as a threat to future job prospects. Although Duke’s commencement speaker, Microsoft Chief Financial Officer Amy Hood, Trinity ‘94, did not specifically mention AI in her commencement address , she alluded to the changes the technology will bring. “You’re graduating into a world where the ground is shifting under every institution and industry and every assumption of what a career might look like,” she said. “It’s OK to be scared of that, but you’re walking into this far more prepared than you realize.” On April 30, Cornell University President Michael Kotlikoff was accused of running over a student’s foot as he left a debate on free speech and war in the Middle East. The student, Aiden Vallecillo, was part of a group who had followed Kotlikoff to his car and “surrounded” it after the event to ask about free expression on campus. Kotlikoff’s reversing car also bumped into another student. On May 15, Kotlikoff released a statement in which he rationalized declining to bring disciplinary actions against the students who surrounded his vehicle, saying the ensuing public hearings would “reward the behavior.” The next day, Cornell’s Board of Trustees cleared Kotlikoff of wrongdoing and found the students’ actions to be “inconsistent with university policy.” Then, nearly two weeks later, Vallecillo was delivered a notice by a university police officer informing him he was banned from Cornell’s campus for a year. Vallecillo had graduated from Cornell with a bachelor’s degree in industrial and labor relations May 23. The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill’s Board of Trustees denied tenure to Kiran Asher, former chair of the department of women, gender and sexuality studies at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, despite her going through the standard hiring process. Asher’s hiring received approval from UNC’s women’s studies department, the College of Arts & Sciences and a committee overseeing the tenure process.
