Mayor Leonardo Williams and Council Member Shanetta Burris voiced similar sentiments, with Burris describing the 60-day moratorium as feeling like “60 seconds.” “Durham needs to indefinitely have a moratorium on megawatt data centers,” Williams added. Earlier on Monday, Durham County commissioners discussed plans for their own moratorium, indicating that a joint effort to extend the moratorium could be in the works. Council Member Matt Kopac said that parts of Durham County outside the city itself would likely attract more proposals for data centers, and urged those who spoke at the council hearing to bring the same energy at the county level. “I look forward to a joint, unified approach to addressing this issue for our great city,” Kopac said.

title: Class of 2026 commencement preview
subtitle: The Chronicle is here to celebrate the Class of 2026's accomplishments and speak to their unique experience. Here, you'll find a look back at their years on campus, a gallery of graduation photos and more.
author: Staff Reports
text: The Class of 2026 both started and left Duke at periods of fundamental change for the University. During their first year, the Class of 2026 experienced the introduction of QuadEx and Experiential Orientation, and in their senior year, the University underwent a vast cost-cutting program in response to federal pressure on higher education. In the middle of their time at Duke, they celebrated the University's centennial and witnessed a contentious election cycle. The Chronicle is here to celebrate the Class's accomplishments and speak to the experience that was uniquely theirs ahead of Commencement Sunday. Below, you'll find a look back at their years on campus, along with a commencement preview, a gallery of graduation photos and more. The Class of 2026's first year was one of new beginnings — a year marked by the implementation of QuadEx, the first seasons of two head coaches and the lifting of COVID-19 protocols. Sophomore year saw Duke celebrating its 100th year, demonstrations over the Israel-Hamas war and the effects of the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down affirmative action in higher-education. The Class of 2026’s junior year saw a high-stakes presidential election, a monumental basketball season and the beginning of sweeping changes in higher education. Senior year saw Duke engage in a year of cost-cutting in response to federal policy changes, historic wins for the University’s sports teams and the rise of artificial intelligence. By Luca Gragnoli By Ana Despa By Jackson Kitts Duke Law will host Maurice “Mo” Green, North Carolina Superintendent of Public Instruction, and the economics department will hear from Lorie Logan, the president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas. By Sarah Diaz and Annie Eilers Dianne McIntyre, Cass Sunstein and Abby Wambach will each receive honorary degrees in recognition of their trailblazing careers on May 10. By Seyun Park

title: First Year: 2022-2023
subtitle: The Class of 2026's first year was one of new beginnings — a year marked by the implementation of QuadEx, the first seasons of two head coaches and the lifting of COVID-19 protocols.
author: Staff Reports
text: The Class of 2026's first year was one of new beginnings — a year marked by the implementation of QuadEx, the first seasons of two head coaches and the lifting of COVID-19 protocols. Duke made national headlines this year. Following the Class of 2022’s graduation, The Chronicle reported that the student commencement speech closely resembled a 2014 Harvard student address. In late August, Duke volleyball player Rachel Richardson reported that she was “racially heckled” during a game at Brigham Young University. The incident resulted in the banning, then unbanning of a fan after the school’s investigation did not find evidence of racial slurs directed at Richardson. The University implemented the QuadEx residential program in the fall of 2022, which saw the introduction of Faculty Fellows and the creation of Quad Arches. The Class of 2026 was the first to experience Experiential Orientation, another QuadEx program, which marked an overhaul in orientation programming that cost $1 million more than the traditional Orientation Week experience. The Chronicle spoke with alumni who advocated for housing reform and examined the differences between QuadEx and other peer institutions’ residential systems. At the same time, students had mixed reactions to the new housing system. Under QuadEx’s “all-gender housing model,” the Baldwin Scholars lost their female-only section. The future of selective living groups on campus remained uncertain. Some chapters of Duke-affiliated Greek life were still recovering from the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic while adjusting to QuadEx, and non-Greek SLGs received conflicting messages from the administration regarding recruitment. The University slowly removed COVID-19 protocols that had become a part of campus life. By late September, masks in classrooms were no longer required. By March, masks on buses were no longer required. In April, Duke ended its COVID-19 vaccine requirement for most students, faculty and staff. As a monumental midterm election season came and went, The Chronicle spoke with candidates, voters and journalists before, during and after Election Day. After all the votes were tallied, the Republican Party fell just one seat short of a supermajority in the General Assembly. In April, North Carolina state Rep. Tricia Cotham changed her party affiliation, shifting the supermajority in the Republican Party’s favor. In December, Duke accepted 800 Early Decision applicants, making the 16.5% acceptance rate the lowest in Duke’s history. Duke admitted 4.8% of its Regular Decision applicants in March. The Class of 2026's first year also saw a new dawn for football culture. Under the leadership of new head coach Mike Elko, the ACC Coach of the Year, the Blue Devils ended the regular season 8-4 and defied expectations to become Military Bowl Champions. In December, line monitors announced a pilot program providing financial aid through tenting supplies. Cameron Indoor Stadium was packed once again, the Blue Devils went undefeated at home and Duke completed a sweep of North Carolina after losing to the Tar Heels in the Final Four the previous year. And despite early road struggles and a controversial no-call against Virginia, the Blue Devils rallied to win the ACC Tournament before losing in the second round of the NCAA Tournament. Women’s basketball enjoyed a special season of its own, falling just short of the ACC regular-season title and earning a No. 3-seed for the NCAA Tournament. Men’s lacrosse won the 2023 ACC title after defeating Syracuse in the Blue Devils’ conference finale. Men’s tennis went to the NCAA Sweet 16 for the first time since 2015 after achieving its best-ever ACC record. Duke Athletics wasn’t the only part of Duke that experienced a changing of the guard. Gary Bennett, vice provost for undergraduate education, was named the next dean of Trinity College. Provost Sally Kornbluth was named the next president of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and will be replaced by Alec Gallimore. Suzanne Barbour joined the Graduate School as its new dean in September. Duke also saw several losses. Peter Nicholas, the Nicholas School of the Environment’s namesake, passed in June. John Burness, former senior vice president for public affairs and government relations, passed in December. Samar Zora, a fourth-year doctoral candidate in the cultural anthropology department, passed away in February. Basketball and baseball legend Dick Groat passed in April. Beloved campus pets Nugget and Peaches died over the summer. As the Bryan Center prepares for renovations that could cost up to $110 million, cultural and identity groups are asking for more equitable space allocations for their organizations. As student groups shared their grievances and sought solutions, the Center for Multicultural Affairs celebrated its 50th anniversary and held its first multicultural graduation. Campus labor unions saw major developments this year. In August, the Duke University Press Workers Union officially won its union. The Duke Graduate Students Union filed for an election with the National Labor Relations Board in March after launching a campaign for formal recognition in September. In response, the University is challenging a 2016 NLRB decision affirming the legal right for graduate students to unionize. This was a big year for climate activism on campus. Duke announced its Climate Commitment, which aims to increase the role of the University in tackling the climate crisis. The Undergraduate Environmental Union — a subcommittee within Duke Student Government — voiced that they believed the Climate Commitment does not go far enough and called for more student input. At the same time, several Duke centers and affiliates have struggled. Duke Student Affairs saw a series of staffing shortages, hampering student group funding and planning for cultural events. The Center for Documentary Studies, a nonprofit affiliate of Duke, has undergone a programmatic overhaul over the past year. A year after the establishment of the Center for Gender Violence Prevention and Intervention, the Center still lacks a permanent director. On the academic side, Duke’s Asian American Diaspora Studies minor saw its first year, while the School of Medicine and the School of Law announced that they would withdraw from US News and World Report’s rankings in January. The Curriculum Development Committee also began thinking about what Trinity College’s new curriculum will look like. Undergraduates chose junior Isaiah Hamilton to be the next DSG president, while senior Sydney Hunt was chosen as the next Undergraduate Young Trustee nominee. Seniors Shreyas Hallur and Qi Xuan Khoo were named Rhodes Scholars.

title: Sophomore Year: 2023-2024
subtitle: Sophomore year saw Duke celebrating its 100th year, demonstrations over the Israel-Hamas war and the effects of the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down affirmative action in higher-education.
author: Staff Reports
text: Sophomore year saw Duke celebrating its 100th year, demonstrations over the Israel-Hamas war and the effects of the Supreme Court’s decision to strike down affirmative action in higher education. Duke rose to No. 7 in the U.S. News and World Report’s Best National Colleges Ranking, the highest in the University’s history. During the admissions cycle, Duke saw a 28% increase in Early Decision applicants and accepted a record low 12.9%. In the Regular Decision round, Duke admitted another record low 4.1% of applicants, resulting in an overall acceptance rate of 5.1% for the Class of 2028. In January, Duke kicked off its centennial celebrations in Cameron Indoor Stadium, hosting student dance groups, former men’s basketball coach Mike Krzyzewski and actor Ken Jeong, Trinity ‘90. The University also received a $100 million award from the Duke Endowment, which will be used to provide financial aid for students from the Carolinas and underrepresented backgrounds; to increase student support, community engagement and experiential learning initiatives; and to remodel the Reuben-Cooke building. The award is the single largest in Duke’s history. Following the Oct. 7 attacks in southern Israel, protests erupted on college campuses across the country. At Duke, pro-Israel vigils honored the victims of the attacks and demanded a return of the hostages. Meanwhile, pro-Palestinian protesters also demanded that Duke call for a ceasefire and that it divest from its holdings in Israel. In an effort to encourage civil discourse, Provost Alec Gallimore launched the Provost’s Initiative on the Middle East, a year-long initiative hosting an array of experts to discuss the conflict in the region. In November, Durham County hosted its municipal general election, which decided Durham’s mayor, Leonardo Williams, and three new City Council members: Javiera Caballero, Nate Baker and Carl Rist. Williams gave his inaugural State of City address in April, emphasizing the importance of investing in education in Durham. In the March 5 primary election, Durham voters set up a gubernatorial race between Democrat Josh Stein and Republican Mark Robinson. Former president Donald Trump came out victorious over Nikki Haley after both candidates campaigned in North Carolina ahead of Super Tuesday. Duke made national headlines in September after a New York Times Magazine article named Duke among the "least economically diverse" of the elite colleges. In response to the article, President Vincent Price characterized it as portraying the University “in a rather harsh light.” However, students resoundingly agreed with the article’s findings. The University received national backlash again after it announced plans to close its herbarium after over 100 years of operations. According to Duke biology professors, the decision holds serious implications for research in climate change and biodiversity. Following the Supreme Court’s June 2023 ruling ending race-based affirmative action, the University announced the end of the Reginaldo Howard Memorial Scholarship Program, a full-ride scholarship for select Black students. The scholarship will be replaced with the Reginaldo Howard Leadership Program and will be open to all undergraduate students, regardless of race. The University also faced a number of lawsuits, ranging from allegations that the Alice M. Baldwin Scholars program engaged in “reverse discrimination” to a class-action lawsuit charging the University with practicing need-aware admissions, which it settled by paying $24 million. Duke football had a whirlwind of a season this year. Students rushed the field in excitement after the team upset No. 9 Clemson during its season opener at Wallace Wade Stadium, the squad rose to an all-time high No. 16 in the AP Poll and College Gameday came to Duke’s matchup against Notre Dame where students watched the beginning of the end of the Elko Era. After an end-of-game injury for junior quarterback Riley Leonard, the season hit a downward spiral, as a number of players entered the transfer portal following coach Mike Elko’s surprise departure to Texas A&M. Incoming coach Manny Diaz would mark a new era of Duke football. Duke men’s basketball had a season of ups and downs, dropping from its No. 2 preseason ranking after an early loss to the No. 12 Arizona Wildcats. The team eventually found its groove, yet still fell to North Carolina during both match-ups at the Dean E. Smith Center and Cameron Indoor Stadium. Throughout the season, freshman guard Jared McCain dominated both on the court with his stellar 3-point shooting performance and off the court with his TikTok dances — most notably to “2 Days Into College” by Aimee Carty. With the top-ranked recruiting class, the team also experienced a transfer craze, with only two starters remaining for the next year’s roster. The Cameron Crazies experienced changes to tenting in Krzyzewskiville this year with the implementation of a Wellness Week and a decision to raise the “grace” temperature from 25 degrees Fahrenheit to 32 degrees Fahrenheit. The policy changes were made in response to complaints from last year’s tenters that tenting was “too mentally and physically overwhelming.” Duke women’s basketball also experienced an electric season, dancing its way past No. 7 Ohio State in the Elite Eight to earn a spot in the Sweet 16 for the first time since 2018. Duke softball also had itself a season, climbing to No. 1 for the first time in program history in April. Duke field hockey found its way to the Final Four in its Cinderella season, falling in a heartbreaker to Northwestern. Divestment was a hot topic on campus this year. Climate-conscious students built on a decade-long campaign to get the University to divest from fossil fuels, holding multiple demonstrations and publishing a report about fossil fuel-funded research at Duke. The Advisory Committee on Investment Responsibility published a report in February recommending Price and the Board of Trustees against divestment. The Duke community also mourned the loss of some of its members this year. Angela Risi, a second-year master of fine arts student, died in October. Hans Joris Van Miegroet, professor of art and art history, died in February. Joseph Izatt, chair of the department of biomedical engineering and Michael J. Fitzpatrick professor of engineering, died in April. After plans to revise the Trinity curriculum beginning in February 2022, the Arts & Sciences Council approved a new curriculum in April to take effect in fall 2025. The new curriculum places a greater emphasis on the humanities amidst declines in humanities enrollment nationwide. The Mary Lou Williams Center for Black Culture reopened in March following a nearly 18-month closure after a burst chilled pipe in the Flowers Building forced the center to relocate to the Bryan Center. The reopening was originally slated to occur in fall 2023 yet was postponed to spring 2024 due to delays in constructions. Duke continued its construction efforts, announcing plans to begin a two-year renovation of Lilly Library. Renovations were initially slated to start in 2020 but were delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Another one of Duke’s East Campus facilities was also shut down indefinitely, this time due to lead dust being detected in various parts of the Brodie Recreation Center. The University notified 2,800 people who had entered the facility when the lead dust was exposed. In this year’s student elections, junior Heather Raslan pulled out victorious in a field of three candidates vying for Duke Student Government president, replacing senior Isaiah Hamilton. Senior Drew Greene was selected as the undergraduate Young Trustee. On the last day of classes, students celebrated their achievements with a live concert headlined by Swae Lee and performances by Weston Estate and Elley Duhé.

title: Junior Year: 2024-2025
subtitle: The Class of 2026’s junior year saw a high-stakes presidential election, a monumental basketball season and the beginning of sweeping changes in higher education.
author: Staff Reports
text: The Class of 2026's junior year saw a high-stakes presidential election, a monumental basketball season and the beginning of sweeping changes in higher education. The academic year began in high spirits. In celebration of Duke’s Centennial, singer-songwriter Ed Sheeran gave a rainy Founder’s Day performance to students and alumni in Wallace Wade stadium. Sheeran’s “Castle on a Hill" might have been an omen of the good to come, as the next day, Duke football rallied to a nail-biting last-minute win over archrival North Carolina. As the election season intensified throughout October, North Carolina emerged as one of the most contested battleground states . Duke saw prominent political figures on campus as eyes turned to the college-aged voter bloc, with then-Democratic vice presidential nominee Tim Walz rallying students in the Bryan Center and former Republican Congresswoman Liz Cheney announcing in Page Auditorium that she would vote for Democratic presidential nominee Kamala Harris. The election was ultimately called in favor of President Donald Trump, sparking mixed reactions across campus. Just days later, Durhamites who voted third party took to the streets to speak against the two-party system, with many pointing to the ongoing Israel-Hamas war as a key factor in their voting decision. The Israel-Hamas war remained a subject of activism on campus. Exactly one year after the Oct. 7, 2023, attack on southern Israel by Hamas militants, pro-Palestinian and pro-Israel student groups organized vigils and demonstrations calling for the end of the “genocide” and the release of hostages, respectively. Following the uptick in student activism and rising political polarization on campus, Duke faculty organized to petition the University to adopt a stance of institutional neutrality and safeguard freedom of speech. As of April 18, 140 faculty members have signed the petition, although Duke has not yet publicly acknowledged it. The Chronicle’s April 2024 inaugural faculty survey found that over 60% of faculty respondents expressed support for the University to officially commit itself to institutional neutrality. Despite the football season coming to a close and the basketball season just beginning, Duke still managed to make headlines in the sports world. At the end of 2024, Crystal Mangum admitted that she fabricated the infamous 2006 Duke lacrosse scandal in which she falsely accused students David Evans, Collin Finnerty and Reade Seligmann of rape. The start of the spring semester came with something Durham had not experienced in more than 1,000 days — snow! Duke campus was coated in white, turning Duke Gardens and Abele Quad into playgrounds for sledding and snowman-building. That was not the only snow that Dukies saw. Another major snowstorm hit just as the Cameron Crazies were set to participate in the Race to the Secret Spots in hopes of securing a white or flex tent in Krzyzewskiville. The race proceeded despite the conditions, although Duke enacted its severe weather and emergency conditions policy shortly after the race’s end. Tenting interest was at an all-time high , with record numbers of students packing Cameron Indoor Stadium to take the tenting entry exam . For those who earned a spot, their “locking-in” paid off, as the Blue Devils thrashed the Tar Heels 87-70. Both men’s and women’s basketball went on to become ACC champions. Duke men’s basketball went deep into the NCAA Tournament, advancing to the Final Four after winning the East Region. However, the team was defeated in a heartbreaking last-minute comeback from Houston. Women’s basketball fell in the Elite Eight . Trump took office in January, and subsequently initiated attacks on higher education institutions. The administration announced a slew of federal funding cuts to universities in February, including slashed funding from the National Institutes of Health, National Science Foundation and the elimination of the United States Agency for International Development. Duke received around $194 million in funding from the NIH alone, and professors who received such funding said that the policies will have a “ chilling effect ” on academia for years to come. Campus was fraught with uncertainty regarding the impact of Trump’s executive orders, which also included attacks on diversity, equity and inclusion and academic freedom . That uncertainty began to materialize in March. Duke announced its first round of cost-cutting measures and a sweeping review of administrative efficiency March 13, which included a staff hiring freeze and the suspension of capital spending. A few weeks later, the Duke Center for International Development, which relied extensively on USAID funding, shuttered its doors, accelerating the University’s cost-reduction efforts. Administrators told The Chronicle that Duke is planning for a future “with a very different funding model [than] in the past.” International students across the United States also felt the impact of the federal government’s crackdown. The Trump administration revoked visas for students involved in pro-Palestinian activism and activities that “ run counter ” to U.S. interests. The administration also threatened to implement a travel ban, prompting concerns from Duke’s international community. The visas of three Duke community members — two Duke graduate students and one alumnus on Optional Practical Training — were terminated in April. They were, however, returned to active status nearly a month later, after the Trump administration reversed its decision to terminate legal status for many international students. Duke has issued travel guidance to its international students, which remains unchanged even after the visa reactivation. The guidance included avoiding non-essential travel outside the country and a warning that students should “be mindful of their digital footprint and recognize its potential impact on their immigration status.” On April 30, Duke drastically escalated its cost-cutting measures, announcing that it would offer eligible employees three-year buyouts, the first among its peers to do so. University administrators said the move was done in an effort to cut 10% of Duke’s total expense base. The rollout of the offers is set to begin May 7. Over the last academic year, the Duke community mourned the loss of some of its members. The community lost graduate student Jiani Yu in December and undergraduate student Ian Hyun Kim in May. Joel Fleishman, founder of the Sanford School of Public Policy and namesake of Duke’s Fleishman House, died in September. Fleishman, who also led Duke’s first capital campaign, is remembered as “ a model for how to live life .” John Feinstein, sports journalism star and former sports editor of The Chronicle, died in March. Professor of Romance Studies Fredric Jameson died in September; Professor of Physics Tom Mehen died in December and Sayan Mukherjee , research professor of statistical science, died in March. The University lost several professors emeriti including Professor Emeritus of Sociology Edward Tiryakian ; Orrin Pilkey , James B. Duke professor emeritus of geology; Michèle Longino , professor emerita of romance studies; Richard Hays , George Washington Ivey distinguished professor emeritus of New Testament; Gerald Wilson , senior associate dean emeritus of the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences; Valentin-Yves Mudimbe , Newman Ivey White distinguished professor emeritus of literature; and Jesse Cavenar , professor emeritus of psychiatry and behavioral sciences. As the year drew to a close, students leaders prepared to shape the University's future. In the Duke Student Government presidential elections, junior Tara Singh was chosen to succeed senior Heather Raslan. Senior Jax Nalley was selected as the Undergraduate Young Trustee and Austin Beason was selected to be the Graduate/Professional Young Trustee. The year ended with a last day of classes concert headlined by “Unwritten” singer Natasha Bedingfield and supported by artists Penelope Road and Rich The Kid.

title: ‘Give yourself the grace to adjust’: Class of 2026 passes advice to new Blue Devils
subtitle: This year's seniors have witnessed fundamental changes to the University, from a year of cost-cutting to the implementation of Quadex. The Chronicle asked four seniors to share advice with incoming first-years.
author: Luca Gragnoli
text: The Class of 2026 has experienced Duke through a period of monumental change. From the full integration of QuadEx into campus life and brand new coaches in top sports to a year of cost-cutting and federal pressure on higher education, graduating seniors have seen it all. The Chronicle asked four members of the Class of 2026 to share advice and lessons they have learned with incoming first-years. Here is what they said: What would you say to first-years at orientation week who are just getting their bearings in this new place? Lizzy Glazer : It's normal to feel intimidated and stressed at first, but if you follow your passions and make decisions based on your values, you'll end up finding people who are willing to support you and become your cheerleaders. Abigail Bergan : Give yourself the grace to adjust. It's a big transition from high school to college, especially if you're coming from far away. It can take a little while for you to get your feet under you — that's super normal ... You are not the only one feeling overwhelmed by this transition. Aiden Lo: Duke has done a great job with orientation and honestly the people that you meet at orientation will become your first friend group ... But, don't stress if you can't find your people in orientation first, because there's going to be so many chances for you to meet new people through Marketplace or your Constellation classes or otherwise. Kaitlyn Williams : Embrace the uncertainty ... No one expects you to know exactly what your 10-year plan looks like post-Duke, and it's going to change. That is completely ok. How would you recommend spending time outside of class? Glazer: Duke has so much to offer. It can often feel overwhelming, since you could live so many lifetimes and never really be able to do everything that Duke has ... I would encourage [first-years] to start out by recognizing what makes them happy and by dedicating time to things that bring them joy and are aligned with their interests. Bergan : Some of the best things that I've done outside of the classroom at Duke have happened spontaneously. Give yourself some free time to relax, to take care of yourself. Self care is really important, so definitely have fun and also enjoy your precious free time. Lo : Definitely attending as many events as possible ... One thing that I regretted in my freshman year was not going to enough clubs and events because I was scared or I didn't have anyone to go with ... If you go to enough events, you start meeting the same people and then that's how you start to make friends ... Just go do it because you never know what you may find. Williams: Take advantage of all the free opportunities that Duke has ... There are so many fun things that go on every day that students don't even know about ... I've met some of my best friends by magically appearing at events or wandering through dorms, and I simply cannot encourage enough to just say ‘yes.’ You don't need to spend all of your hours in Perkins [Library]. That does not make a successful Duke student. Any advice on balancing academics with extracurriculars, social life, hobbies and everything else? Glazer: You don't get a grade on life in the end, so it’s really important to learn how to balance your education with the Duke experience. The best part of Duke is truly the people, and so your Duke experience really would not be fully appreciated if you aren't spending time getting to know humans and forming connections. Bergan : The people are the best part of Duke ... You're going to be juggling a lot of balls in college and in life in general; inevitably, some of them are going to fall. It's just up to you to figure out which ones are rubber and which ones are glass. Lo : Duke really is one of the most flexible schools when it comes to academics. In terms of the credits that you have to accumulate, it's not as limiting as other schools, and you can choose your major so late. So, that really means that you should ... pick classes that you find interesting first, then really delve into the social aspect, making sure you have a group of friends that you're comfortable with and that you could do things with. Williams : Be realistic about budgeting your time. You are going to want to do 500 trillion things, and that's so normal, but you have to set up hours to be productive ... It was always really important to me to set out a good chunk of the day ... where I'm going to do work and then the rest of the day is allowed to do whatever I would like. What do you wish you could have done or known more about before graduating? Glazer: Everything ends up working out. Everyone is working hard and doing their best and there's no point in comparing yourself to anyone else ... As long as you're making decisions that align with your values and the things that are important to you, you're doing everything right. Bergan: I wish I could have explored more of Durham. It's pretty hard to get too far into Durham without a car, but it's a great city ... I also wish I'd known how fast the time was going to fly. People say that the time goes by really fast, but I don't think that hits you until you're a month out from graduation. It feels like East Campus move-in was yesterday. Lo: One of the things that I missed was professor relationships. Duke has a really good professor-to-student ratio and it would be a shame to not utilize it. FLUNCH is a really nice system and a lot of people don't utilize it often. Freshman year you get introduced to that system, so you use it a lot, but I slowly started FLUNCHing less. That's something that I regretted not doing as much, because the professors here are very willing to help you. Williams: I wish I had known more about all of the free travel opportunities that you can suck out of Duke ... There are so many opportunities like that all over campus that you just have to be willing to spend a little bit of time looking [to find]. It is such a privilege to go to a place like this, where there are so many pockets and holes to find money and find opportunities and make really cool memories. What are the most important lessons you learned at Duke, or any words of wisdom you've carried with you throughout these past four years? Glazer: You're enough just the way you are — there's something special about each individual here at Duke. Everyone brings something unique, whether it's their previous life experiences or their opinions ... Also, there's so much learning that occurs outside of the classroom ... Particularly at Duke, getting to have those unique experiences whether it be travel or getting to know somebody who's lived different lives than you have is where people end up learning the most. Bergan : Long-term planning is overrated ... Life's a lot more fun when you don't have it all planned out by the time you're 18. Most of the value and most of the lessons that I've learned from Duke have come from unexpected places or unexpected people: things I did, classes I took or experiences I had that would never have been a part of my plan at 18 ... At the end of the day, college is a place where we're taught how to be grown ups, but there's still a lot of mechanisms for us to be able to make mistakes and be totally ok with that. Lo: Duke is a unique college for three factors. Only Duke does all three really well. It's secluded: You get to see familiar faces all the time. The school spirit is absolutely insane: Even people who don't like sports get dragged into sports. I never watched a basketball game in my life before coming here, and I became a line monitor ... Then the third one is the selectivity of everything. So there's only one club per industry. I find that to be really cool, because if you want to find your group of people, you know exactly where to look for them. Williams: Something that I was really hit with recently is that Duke is the last time in your life where people are really paid to support you and to get you where you need to be. Once you enter the workforce, that dynamic looks very different. There is no one to push you in that same manner, so take advantage of all those resources. The most valuable thing that I'm gaining with my degree is the network of mentors and friends that I made at this University, and individuals who, even if we don't talk until our five-year reunion, I know that I will be so excited to see and catch up with and just be so proud of for everything that they have accomplished.

title: Meet 2 of Duke's other Class of 2026 commencement speakers
subtitle: Duke Law will host Maurice “Mo” Green, North Carolina Superintendent of Public Instruction, and the economics department will hear from Lorie Logan, the president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.
author: Jackson Kitts
text: Microsoft Chief Financial Officer Amy Hood, Trinity ‘94, will headline Duke’s main commencement ceremony on May 10, but she will not be the only notable name at a podium this spring. Across campus, individual schools and departments are hosting their own graduation ceremonies, with some also inviting a speaker of their own. Two of those are worth noting: Maurice “Mo” Green, who will address Duke Law School graduates, and Lorie Logan, who will address Duke economics graduates. The North Carolina Superintendent of Public Instruction is coming home. A double Duke graduate, Trinity ‘88 and Law School ‘91, Green received his bachelor's degree in political science and economics as well as his law degree from the University. Green, a Democrat, took office in January 2025 after narrowly defeating Republican Michele Morrow in the 2024 election. Since taking office, Green has embarked on a statewide tour to push his plan to make N.C. public schools the “best in the nation.” Before assuming his current role, Green served as superintendent for Guilford County Schools, the third-largest district in North Carolina, for more than seven years, during which the district saw improved academic performance, increased graduation rates and a heightened emphasis on character development. He then led the Z. Smith Reynolds Foundation as executive director from 2016-23, where he helped develop a grantmaking and learning framework aimed at strengthening democracy, advancing public education and promoting social and economic justice across North Carolina. Green has been recognized for educational leadership with awards such as the Bob Grossman Leadership in School Communications Award in 2011 and the Distinguished Educator of the Year by the N.C. Association of Supervision and Curriculum Development in 2014. The Law School’s convocation will take place from 5:30 to 7:30 p.m. on May 9 in Cameron Indoor Stadium. It is open to all graduating law students and their guests. Taking the stage for the economics department, Logan is the president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas. As the Bank’s 14th president, Logan is the first woman to permanently hold the role. Since taking office in August 2022, Logan has led nearly 1,400 people in the Eleventh Federal Reserve District, which covers Texas, northern Louisiana and southern New Mexico. Logan represents the district on the Federal Open Market Committee, the body that sets national monetary policy. This year, she holds a voting seat. In that role, Logan has been cautious about cutting interest rates due to concerns of persistent inflation. Separate from voting on the FOMC, Logan oversees all Bank functions: economic research, banking supervision, financial stability, community engagement and economic development. Under Logan’s leadership, the Bank launched its Global Institute, which conducts policy-driven economic research with a focus on the relationship between the U.S. and Mexico. Before assuming her current position, Logan spent more than two decades at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, rising to the role of executive vice president. She managed the Federal Reserve’s securities portfolio and led the implementation of monetary policy for the FOMC. She also played a crucial role in the Fed’s response to both the Great Recession and the COVID-19 pandemic. She holds a bachelor’s degree in political science from Davidson College and a master’s in public administration from Columbia University. The economics department will hold its graduation ceremony from 12 to 1:30 p.m. on May 10 in Cameron Indoor Stadium. The event is open to all graduating economics majors, master's students, doctoral students and their guests.

title: Guide to 2026 commencement events
subtitle: AsDuke celebrates its graduates, The Chronicle compiled a guide to the weekend’s activities.
author: Seyun Park
text: Graduates of Duke’s 10 schools will be awarded degrees Sunday, May 10, in the University’s commencement ceremony. Commencement will take place in Wallace Wade Stadium, starting at 9 a.m. with the procession of candidates. President Vincent Price will preside over the ceremony, and Microsoft Chief Financial Officer Amy Hood, Trinity ‘94, will deliver the commencement address. The ceremony, which is scheduled to conclude by 11 a.m., will be livestreamed on the commencement website and Duke’s Youtube channel . Gates to Wallace Wade will open at 7:30 a.m., and the University encourages guests to be in their seats by 8:30 a.m. All guest seating is unassigned. Graduates must report to Pascal Field House via the Blue Zone 7 or Grounds parking lots no later than 8:15 a.m. Parking is free on campus all weekend and available on a first-come, first-served basis. On Sunday, shuttles will run to the Bryan Center and Science Drive garages from 7:30 a.m. to 1 p.m. Graduates can pick up their commencement gift during the Forever Duke kickoff on Thursday at the Karsh Alumni and Visitors Center. There, they will toast to graduate and professional students at 5 p.m. and undergraduates at 6 p.m. Students are encouraged to arrive 45 minutes before their toast. The University will hold two baccalaureate services at 1 p.m. and 4 p.m. on Friday in the Duke Chapel. The multi-faith services will include prayer, music, readings from various religious traditions as well as remarks from Price and Dean of the Chapel Luke Powery. Overflow seating will be available in Page Auditorium and the services will be livestreamed from the Duke Chapel YouTube channel . Many schools and departments will hold smaller ceremonies and receptions throughout the weekend. The Divinity School will hold its baccalaureate service and hooding ceremony at 6:30 p.m. on Saturday in the Duke Chapel, with overflow seating in the 00 level of the Westbrook Divinity Building. The service will include a Divinity School faculty member’s sermon and graduates receiving their academic hoods. The Fuqua School of Business will hold a ceremony for recipients of its Health Sector Management Certificate at 1 p.m. on Friday in the Fuqua building. At 5 p.m., the School will honor graduates of its Master of Management Studies and Master of Quantitative Management programs in Cameron Indoor Stadium. Additionally, at 9 a.m. on Saturday, graduates of the Master of Science in Quantitative Management, Global Executive Master of Business Administration and Weekend Executive MBA programs will have a commencement ceremony in Cameron Indoor Stadium, followed by a ceremony for Daytime MBA graduates at 1 p.m. in the same location. The Nicholas School of the Environment will host an undergraduate recognition ceremony at 9 a.m. on Saturday at the Wilson Recreation Center. The school will also recognize graduates of the International Master of Environmental Policy at 4:30 p.m. on Thursday in Grainger Hall. Doctoral students will be honored at 10 a.m. on Friday in the Levine Science Research Center’s Love Auditorium and Hall of Science, followed by a recognition ceremony for professional program graduates at 4 p.m. at the Wilson Recreation Center. The Pratt School of Engineering will hold a diploma ceremony for undergraduates at 2:45 p.m. on Sunday in Cameron Indoor Stadium. Doctoral students will be recognized at a ceremony at 11 a.m. on Friday in Schiciano Auditorium and on the ground floor atrium of the Fitzpatrick Center. The ceremony for graduates of the Institute for Enterprise Engineering will be at 10 a.m. on Saturday in Page Auditorium, followed by a graduation ceremony for Master of Engineering and Master of Science graduates at 2 p.m. and Master of Engineering Management graduates at 5 p.m., both also in Page Auditorium. The Sanford School of Public Policy will honor all graduates, including undergraduate, master's, and doctoral students, in one ceremony at 9 a.m. on Friday in Cameron Indoor Stadium. The Law School will host its convocation ceremony at 5:30 p.m. on Saturday in Cameron Indoor Stadium. The School of Medicine will host a Hippocratic Oath ceremony for Doctor of Medicine graduates at 7 p.m. on Friday in the Duke Chapel. The school will recognize graduates of its Master of Biomedical Sciences program at 10 a.m. on Saturday in the Duke Reynolds Industries Theater. On Sunday, Master of Management in Clinical Informatics graduates will have a ceremony at 11 a.m. on the 10th floor of Hock Plaza. Doctor of Physical Therapy graduates will be recognized at 9:30 a.m. on Monday in Cameron Indoor Stadium. The School of Nursing’s hooding and reception ceremony will take place at 6 p.m. on Sunday in Cameron Indoor Stadium. The Graduate School and Trinity College of Arts & Sciences will host ceremonies by department and other organizations, including religious and affinity groups and scholars programs, will host ceremonies and receptions throughout the weekend. A full list of ceremonies and receptions can be viewed on the Duke commencement website .

title: Senior year: 2025-26
subtitle: Senior year saw Duke engage in a year of cost-cutting in response to federal policy changes, historic wins for the University’s sports teams and the rise of artificial intelligence.
author: Staff Reports
text: During its senior year, the Class of 2026 witnessed a year of cost-cutting in response to federal policy changes, historic wins for the University’s sports teams and the rise of artificial intelligence. Before students returned to campus, the Trump administration began to target Duke. In May, the federal government terminated $129 million in funding dedicated to HIV vaccine development led by the University. Then, in July, the Trump administration froze $108 million in National Institutes of Health funding to Duke after two federal agencies launched investigations into the University for alleged racial discrimination and violations of the Civil Rights Act. Alongside pressure from the federal government, Duke spent the summer initiating an array of cost-cutting measures that had been announced in March of the Class of 2026’s junior year. Nearly 600 staff members departed the University through a voluntary separation incentive program, with involuntary layoffs soon following. Campus started to feel different in the fall as the full effects of Duke’s cost-cutting program — which produced $229 million in savings for the fiscal year 2026 budget — started to take root. Subject librarians were quietly laid off and graduate student programs were shuttered . The academic year also began with a new curriculum for the Class of 2029 in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences, which drew mixed reactions from first-years. The new class had to participate in either the FOCUS program or join a Constellation, a set of three courses exploring an interdisciplinary topic over the course of the academic year. In November, Durham held its municipal elections , in which Mayor Leonardo Williams and Councilmember Chelsea Cook were reelected while newcomers Matt Kopac and Shanetta Burris defeated incumbents DeDreanna Freeman and Mark-Anthony Middleton, respectively, for spots on the council. The impact of the federal government’s policy changes went far beyond higher education, reaching Duke’s international and immigrant community members. As international students and faculty faced visa delays , beloved C1 driver Luis Juaréz lost his immigration status, had it restored and then lost it again, prohibiting him from driving his “party bus” across campus. Margoth Erazo, a former University housekeeper, also lost her immigration status and was forced to stop working. Duke Kunshan University, the University’s flagship campus in China, also came under scrutiny, with its future unclear . Right before Thanksgiving, Border Patrol agents entered Durham, sparking fear among community members and encouraging some students to protest monthly to call on Duke to become a “Fourth Amendment Campus.” In a spring referendum, over 90% of students endorsed the demand. The new semester kicked off with a series of snow days after winter weather swept through North Carolina. Students fanned out across campus to go sledding in the Duke Gardens and partake in frosty fun. As the campus warmed up, community members returned to the Gardens to see the iconic cherry blossoms, breaking visitation records. In the athletics world, Duke saw many successes during the 2025-26 season. The academic year marked the first in which an ACC school won the men’s basketball , women’s basketball and football conference championships in a single year. Men’s fencing also won the ACC Championship, its third of all time. Hundreds of students camped out for five weeks in Kryzyzewskiville to see men’s basketball take on North Carolina in Cameron Indoor Stadium. Cameron Crazies broke the Guinness World Record for the largest knockout game, with 711 participants . The long wait in tents was worth it as the Blue Devils got revenge against their rivals after February’s loss in a buzzer-beater upset to the Tarheels at the Dean E. Smith Center. Both men’s and women’s basketball went to the Elite Eight in their respective NCAA tournaments this year, with each losing heartbreakers to UConn and UCLA , respectively. Football took home the Tony the Tiger Sun Bowl trophy after defeating Arizona State, and head coach Manny Diaz earned a contract extension through 2031. Two years after the Oct. 7, 2023, Hamas attack on southern Israel, activism around the war quieted down. However, Duke made national headlines for two controversies in this area. In September, comedian Jerry Seinfeld compared the “Free Palestine” movement to the Ku Klux Klan in a surprise appearance on campus. In the spring, Student Affairs initially froze the status of Duke’s Students for Justice in Palestine chapter, then restored it following an investigation. The University tried to capitalize on the expansion of artificial intelligence this year, launching a Provost’s Initiative on AI over the summer that led to a March report discussing how the University can lead on AI. Duke granted all undergraduates unlimited free access to ChatGPT-4o and even developed its own AI model, DukeGPT. Professors found increasingly innovative ways to use AI in the classroom, while the University’s libraries looked to protect their collections against developers. As the federal government cracked down on diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives across America, particularly in higher education, Duke took notice . It announced a new approach, known as “inclusive excellence.” As part of this transition, the University made changes to key programs, including opening Baldwin Scholars to male applicants and halting the Black Student Alliance Invitational and Latino Student Recruitment Week for admitted students. Duke slipped to No. 7 in the U.S. News and World Report’s Best National Colleges Ranking. During the admissions cycle, the University saw a 7% decrease in Early Decision applicants and accepted 13.1%. In the Regular Decision round, Duke received a record high 55,776 applications, admitting 3.7%, resulting in an overall acceptance rate of 4.7% for the Class of 2030. During the March primary elections , North Carolina voters set up a U.S. Senate race to replace retiring Republican Sen. Thom Tillis, choosing former Democratic Governor Roy Cooper and Republican Michael Whatley as the nominees. Closer to home, Durham Democratic primary voters narrowly renominated incumbent Rep. Valerie Foushee after a heated challenge from Durham County Commissioner Nida Allam. Over the course of the year, several new administrators took the helm, including Deborah Rutter , vice provost for the arts; Mary Frances Luce , dean of the Fuqua School of Business; David Zaas , CEO of the Duke University Health System; and JR DeShazo , incoming Joel L. Fleishman dean of the Sanford School of Public Policy. Departing Duke were Dean of Undergraduate Admissions Christoph Guttentag ; Colin Duckett , executive vice dean for basic and preclinical science; Ed Balleisen , senior vice provost for interdisciplinary programs and initiatives; and Greg Victory , assistant vice president of student affairs and Fannie Mitchell executive director of the Career Center. The Duke community also lost valued members, including junior Olivia Ayiku , senior Abigail Yohannes and Bradley Simmons , instructor of music and director of the Duke Djembe and Afro-Cuban Ensembles. The University mourned a number of emerita community members this year, including two deans emerita of the School of Nursing, Marion Broome and Mary Champagne . Others who died this year include Sheridan Johns , professor emeritus of political science; George Christie , James B. Duke professor emeritus of law; Daniel Kaplan , former assistant professor of medicine; Robert Sawyer , founding executive director of the Duke Talent Identification Program; and former Professor of Medicine Robert “Jess” Peter . In this year’s student elections, junior Angela Chen was chosen to succeed senior Tara Singh as Duke Student Government president. Senior Kaitlyn Williams was selected as the undergraduate Young Trustee. On the last day of classes, students celebrated with a live concert headlined by Quavo and featuring performances by Slayyyter and WHATMORE.
