title: From declining trust to Epstein-related retirements: 4 key higher ed updates from April
subtitle: The Chronicle recapped four major updates in the world of higher education from the month of April.
author: Ella Moore
text: Universities across the country continue to face scrutiny on their DEI practices and graduate students face a new financial landscape as the Trump administration makes changes to student loans. From investigations into the declining trust in higher education, to an administrator’s departure after discoveries about his ties to Jeffrey Epstein, here’s the biggest news in higher education from last month. “Trust is earned by doing what you say you’re going to do — and, ideally, doing it well,” a committee made up of 10 Yale University professors wrote in an April 10 report, describing “widespread uncertainty about the fundamental purpose and mission of higher education.” The report, which was tasked in April 2025 with examining “the problem of declining trust in higher education,” cited rising tuition costs, a lack of transparency in undergraduate admissions and self-censorship on campus as reasons for a lack of trust. In the report, authors also emphasized that a “constellation of pressures” has led to the deprioritization of academics on university campuses, and described “the devaluing of teaching and learning” as the greatest threat to higher education. They noted that often, tenured faculty are encouraged to prioritize research over teaching, and that grade inflation has made GPA a less trustworthy measure of academic success. Although Yale President Maurie McInnis said she accepted the judgment of the report “fully,” administrators elsewhere have split on its conclusions. Michael Gordin, dean of the college at Princeton University and the senior administrator responsible for the University’s academic program, questioned whether declining public trust in universities lies entirely on the institutions themselves. “Given that there’s this large-scale decline of trust, it seems like something broader might be going on that cannot entirely be laid at the feet of the institutions,” Gordin told the Daily Princetonian, the University’s student publication. “Even if it were the case that the things they identified are the reasons why trust went down in higher education, it’s not obvious that undoing them would restore the trust.” Duke has faced criticism for several of the issues listed in the report, including grade inflation and its policy on protests, which has incited free speech concerns. A Duke faculty committee recently reviewed its Protests, Pickets and Demonstrations policy, calling for its complete overhaul. Although institutions such as Harvard University have been looking to crack down on grade inflation, Duke has not publicly announced any measures seeking to substantially change its grading systems. On April 6, the Department of Education published a press release praising the Trump administration’s work to eliminate diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives in higher education. The release said that over 300 colleges and universities have discontinued DEI requirements, shuttered DEI offices, removed diversity statements from hiring practices and altered or removed DEI policies. The University of North Carolina System was one of the “prominent examples,” the Department cited. It took swift action after the Trump administration took office to eliminate general education requirements related to DEI. Despite their claimed success, the Trump administration recently opened a new investigation into Stanford University for alleged racial discrimination. In an April 29 press release , the Department claimed that Stanford discriminated based on race by basing access to their National Board Resource Center, which helps aspiring educators receive certification, on race rather than merit. “Instead of helping students achieve their goals through merit, Stanford appears to be conditioning access to National Board Certification programs based on skin color,” said Kimberly Richey, assistant secretary for civil rights in the Department. “... If the allegations are true, Stanford is engaged in discrimination — pure and simple.” Stanford has since discontinued the program. Duke has not publicly faced as much federal scrutiny from the Department of Education since two federal investigations were announced last summer into systemic racial discrimination into the University, the Duke Law Journal and Duke Health. However, the University has continued to pause identity-based programs, including the Black Student Alliance Invitational and Latino Students Recruitment Week, which focus on welcoming admitted students of color. On April 30, the Trump administration announced it would finalize new loan limits for postbaccalaureate degrees. The new rule maintains that only students pursuing education within the Department of Education’s definition of professional degrees will have access to the highest amount of federal loans, or $50,000 per year. The 11 degrees that meet this criteria include pharmacy, dentistry, veterinary medicine, chiropractic, law, medicine, optometry, osteopathic medicine, podiatry, theology and clinical psychology. Other occupations, such as nurse practitioners or physical therapists, will have access to a significantly smaller amount: $20,500 per year. The Trump administration’s designation of certain programs as professional has previously received criticism from a coalition of healthcare organizations, as enrollments for key positions in therapy, nurse practitioner and other programs are expected to decline. Under Secretary of Education Nicholas Kent said the new policies put “students and taxpayers first” and make “higher education more affordable.” The new policy also ends the Grad PLUS program for new borrowers. The end of the program, which reportedly helped over 440,000 graduate and professional students to pay for higher education, leaves many borrowers in search of a new form of financial assistance. The Department of Education said in the same release that ending the program will help curb tuition growth and touted a new “Tiered Standard” plan in addition to “a new income-driven plan” for borrowers. The new changes will take effect July 1. Leon Botstein, who served as president of Bard College for more than 50 years, will retire after inquiries into his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein. Bard had commissioned an independent review of Botstein’s ties to Epstein after the release of thousands of files involving the convicted sex offender. The review concluded that there was no evidence of illegal actions, but raised concerns about the ethics of his leadership. The review found that Botstein remained in contact with Epstein from 2012 to 2019, and had approximately 25 visits to his townhouse in New York. He also reportedly received payments from Epstein, which Botstein said were provided to Bard under his own name. According to the review, Botstein said, “I would take money from Satan if it permitted me to do God’s work.” Other universities have continued to contend with their financial ties to Epstein and have faced pressure to remove the names of individuals found to be in connection with Epstein from campus buildings. At Duke, The Chronicle reported in February that Dan Ariely, professor of business administration in the Fuqua School of Business and Graduate School ‘98, had a longstanding friendship with Epstein.

title: Greg Victory to step down as Career Center executive director, assistant VP of student affairs
subtitle: Victory led the Career Center for six years, promoting the philosophy of “Career Everywhere for Everyone.” He is set to become associate vice president at the University of Miami.
author: Dylan Halper
text: Greg Victory, assistant vice president of student affairs and Fannie Mitchell executive director of the Career Center, will leave Duke at the end of May to become associate vice president at the University of Miami. Since 2020, Victory has led Duke’s Career Center, which helps prepare students for future careers through consultations, training programs and related events. Victory has also served as the faculty-in-residence for Gilbert-Addoms dorm since 2024. His departure was announced in a Thursday email to students from Mary Pat McMahon, vice provost and vice president of student affairs. At the Career Center, Victory championed the philosophy of “Career Everywhere for Everyone,” in which all Duke community members, not just the Career Center, should play a key role in helping students achieve their career goals. For Victory, this meant equipping faculty, staff and alumni with the tools necessary “to create consistent messaging for all Duke students.” Under Victory’s leadership, the Career Center also started holding virtual advising appointments to increase accessibility to career services. “If you know Greg, you know how deeply he cares about all students fulfilling their potential and developing purpose-driven pathways of impact in the world,” McMahon wrote. “... The Duke community has benefited tremendously from his time here in Durham.” In his new role, Victory will lead the career center at the University of Miami alongside “a set of coordinated success initiatives,” according to McMahon. Before coming to Duke, Victory served as the executive director of Tufts University’s Career Center and held administrative roles at the Rhode Island School of Design where, for some of his tenure, he also led their career center. Rachel Coleman, director of career development and education, will serve in Victory’s role on an interim basis from June 1 to Dec. 31. She has worked at the Career Center for the past eight years and has served in her current role since July 2021. McMahon announced that a national search for Victory’s permanent replacement will be initiated over the summer. She said that elected undergraduate and graduate student leadership will be consulted to “ensure students have meaningful representation” in the search process.

title: Duke Libraries signs on to national statement of shared AI practice
subtitle: The agreement, originally commissioned by the University of Virginia, seeks to protect research library collections from being misused to train AI models.
author: Seyun Park
text: Duke University Libraries has signed on to a national “Statement of Shared Practice” on how to manage collection requests from artificial intelligence developers. Originally commissioned by the University of Virginia Library, the statement principally aims to prevent “archival, manuscript, rare and special collections” from being misused to train AI, by focusing on issues of ownership and provenance, a term for the origins of a document or object. “The question before us is not whether to engage with AI, but on what terms, for what purposes and with what protections for the communities and collections we serve,” the statement says. The agreement, described by the UVA Libraries as a framework “designed to protect the integrity of cultural heritage materials as AI developers increasingly seek access to them,” was announced in early April. Although Duke signed onto the statement, the agreement itself is non-binding, meaning the University still has the autonomy to make AI access deals. “We wanted to be at the table as more people were talking about [AI],” said Joseph Salem, Rita DiGiallonardo Holloway University librarian and vice provost for Duke library affairs. Salem also serves as a co-chair on the AI at Duke Steering Committee which authored the University’s recent AI report . “We're creating a bigger cohort of different institutions who really just want to get a sense of ‘What does the landscape look like?’ and ‘How do we do this ethically?” Salem said. The two-page statement contains six main “shared commitments,” which include to “favor methods where source materials remain under institutional control” and “apply heightened scrutiny and a presumption against approval” of broad commercial training requests. AI has increasingly played a larger role in libraries, ranging from digitization of library collections to training and education in classes. However, generative AI models — once given access to a library’s collection — could lose the connection to the original owner and not correctly cite the original source. The tendency has made it increasingly important for libraries to track their collection ownership and manage AI model usage. “What struck me was that the materials being requested are irreplaceable,” UVA Dean of Libraries Leo Lo said in an April 3 press release explaining the purpose of the statement. “Unpublished letters, photographic archives, oral histories, manuscript drafts ... That felt like something the archival community needed to address together, not one library at a time,” Lo continued. Salem said Duke Libraries has been increasingly receiving requests from AI developers for access to their special collections, and that the statement provided a non-legally binding path to establish a more concrete method of processing those requests. “We wanted to make sure that we had a framework to be able to respond to those requests systematically, thoughtfully and to make sure that it was all vetted the same way,” Salem said. The University has not yet signed any access deals, but Salem suspects that much of DUL’s open-access materials available online have already been scraped by bots for AI training. DUL has since added an anti-bot tool to the back end of its catalog to prevent bot interference. The Statement of Shared Practice calls for a confidential shared ledger among member universities that records requests received by libraries and tracks general terms of agreement and pricing. Although universities are not required to contribute to the ledger, the agreement discourages signatories from signing agreements with AI developers that would prevent them from “category-level information sharing.” The commitments in the statement are scheduled to run through April 2027, but the signatories will reconvene in February 2027 to discuss results and decide whether to extend, revise or conclude the agreement. Ultimately, Salem hopes the statement will encourage library leaders to discuss AI and training requests “in a way that recognizes the moment and contributes to innovation, but without giving away our content irresponsibly.” UVA Libraries said that more than 42 institutions contributed to the development of the Statement of Shared Practice, but the agreement was made official with 11 initial signatories. Besides Duke and UVA, the other signatories include the libraries of Florida State University, Northwestern University, Oklahoma State University, Rice University, Tulane University, the University of Florida, the University of Rochester, the University of Wisconsin-Madison, Washington University in St. Louis and Wayne State University.

title: ‘A guiding presence’: Professor of Medicine Robert Peter remembered for mentorship, influence on cardiology
subtitle: Peter died unexpectedly March 7 at his home in Hillsborough, North Carolina, at the age of 90. His former cardiology fellows remember him for his “larger than life character,” compassionate mentorship and pioneering work in interventional cardiology.
author: Lila Cohen
text: Former Professor of Medicine Robert “Jess” Peter, Trinity ‘57 and School of Medicine ‘61, is remembered for his “larger than life character,” dedication to mentorship and pioneering work in the field of interventional cardiology. Peter, 90, died unexpectedly March 7 at his home in Hillsborough, North Carolina. “He really was a character just full of life and zeal. He loved patients. He loved stories ... He was so curious about what’s the right treatment” and how to deliver it, said William Schuyler Jones, professor of medicine and an interventional cardiologist for Duke Health. Originally hailing from Rockville Centre, New York, Peter first entered Duke as an undergraduate and “stand-out” track athlete, becoming team captain his senior year. After earning his Bachelor of Science, he stayed at Duke, receiving his medical degree from the School of Medicine and then completing part of his residency and a cardiology fellowship at the University. Following a two-year stint in the U.S. Navy as director of the Cardiopulmonary Laboratory at Portsmouth Naval Hospital in Virginia, Peter returned to Duke University Medical Center in 1971 to serve as the associate director of the cardiology laboratory until 1987. He later became a professor of medicine in 1980, and he served in the role for over three decades until his retirement in 2005. Manesh Patel, Richard Sean Stack distinguished professor and chief of the clinical pharmacology and cardiology divisions at the Duke University Health System, first met Peter in 2000 as a first-year cardiology fellow. “For students and fellows, he was just gracious,” Patel said. He described Peter as “constantly curious, always wanting to figure out new ways to improve how we did the procedure of teaching” and working hard to ensure the fellows knew how to do their practice well and take care of their patients. Michael Sketch, professor emeritus of medicine and interventional cardiologist for Duke Health, offered similar praise for Peter’s mentorship, saying he trained over 300 fellows in cardiac catheterization. “He was far more than an outstanding interventional cardiologist,” Sketch said. “He was a guiding presence, a master clinician and a deeply generous teacher,” who could relate to people on all different levels. Sketch shared an anecdote in which he was working on a complex case in the middle of the night and was struggling to keep a coronary artery open. After phoning Peter for assistance at 3 a.m., Peter showed up “in 10 minutes” to help despite not being on call. “‘You never leave your buddy,’” Jones recalled Peter telling him when he was summoned over the loudspeaker to help another doctor while already working on a “very difficult procedure.” According to Patel, Peter was the “go-to guy,” one of the doctors who would constantly deal with challenging and complicated cases, and led the charge when it came to interventional cardiology. “He was such an influential figure in how you think about medicine, how you think about cardiology,” Patel said. Peter was considered a “pioneer” in the field of interventional cardiology, which focuses on using minimally invasive procedures to address cardiac and blood vessel health problems. Patel credited Peter as one of the earliest cardiologists to bring the practice of rapidly opening arteries for patients experiencing heart attacks to the North Carolina medical community. Peter authored or co-authored over 90 articles and book chapters in medical journals during his career. He also helped develop a reporting system for catheterizations that worked to provide follow-up data on all patients treated. Jones attributed Peter’s successes to his lack of fear “to go that extra mile,” and his love for patients. Jones was part of the last group of cardiology fellows to train under Peter, and described him as a “great teacher” and the “most capable faculty member.” He remembers Peter for establishing a culture of collaboration and kindness at the Cardiology Laboratory. “He was so patient-centered before that was even a term,” Jones said. “An example for all of us.” Peter was widely recognized for his abilities both in teaching and cardiology throughout his career. He received the Thomas D. Kinney Excellence in Teaching Award in 1979, the Medal of Merit of the Polish Cardiac Society in 1995 and the Thomas M. Bashore Teaching Award in 2003. He was also a member of the North Carolina Heart Association and the American Heart Association, and a fellow of the American College of Physicians, the American College of Cardiology and the Council on Clinical Cardiology. Patel said he still passes on the lessons he learned training under Peter to his own fellows today. “If you were to pull 20 years of interventional cardiologists, you would probably find that he’s had a lasting impact on every one of them,” Patel said.

title: Student Affairs restores Duke SJP chapter's status, OIE determines Instagram post did not violate policy
subtitle: Duke’s Students for Justice in Palestine chapter had its status restored on Friday after the Office of Institutional Equity found that an Instagram post by the group, which was reported for alleged antisemitism, did not violate University policy.
author: Dylan Halper
text: Duke’s Students for Justice in Palestine chapter was unsuspended from organizational activity on Friday after Student Affairs lifted a previously imposed status restriction and funding freeze. The move came after the Office for Institutional Equity separately determined that an Instagram post by the group — which had been reported for alleged antisemitism — did not warrant an investigation nor violate University policy. “... The post, while offensive, in that it alludes to antisemitic tropes, does not violate the Policy on Prohibited Discrimination, Harassment and Related Misconduct [PPDHRM] because there was insufficient information to support the existence of a hostile educational environment,” Sharon Gooding, associate vice president for institutional equity, equal opportunity and compliance, wrote in an April 23 letter to SJP leadership obtained by The Chronicle. OIE’s assessment began after 10 students filed complaints with the office over a March 13 post on SJP’s Instagram account that promoted a general body meeting about the Iran war. The post featured an illustration by artist Emory Douglas, originally published in the newspaper The Black Panther in 1970, depicting two pigs — one labeled "U.S. Imperialism" holding the torch of liberty and the other labeled "Zionism" holding the Star of David. Gooding told SJP leadership in the April 23 letter that OIE’s initial assessment had centered on whether they should investigate “Hostile Environment Harassment” allegations against the group. Under PPDHRM, “Hostile Environment Harassment” is defined as unwelcome conduct based on religion, race, gender or other “protected characteristics” that is “subjectively and objectively offensive and is so severe or pervasive that it limits or denies a person’s ability to participate in or benefit from Duke’s programs or activities.” The policy adds that isolated incidents typically do not create a hostile environment “unless sufficiently severe.” Gooding’s letter noted that OIE had concluded future investigation was unnecessary. Despite the post not violating University policy, Gooding wrote that “to ensure that further impact to the community is minimized,” the post, which had been voluntarily taken down after a March 24 request by Student Affairs, should remain permanently removed from SJP’s account. Student Affairs froze SJP’s status as OIE was processing its initial assessment in March. In addition to asking SJP to remove the post from their account, Ben Adams, senior associate dean of students for QuadEx, also restricted SJP’s recognized group status, according to emails obtained by The Chronicle. After receiving Gooding’s letter, SJP leadership followed up with Adams and other Student Affairs personnel on the evening of April 23 to ask whether or not the freeze had been lifted. The next day, Adams responded that Student Affairs had completed its own assessment of the matter and confirmed that SJP “has been restored to full status.” Adams wrote in his April 24 email to SJP leadership that Student Affairs “acknowledges and fully supports OIE’s determination” regarding the post. Consistent with OIE, Adams reiterated that the original post needed to remain permanently removed from the group’s social media accounts. “Not complying may result in further review and potential disciplinary action under applicable policies,” Adams wrote. In a written statement to The Chronicle, SJP leadership, who asked that their names not be included due to previous doxing concerns, expressed frustration with University administrators for having imposed the freeze. They described Student Affairs’ decision as a move that fell under “a broader pattern” of the University “silencing, sidelining and suppressing its own students,” citing the end of the Black Student Alliance Invitational as an example. “It took over a month of written correspondence, legal counsel and public advocacy for our organization to access the basic procedural rights Duke's own policies guarantee to every student organization,” SJP leadership wrote. “The fact that we had to fight at all is the problem.” Though the freeze has been lifted, SJP had previously contested Student Affairs’ jurisdiction to impose the measure in the first place. Under PPDHRM, OIE holds the authority to enact measures against student organizations. Adams told The Chronicle in an April 27 email that Student Affairs uses “interim measures as a non-disciplinary step" when "complaints are referred to OIE and/or during an ongoing Student Affairs review of an organization’s activity.” He previously told SJP in a March 26 email that the Dean of Students office held independent authority to implement interim measures on recognized student organizations, saying that organizational recognition is a “privilege” that comes with responsibilities. Gooding echoed Adams in an April 2 email to SJP leadership, saying that while OIE holds authority under PPDHRM, “reported conduct may fall within the purview of more than one University policy.” Adams attributed the decision to freeze SJP’s funding and group status to complaints about this specific post as well as "the cumulative context of prior conversations" with SJP leadership over the past two years regarding the group’s social media activity. In an April 14 response to The Chronicle’s inquiry on the intention of the cartoon in the general body meeting post, SJP leadership said the image “was never intended to be antisemitic and we do not believe it is.” They said the cartoon was a critique of Zionism as a “political movement,” and that “criticizing it is not the same as targeting Jewish people.” Students Supporting Israel leadership, including senior Noyah Shebshaievitz, junior Shira Shasha and sophomore Noa Miasnik, said in a written statement to The Chronicle that while they acknowledged SJP’s intent and respected OIE’s decision, they felt the post still caused harm that needed to be addressed, even if it did not meet a “formal threshold” determined by the University. “We respect that process and trust that it was done with due diligence, though we disagree with the conclusion that the group’s post does not contribute to a hostile environment,” they wrote. “... Duke’s process has concluded, but the broader climate in which incidents like this occur does not resolve with an administrative finding.” Now that their status has been restored, SJP leadership promised to continue organizing for “the liberation of Palestinian and oppressed people everywhere.” “No administrative action, no freeze and no external pressure campaign will change that,” they wrote.

title: Duke alumnus recounts Holocaust survivor Abe Piasek’s story at Holocaust Remembrance Day event
subtitle: Steve Goldberg, Trinity ‘90 and Graduate School ‘95, told the story of Holocaust survivor and friend Abe Piasek at a Holocaust Remembrance Day event in Gross Hall.
author: Ava Littman
text: Over 30 Duke students gathered in Gross Hall on April 15 to hear Steve Goldberg, Trinity ‘90 and Graduate School ‘95, tell the story of Holocaust survivor, and former Triangle resident, Abe Piasek. The event — co-hosted by Durham Alpha Epsilon Pi and the Coalition for Preserving Memory, a student group which seeks to honor the victims of genocide — was held in observance of Holocaust Remembrance Day, or Yom HaShoah, which took place from the evening of April 13 to the evening of April 14. “The idea is just to stop and pause and reflect on what the meaning of the Holocaust is,” Goldberg said. Piasek, referred to by Goldberg as “Abe”, died in 2020 at age 91 in Raleigh, where he lived for the last decade of his life. A few days before Piasek’s death, he asked Goldberg, a history teacher in the Triangle, to keep telling his story. Since then, Goldberg has shared Piasek’s story over 200 times through his initiative “My Friend Abe.” Goldberg’s presentation included video clips of Piasek speaking alongside his own retelling of Piasek’s story. Piasek was born in Poland in 1928, but, after the Nazi invasion of Poland in 1939, Jews started to be forcibly confined into ghettos. Goldberg played a video of Piasek telling the story of a near-death incident when he and his friend snuck out of the ghetto. “One of [the Nazis] took out the pistol, shot my friend in the head and he fell down,” Piasek said in the video. “And I ran so fast they were shooting at me, and they didn't hit me. I was hiding in the place where I used to [play] hide and seek; they couldn't find me.” Then, in 1942, Piasek’s uncle, Jacob, had to make what Goldberg described as a “choiceless choice.” Jacob was part of the Judenrat, Jewish councils established by the Nazis to oversee ghettos and serve as intermediaries between the occupied Jewish population and the Nazis. The Judenrat in Piasek’s ghetto were told to send one member of each household from the ghetto to forced labor camps, meaning Jacob had to send a member of his own family. That’s how, at age 13, Piasek ended up at Radom, which according to Goldberg was a “slave labor camp” in Poland. Piasek never saw his family again. Piasek wore the same clothes that he arrived with for two years while at Radom. There, he worked in an arms factory, and was brutally whipped when he would make a mistake. As the U.S.S.R. army advanced from the East, the Germans destroyed the factory and Piasek, alongside the other forced laborers, were marched 55 miles to a train station. Piasek was placed in a cattle car crammed with other people, and he was sent to Auschwitz. Once he arrived, he survived the selection process, where people were either delegated to work or be gassed to death, with the help of two friends lifting him, making him look taller and more capable of work. Piasek was then sent to Vaihingen an der Enz, a labor camp in Germany, where his job was to haul heavy bags of concrete. “If you dropped your bag of concrete, they would machine gun you to death,” Goldberg said. “Abe recalled having to step around bodies of people who [the Nazis] had left there intentionally.” Soon after, Piasek was sent to Hessental, another German camp, and from there was put on a train to Dachau, where he was finally liberated by the U.S. Army. After the war, Piasek spent another two years in displaced persons camps, which were run by the Allied authorities to house uprooted survivors, before being able to make his way to the United States. Goldberg shared a photo taken by Piasek’s friend while aboard the boat that carried them across the Atlantic Ocean, which he kept and later framed. It showed the Statue of Liberty and read, “August 3, 1947, freedom for me.” Piasek had been sharing his story with communities across North Carolina since 2009, but he had not always felt comfortable sharing his experience. It was only once he was interviewed by the Shoah Foundation in 1995 did Piasek slowly start to open up. Goldberg first met Piasek two years before his death when he spoke to students at Research Triangle High School in Durham County, where Goldberg taught history. After their initial meeting, Goldberg interviewed Piasek in his home for over two hours, and they quickly became friends. Piasek even accompanied Goldberg’s class to the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington where he stood in an exhibit of a cattle car and told the story of his liberation from a car just like it. Those who knew Piasek, including Goldberg, described him as a “joyful man.” Goldberg talked about how Piasek’s granddaughter Katie, described Piasek to him after Piasek’s death. “Katie said something really profound to me. She said, ‘I wish Billy [her son] could hear Abe's classic jokey, cheerful voice,’” Goldberg said. “I think those are perfect adjectives to describe Abe.” As the talk concluded, Goldberg challenged the audience to “honor Abe’s legacy.” Piasek had poignant words for the crowd in the final videos Goldberg showed. “Don’t hate anybody. Even if you don’t like somebody, don’t hate,” Piasek said in one of the videos. “That’s my motto for the kids.”

title: ‘Community builder’: Undergrad Abigail Yohannes remembered for kindness, welcoming spirit
subtitle: The University announced the death of Yohannes April 5. Classmates and professors remembered her as a bubbly, selfless and perceptive friend.
author: Lucas Lin, Ella Moore
text: Duke undergraduate Abigail Yohannes is remembered as a caring friend, a grounding presence and a thoughtful student who always looked to uplift those around her. Yohannes, a senior in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences, died unexpectedly April 5 in the Hollows residential quad. Hailing from Lawrenceville, Ga., Yohannes was pursuing a major in sociology and part of Alpha Kappa Delta, the sociology honors society. “Abigail was definitely one of the most loving, selfless people,” Nahom Theodros, a sophomore who had grown up with Yohannes, said. “She was very considerate of people. She always had a smile on her so her energy was always amazing.” Many describe Yohannes as a person who was always present for others, especially her family and friends. She could often be found hanging out with peers in the Mary Lou Williams Center for Black Culture, enjoying black tea in Bella Union or attending events with the Duke Ethiopian/Eritrean Student Transnational Association (DESTA). Theodros recalled that Yohannes’ support was a big part of his decision to come to Duke, describing her as “loving” and an “open resource” while he navigated his acceptance. Besides expressing excitement, Yohannes provided Theodros with a “very detailed in-and-out synopsis” of Duke and all of its offerings, which convinced him to attend the University. “She was so excited to give as much information as possible as to how to thrive as a freshman, and just give me all the resources she thought a Duke freshman should have,” Theodros said. “Just showcasing her energy and excitement to both of us being able to attend the same school.” Theodros said Yohannes was a “big addition” in fostering close-knit friendships among DESTA members. Yohannes, who was Ethiopian, had previously served two years on the executive board of DESTA as treasurer, and was significant in shaping DESTA as well as the community within the Mary Lou Williams Center. “My biggest takeaway is just her bubbly energy and how welcoming she is, and how much of a pleasure it is being around her,” Theodros said. What stood out about Yohannes was her commitment to making others feel included, according to senior Chanté Davis. If she saw someone standing off to the side in a room or left out from an inside joke, she would approach them and bring them into the conversation. “She was one of the people in the group that always made you feel seen,” Davis said. Yohannes was also a “moral compass” in her friend group. Davis recalled that although Yohannes could “chill in the background,” she often stepped up to hold people accountable. As an underclassman, while masks were still required, she and Yohannes were riding back on the bus from an event and noticed that some fellow students had their masks down. “‘Boy, pull that mask up,’” Davis recalled Yohannes saying, a memory she said has kept popping up in her mind as she thinks of her friend. “She was so sweet, so kind, but she could also read people, like she would have her little clapbacks here and there.” In the classroom, Yohannes is remembered for being present and opening up spaces for her peers. When recalling her first impression of Yohannes, Anna Storti, assistant professor of gender, sexuality & feminist studies, described her as a “deep and attentive listener.” She added that Yohannes carried an “intentional presence,” whether she was speaking or not, and that her curiosity and ability to connect ideas was coupled by her attention to power dynamics in the classroom. “She does that thing that fewer students know how to do, which is really just try to make the space as equitable as possible,” Storti said. Storti noticed that Yohannes paid particular attention to the relationship between the self and the broader world. She was also interested in issues and inequities of race, gender and disabilities. Davis especially appreciated Yohannes’ ability to bring the “humanities perspective” to their discussions. “I'll personally remember her just being the exemplified version of a Duke student: well read, opening up space for others, teaching others to be kind, having a passion for learning and just being yourself,” Davis said. To Storti, Yohannes was a “community builder” whose incredible generosity and thoughtfulness should challenge others to be more connective. Storti said that “really bringing honor to [Yohannes’] legacy” means “allowing ourselves to be changed and be better, to be better listeners, to be more understanding.” You can read community members' remembrances of Yohannes here .

title: Send us your graduation photos
subtitle: Graduates, we want to see your faces! Each year, we highlight graduation photos in celebration of commencement. Submit yours by Friday, May 1.
author: Dylan Halper
text: The Chronicle is starting its coverage of 2026 commencement, and we can’t do it without our graduates. Seniors, you should be proud of your accomplishments, and we look forward to honoring your time at Duke. Each year, we highlight graduation photos in celebration of commencement. Submit them via this Google form by Friday, May 1 at 11:59 p.m. You can submit solo photos or photos with your friends! If you are having trouble, please email dylan@dukechronicle.com with any questions. Seniors, congratulations on graduating, and stay tuned next week for our pre-commencement coverage.

title: ‘Immensely disappointed’: Student organizations criticize end of Black Student Alliance Invitational
subtitle: The Black Student Alliance and 13 other student organizations sent a joint letter to Duke administrators Friday expressing concerns about “the future of Black Duke,” following the University’s decision to pause the Black Student Alliance Invitational.
author: Dylan Halper, Ella Moore
text: The Black Student Alliance and 13 other student organizations sent a joint letter to Duke administrators Friday morning criticizing the end of the Black Student Alliance Invitational and outlining demands to reemphasize the University’s commitment to its Black students. The letter expressed concerns about “the future of Black Duke” given the end of BSAI, which introduced prospective first-year students to Duke from a Black perspective through networking events, mentorship opportunities with upperclassmen, cultural performances and a two-night stay in East or West Campus residence halls. Individual students, student organizations and affinity groups all sent copies of the letter to various senior administrators at 9 a.m., including President Vincent Price, Mary Pat McMahon, vice provost and vice president of student affairs and other Student Affairs personnel. “With the silent dismantling of the Black Student Alliance Invitational, Black students, alumni and faculty are both outraged and immensely disappointed in the Duke administration,” the letter said. The Chronicle first reported the pause of BSAI on April 9. Prior to the article’s publication, the University had released no public communication regarding the status of the program, which the letter said “reflects a clear disregard for student voices and input.” Sophomore Ashley Coburn, incoming president of BSA, said that the letter was an important step in communicating to Duke that it “cannot just silently remove something that means so much to so many students” without transparency. “We just feel like there are better ways to communicate or just acknowledge what's going on and not just leave students in the dark,” Coburn added. According to emails obtained by The Chronicle after publication, McMahon and Executive Vice Provost Mohammad Noor — alongside members of Duke’s Strategic Enrollment team — will meet with members of BSA and other student organizations to address the concerns raised in the letter “and talk through next steps.” “We also appreciate the care for the Duke community reflected in the messages we’ve received today,” McMahon and Noor wrote in a Friday evening email response to multiple students who sent copies of the letter this morning. Earlier Friday at 4:14 p.m., a University spokesperson wrote in an email to The Chronicle, “We appreciate the Black Student Alliance sharing their concerns ... Fostering connections to programs that reflect and support students’ identities and communities — including for our Black students — remains central to the program.” Junior Gabriela Mendoza, one of the BSAI co-chairs, said that administrators told her that BSAI would not occur during a meeting initially described as a preliminary planning session. As part of the change, Mendoza and her co-chairs’ positions were renamed to “Impact Ambassadors," and BSAI was replaced with the new “ Blue Devil Nights .” Mendoza felt that she applied to be BSAI co-chair under “false pretenses” and that the University did not provide adequate transparency in its decision-making on the program. The spokesperson added that Blue Devil Days and Nights were designed in consultation with student leaders associated with identity and cultural centers. They said that the University received “much positive feedback” on the Blue Devil Nights and looks forward to “refining our approach” to serve admitted students from diverse backgrounds in the future. The letter also aimed to challenge Duke administrators’ reasoning for pausing BSAI. Jenny Wood Crowley, associate vice provost for strategic enrollment management, previously told The Chronicle in an April 9 email that the decision to pause BSAI was made after a regular review of all University programming to “ensure they align with evolving legal guidance and institutional priorities.” The authors of the letter argue that since BSAI “takes place after ‘color-blind admissions,’” and therefore does not violate the Supreme Court ruling banning race-based affirmative action, they deserve more clarification on what legal guidance the University was referring to in its decision. The letter also noted the timing of BSAI’s closure, saying the University is following a trend of the “erasure of infrastructure that aims to support and uplift the broader Duke community.” It pointed to a 2024 decision to close the Reginaldo Howard Scholarship program — which provided full-ride scholarships to “top applicants of African descent” — in the wake of the Supreme Court ruling. In addition to BSAI, the University also halted Latino Student Recruitment Weekend, which served a similar function for prospective Latino first-year students. Junior Aaron Andino, co-president of Mi Gente, which also signed on to the letter, criticized the lack of communication regarding that decision. “Mi Gente specifically never received any communication from administration about the removal of LSRW, which I think for a lot of us, we would have appreciated,” Andino said. To address the outlined concerns, the letter listed five specific demands for the University designed “to increase transparency in decision-making and preserve spaces specifically tailored to Black members of the Duke community.” The demands include the reinstatement of BSAI, a meeting with Black student leaders and a public statement from Student Affairs that will provide “full transparency” on the reasons for BSAI’s cancellation. Additional demands involve increasing communication between University administrators and Black student leaders, including through monthly meetings of the President’s Council on Black Affairs . Finally, the letter calls for a “visible acknowledgment” of the Allen Building Takeover, which occurred in 1969 when over 50 students — the majority of whom were Black — occupied administrative offices to raise awareness around Black students’ concerns of exclusion from Duke’s academics and campus culture. Mendoza said she felt the demands were neither “difficult” nor “obscure." “They have the plans to do it, they have the facilities to do it, they have the administration to do it,” Mendoza said. “It’s just a matter of if they want to.” The Duke Student Government Executive Board also endorsed the letter, with DSG President Angela Chen, a junior, saying that it is important for DSG to use its platform to support student affinity groups. “Now, more than ever, the University should be investing in programs that bolster care for minority communities,” Chen said. In addition to BSA, the DSG Executive Board and Mi Gente, the letter was also signed by the National Pan-Hellenic Council, Black Pre-Law Society, Duke Persian Student Association, Beyond Borders, Duke Chapter of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People, Duke Chapter of the National Society of Black Engineers, BlackGen Capital Duke, Sunrise Duke, Black Muslim Coalition and DukeAFRICA. Editor's note: This story was updated at 7:50 p.m. to reflect McMahon and Noor’s responses to student emails.

title: The Chronicle’s guide to 2026 residence hall move-out
subtitle: As the semester nears its end, The Chronicle has compiled everything Duke students need to know to prepare for move-out.
author: Lila Cohen
text: As students prepare for final exams and finish lengthy papers, moving out may not be top of mind. In case you’ve missed the last few emails from Housing and Residence Life, The Chronicle has compiled the key information and dates you need to be aware of to ensure a seamless start to your summer. Campus residence halls and apartments will close May 4 at noon. Graduating seniors and students who have received approval for extended housing may remain in their assigned housing until May 11 at noon, the day after Commencement. Students can apply for extending housing on the Housing Portal , with applications considered on a rolling basis. Those who are approved might be asked to relocate to a different location on campus and will be charged a daily rate of $45.44 for a double, $60.08 for a single or shared apartment bedroom or $74.67 for a private apartment bedroom. The rates represent a slight increase from last year. Before checking out, students should remove all personal belongings from their rooms, dispose of all trash and recyclables and ensure all original furniture is present. Students can incur charges if their room needs to be excessively cleaned, if there is damage to any part of the room or if the original furniture is misplaced. Then, students should check out via the form posted on the doors of their dorm rooms. If students have room keys, they should be returned to the relevant HRL Office – 300 Swift, Blue Light, Lancaster Commons, Southgate or Craven House D – in a check-out envelope labeled with the student’s name and Unique ID. Lost or unreturned room keys will result in a $135 fee. Students not returning to Duke should turn in their mailbox key to the Student Mailbox Center located in the basement of the Bryan Center. Those approved to stay for the summer — which includes students taking classes in either Summer Session I or II, international or DKU students and Plus (+) Program participants — can move in on May 12 ahead of Term I, or June 28 ahead of Term II. Devils Care Donation will place donation bins in all residence halls and apartment common spaces during move-out until mid-May. Students can donate non-perishable food, clothing, school supplies, lamps, books, gently used bedding, electronics and general household items. The Kenan Ethics living-learning community will also host a “Really Really Free Market” for students to donate items from 1 to 3 p.m. on April 26 on the East Duke lawn. More information is available on their website for those that need assistance picking up larger items. Students looking to store their belongings in Durham over the summer can do so through several local storage units and moving companies that service Duke’s campus. Student-run services include University Shipping & Storage and Storage Scholars , which will pick up boxes from students’ rooms and deliver them back in the fall. Storage Scholars can also ship belongings to a student’s home, while University Shipping & Storage can deliver students’ belongings anywhere along the East Coast north of Durham. title: Churros, Coca-Cola compliments and catalysis poetry: A look into Duke’s last day of classes celebrations
subtitle: From free food to puppies to cornhole games outside the Chapel, students commemorated the last day of classes with a variety of campus activities.
author: Jackson Kitts, Seyun Park, Ella Moore, Luca Gragnoli, Lila Cohen, Lila De Almeida
text: As the last day of classes for the academic year began, students fanned out across campus to celebrate. From puppy guest stars and magical performances on Bryan Center Plaza to Chapel climbs and competitive cornhole games, here are some of the scenes across campus from LDOC. The hum of Duke’s main dining hall is undisturbed, for now. Students sit at tables finishing breakfast, studying or squeezing in one more coffee chat. Outside, on BC Plaza, it’s similarly business as usual. But as students walk to class, two groups of friends have already set up shop, engaged in thoughtful conversation, mimosas in hand. Students and staff, donning olive-green LDOC shirts, are still finishing decorations. Some hang up streamers, while another group struggles as they erect a small canopy next to the Bryan Center's University Store. Another student, climbing up a ladder, reaches up a tree to tie a banner. The cool breeze drifts by in the early morning, the air relatively quiet except for the occasional click — click, click, click. It’s the Poetry Fox, sitting in his chair, typing away at his typewriter in his signature orange, furry suit. The process is simple: students approach the Fox with any word they can imagine, no matter how strange, and he transforms it into a custom poem within minutes. “The last student who came by gave me the word ‘catalysis,’ which is a chemical process,” the Fox said. “So I wrote him a catalysis poem.” The Fox expects to write 60 to 70 poems today despite the rising heat. But he won’t slow down, because even though it gets hot in the fox suit, he’ll have Gatorade to fuel his stanzas. The buzz on campus steadily grows, starting with the occasional thump of a bean bag against a cornhole board. A pair of friends take turns with their tosses, without success. At 10:43 a.m., though, the cornholers’ luck changes. One friend finally sinks a bean bag, then another, then a third. He crosses his arms and nods at his competitor with a smug smile. A few more minutes pass before they mutually lose interest and walk away. Behind the cornholers, a steady flow of students claims their LDOC T-shirts — at the moment, only the large and extra-large piles remain. 15 minutes later, only extra-large. A line stretched across the plaza early Wednesday morning as students waited to visit with two guest stars on campus. Two puppies, one around 8 pounds, the other about 2, small enough to fit in the palms of your hands. The pups were soon greeted by their elder, Gold, the beloved golden retriever frequently spotted on BC Plaza and the Brodhead Center as students rush to class. “They were so cute ... they just wanted to climb all over you,” junior Tali Sigal said. LDOC, she said, is off to a good start. “Step up, say something nice,” prompts a Coca-Cola vending “machine” next to the University Store. A man stands concealed inside the machine and sticks out his red-gloved hand to reward students with cans of Minute Maid lemonade in exchange for their kind words. “Duke is the best school ever,” says one student into the microphone. Others comment on their excitement for LDOC, the beautiful weather or how much they love their friends. The man-machine occasionally replies — “We love Duke too" — and also dispenses hats, T-shirts, tumblers and Coca-Cola water bottles for particularly thought-out compliments. “Coke is far superior to Pepsi,” first-year Dani Levy says. She earns a drink and stickers. Besides the puppies and Coca-Cola, there was another type of magic on BC Plaza today: the Comedy Magician. David Lucas doesn’t look like a magician at all. He dons a blue polo shirt instead of a top hat and has a pocket full of foam balls instead of a rabbit. Nonetheless, he transfixes every cluster of BC Plaza goers as they pass. He transports coins between his hands and into the ears of his watchers. His hands move so confidently and swiftly that when he asks first-year doctoral student Callie Hundley to take off her left shoe, she does not hesitate. Lucas breaks into a smile when it becomes clear that her shoe is empty. He’s the comedy magician after all — half of his tricks are pure sarcasm. “I can’t believe I took my shoe off for that man,” Hundley said. David Lucas, the Comedy Magician As students rejoiced in LDOC festivities across campus, some students opted to stay indoors and study. Indeed, Perkins Library was far from empty on Wednesday morning, with many sporting concert attire as they diligently typed away on their laptops. Some students were calmly studying, while others scrambled to finish last-minute assignments. Despite six separate attempts, students in Perkins declined to comment to The Chronicle about their studying, citing impending deadlines or exams. The cornhole games and popcorn-making were suddenly interrupted by the black-cloaked members of the Old Trinity Club. Some students stared and whispered to their friends; most seemed undeterred. Concealed with sunglasses and equipped with a beer or two, the crows walked in unpredictable directions and shouted out unintelligible phrases, with a “Duke” thrown in here and there. One member grabbed a blue cornhole bag and tossed it high in the air. After about two minutes, the black-cloaked members descended the steps of the Chapel, ready to take on the day with a few well-placed bows and waves. From Ginger & Soy to Cafe, the Brodhead Center’s restaurants came out in full force to help its diners celebrate LDOC. The Chronicle set out to inform its readers of the dos and don’ts of the special LDOC meals. Thai Turkey Burger - Chef’s Kitchen (Smashers) - 5/10 The burger did not have the most compelling presentation, and unfortunately, its small portion was not offset by amazing quality. The only Thai component of the burger that Smashers presented for LDOC was the spicy sauce. Incidentally, that was also the only redeeming part. Even with the spicy sauce, the burger itself was quite dry. Stir-Fry Noodles with Chicken - Ginger & Soy - 6/10 For a restaurant as beloved as Ginger & Soy, the stakes were high. The noodles, layered within carrots and chicken, looked pleasing to the eye. Unfortunately, looks did more than taste with this dish. 10-inch Caramel Filled Churro - Sazón - 8/10 The desserts saved LDOC. The churro from Sazón truly holds as a masterpiece of baking. Though the caramel inside was hard to identify, the outside was perfect. The fried texture was everything a Duke student could wish for in a churro. Gianduja Chocolate Mousse Cake With Vanilla Gelato - Cafe - 8.5/10 Last but not least, the chocolate cake at Cafe. With the chocolate syrup drizzled, two vanilla gelato scoops and a not-too-rich mousse, the plate was well-balanced. The cake was less desirable — a bit too thick and sweet — but the dessert overall is a perfect treat for students to enjoy on LDOC. With the encouraging backdrop of the Old Trinity Club members’ caws outside, some of the Sports staff huddle around a ping pong table discussing probate law. Various staff members run in and out of the office, clad in white dresses, jean shorts and black boots. One girl sits at the table working on a military history paper, conveniently due at 11:59 p.m. Two editors sit on their laptops preparing to publish this article, so that they can finally join the LDOC festivities they’ve written so much about.

title: First-years report mostly liberal political beliefs, low religiosity and support for institutional neutrality
subtitle: For the ninth consecutive year, The Chronicle surveyed the first-year class about their political leanings, religious beliefs and lifestyles.
author: Lila Cohen
text: For the ninth consecutive year, The Chronicle surveyed the first-year class about their political leanings, religious beliefs and lifestyles. Alongside questions about political discourse and voter registration, we gauged student sentiment on topical issues in higher education, including diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives, institutional neutrality and legacy students. Like previous classes, members of the Class of 2029 generally lean to the left. Just over two-thirds of our respondents identified as very or somewhat liberal, and less than 10% identified as very or somewhat conservative. The proportion of our respondents who identified as either moderate or conservative is lower than surveyed students in both the previous years but more in line with trends displayed by earlier classes. Although many members of the Class of 2029 came of voting age during an off-election year, most first-years were still civic-minded. About 71.20% of our respondents said they were registered to vote, with just over half of those students registering in North Carolina — 38.00% of the total sample. Another 9.20% said they were ineligible to vote due to factors like age or citizenship status, and the remaining 19.60% were unregistered. The proportion of eligible but unregistered first-years is 13 percentage points higher than last year’s survey , when voter registration movements swept many college campuses — including Duke’s — in the lead-up to the 2024 election. First-years who identified as somewhat conservative or moderate were least likely to be registered to vote at 26.32% and 25.00%, respectively. Of respondents who were registered to vote, those who identified as somewhat conservative or moderate were more likely to be registered in their home state, while those who identified as somewhat or very liberal were more likely to be registered in North Carolina. Only three students identified as very conservative, so their responses were not analyzed as reflective of a larger population. Most surveyed first-years said they do not have trouble sharing their political beliefs on campus. Almost two-thirds found it somewhat or very easy to express their political opinions freely on campus — 38.40% and 25.20%, respectively. About 11.20% found it somewhat difficult, and only 2.40% described it as very difficult. The remaining 22.80% found it neither easy nor difficult. Very liberal and somewhat liberal students were most likely to describe sharing their political beliefs as easy. By contrast, conservative and moderate students were most likely to portray sharing their opinions as neither easy nor difficult. This year, we gave first-years the option to explain why they find it relatively easy or difficult to express their political opinions freely on campus. Our respondents gave mixed reviews on the state of discourse at Duke. Several first-years identified a liberal-leaning culture at Duke. For many who align with those opinions, that meant “most people either agree with or at the very least are familiar with and respect my political views,” as one respondent who identified as very liberal and found it very easy to express their views wrote. “I feel like I share similar political beliefs with the people I surround myself with, which makes it easier to share my opinions without fear of judgment,” added another. But others, including one somewhat conservative respondent who found it very difficult to express their views, asserted that their peers may “shun and outcast people if they have the wrong opinion.” A few students worried about being “targeted or looked at differently” for expressing their views, citing “fear of judgement from fellow classmates.” Still others disagreed with the premise that discourse is siloed at Duke, with many writing that “all perspectives are valued on campus” and that “students have generally been welcoming of other individuals' political beliefs.” Multiple international students in the respondent pool raised concerns that sharing their political opinions could leave them vulnerable, citing visa concerns and a “lack of free speech protections.” Just as we did in last year’s survey, we asked first-years about their opinions on institutional neutrality, based on a definition drawn from the University of Chicago’s Kalven Report : “A university, as an institution, will remain neutral on political and social issues and leave political pronouncements to individual faculty and students, except when circumstances arise that ‘threaten the very mission of the university and its values of free inquiry.’” A small majority of respondents said they would support Duke’s adoption of such a policy, while less than a quarter said they would be opposed. Support is up from last year’s survey, where 46.88% of respondents approved of institutional neutrality. Conservative students were more in support of adopting institutional neutrality than their liberal peers, with a 57.89% majority of somewhat conservative students expressing strong support. “Somewhat support” was the most common response for respondents of every other political leaning. Nearly half of surveyed students — 49.20% — feel Duke is doing just enough to consider diversity, equity and inclusion in hiring, curriculum, student life and campus activities generally. Of the remainder, more students feel Duke isn’t doing enough to consider DEI than expressed that the University is doing too much. Liberal students were the most likely to think Duke is not doing enough to emphasize DEI, while somewhat conservative and moderate students were the most likely to think Duke is emphasizing DEI too much. But a large proportion of respondents of all political leanings maintained that the University is doing just enough. This year, we asked students for the first time whether they support preferences for legacy students in Duke’s admissions process. Duke is one of roughly a quarter of four-year U.S. colleges and universities who consider legacy status, or whether an applicant’s parent or sibling previously attended the institution, in their admission process — a declining practice . A majority of our Class of 2029 respondents said they oppose legacy admissions — 28.80% somewhat and 27.20% strongly. Another 26.40% indicated they are neither in support of or opposed to the practice. About 14.80% said they somewhat support legacy admissions at Duke, and only 2.80% said they strongly support the practice. Opinions on legacy admissions were closely aligned with political leanings. A majority of liberal first-years said they opposed the practice, while equal amounts of somewhat conservative students opposed the practice and expressed no opinion on it. Respondents’ household income levels didn’t seem to be correlated with their opinion on legacy admissions. Perhaps unsurprisingly, first-years with a parent or sibling who attended Duke were much more in favor of preferences for legacy students in University admissions than their non-legacy peers. Duke students come from a variety of religious backgrounds, with over 20 faith groups represented on campus. Three in five respondents, however, reported low religiosity. Only about 12.00% surveyed first-years said they were very religious, while another 28.00% considered themselves somewhat religious. The remainder identified as not very or not at all religious, at 27.20% and 32.80%, respectively. In line with this trend, two in five of our respondents reported nonreligious identities — 22.00% agnostic and 18.40% atheist. About a third of our respondents identified as Christian: 16.00% Protestant, 12.00% Catholic and 6.00% another form of Christianity. Muslim first-years were the next-largest group at 6.40%, followed by Jewish first-years at 6.00%, Hindu first-years at 5.60% and Buddhist first-years at 4.40%. The remaining 3.20% of respondents identified with a religious affiliation not listed. Muslim students were most likely to describe themselves as very religious, followed by Protestant and Catholic students. Agnostic, atheist and Jewish students were the only groups with no students identifying as very religious. First-years who identified as more religious were also more likely to identify as conservative, and vice versa for non-religious and liberal students. The Class of 2029 has experienced its fair share of firsts. Nearly two-thirds of our Class of 2029 respondents said they had drunk alcohol, and a third had used some form of recreational drug — tobacco or nicotine, marijuana, or otherwise. Slightly fewer have had sex, and about one in five have used a fake ID. A third of our first-year respondents have done none of the above. About a quarter of our respondents, 26.40%, engaged in these behaviors for the first time since arriving at Duke.

title: The Triangle's print industry has retired. They're delivering what's left
subtitle: The last stewards of print media — publishers and delivery personnel — are not doing it to keep local journalism alive. They're doing what they've always done, for the people they've always done it with, for as long as someone still wants the product delivered.
author: Ana Despa
text: Steve Cannaday has been woken up by police twice at stoplights, passed out mid-route, engine still running, newspapers piled in the back. He pulls off to the side of the road only when he physically can’t drive any further. His record is 48 hours straight. Sometimes he doesn’t make it through the door of his house, falling asleep in the driveaway. He keeps his wife worried. Cannaday, 63, is one of the last print delivery personnel in the Triangle. His mornings start around 4:30 a.m. He loads his truck with prints by 6. His first drop at Wake County Schools — a big customer for Triangle Web Printing, the company he contracts for — is by 7. Most days he's going until 2 or 3 p.m., then picks up another load: the N.C. State Technician, the Elon News Network, a city paper in Lewisburg. Some weeks he drives to the mountains. Other weeks he is at the beach. He runs around 100,000 miles a year across three or four vehicles he mostly maintains by himself, and that’s still less than what it used to be. Cannaday has been delivering newspapers since 1974, when he was in fifth grade. He has no plans to retire. "It's in my blood," he says. "It's always what I've done." Steve’s father was diagnosed with cancer during his childhood, prompting the family to move back to North Carolina where his father picked up the family’s paper route delivering The Herald-Sun. Steve and his brother got up at 2 or 3 a.m., seven days a week, rolling papers and riding their bicycles alongside their father or mother. Steve was what they called a “hawker” or “newsboy,” selling newspapers on the street. He was 16 when his father died. He spent four years in the Navy after high school, then went straight back to the Herald-Sun. He worked his way from paper racks to district manager to head of the transportation department. Soon, he figured he could make more money by going independent and serving as a contractor for a host of papers. "There's not too many people like me around anymore," he says. That's not nostalgia. It's market analysis. Triangle Web's nearest competition is now in Greenville, S.C. They've stopped competing and instead link up, trading overflow print jobs back and forth. As the printing industry has hollowed out, those who stayed, like Cannaday, have become indispensable. Some days he makes $1,000. The routes have gotten longer. The people who used to share the roads with him at the crack of dawn are mostly gone. "I became good friends with people," he says. "Most of them are passed away now, or in too bad of health where they don't run no more." He told both his kids — now 31 and 35 — to get away from printing. The industry won't be there when they get older. And then, when they left, he kept going. *** At the INDY Week, Brenna Berry oversees the team responsible for distributing print across four counties in the Triangle. The INDY has survived the digitization of news that wiped out many of its peers. The newsroom prints twice a month now, down from weekly. It produces less copies. The work is less demanding than it used to be, that’s partly why Berry has continued to do it. She remembers the morning and afternoon print editions thrown out in the driveway during the 1980s and 1970s. They were practically served alongside morning Folgers coffee and toast. A journalism and English major in college, she found herself drawn to the business side of newsrooms. Berry started on the circulation team of the News & Observer in the late 1990s then moved to the INDY in 2013. “That’s where the jobs were,” she said. But at the turn of the century, circulation meetings were consumed by a single question no one could quite answer: how does print compete with all of this? “This” refers to the internet and the early rise of social media. "The truth is," she says now, "I don't think competing with it was ever what we should have tried to do." 9/11 was one of the last memories she has of print still feeling “pertinent.” At around 2 p.m., the newsroom got word they would be printing a special edition for that afternoon. In all Berry’s years, that had never happened. The News & Observer was bustling, filled with reporters trying to make sense of the day’s events and laptops humming as editors finalized the live accounts of history. Berry helped pick up the remaining newspapers from the rack the next day. “You couldn’t find a copy,” she recalled. There hasn’t been a similar need for print since. The information is online now. Part of the reason, Berry says, is because it's easier. With print, there's paper and ink, and there’s somebody driving the product around, distributing it. “It's an expensive deal,” Berry adds. “And digital, look how relatively easy it is. It takes fewer people. You can circulate more. You can get more clicks and more views.” *** Newsrooms started putting their content online in the late 1990s as the internet grew in popularity. It presented a sea of opportunity. Local newsrooms realized they could reach an audience of a size that was unfathomable just years ago. There were other incentives too. The marginal cost for a publication to add a subscriber to an online subscription versus a print one was near zero. Breaking news could be published immediately rather than being held until the next morning’s print. Local newsrooms didn’t do away with print immediately, though. According to Phil Napoli, James R. Shepley distinguished professor of public policy, media policy expert and former paperboy, early in the days of digital news there was the assumption that the increased exposure of the online product could bring in print subscriptions. Advertising dollars, too, were still more favorable in print per subscriber. There was a saying meant to poke fun at the changing supply and demand dynamics of the online versus print audiences that said papers were trading advertising print dollars for "digital nickels,” Napoli explained. Still, family ownership of local newsrooms gave way to corporate chains, advertising dollars trickled online and, as Berry recalls, the Thanksgiving paper shrank from three inches thick to something you could fold in your pocket. But newsrooms don’t really know why they rolled back print. “When people did survey research later on and asked local newspapers, why did you go online? The number one answer was because everybody else was doing it,” Napoli said. He added that the fatal mistake was the immediate pull of newsrooms placing their content online for free. A 2025 report on the state of local journalism from Northwestern University found that almost 40% of all local U.S. newspapers have vanished since 2005, leaving 50 million residents with limited or no access to a reliable source of local news. Print newspaper circulation dropped considerably further. Since 2005, circulation across the country has dropped by an estimated 80 million, a loss of 70%. In the Triangle, the News & Observer Publishing Company was one of the first to go online in 1994. The following year, the Daniels family sold the paper to McClatchy Newspapers, a corporation based out of Sacramento, Calif. In the early 2000s, a string of cost-cutting efforts in response to lower advertising revenue saw the newsroom lay off 70 staff members, then offer buyouts to the entire newsroom staff in 2008. In 2015, the newspaper announced it would sell its facility in downtown Raleigh for redevelopment, which permanently moved its printing presses to an auxiliary office in Garner, N.C. In March 2020, the paper decided to stop printing on Saturdays, citing readership shifting to online. The INDY was previously known as The North Carolina Independent before becoming the Independent Weekly in 1989, when it printed once a week. In 2023, it joined the Assembly network, a digital North Carolina-based news organization. The INDY now prints twice a month, down from weekly. The Herald-Sun based in Durham began in 1991 as a consolidation of the Durham Morning Herald and the Durham Sun, which had both been around since the early 1910s. In 2004, the Paxton Media Group bought the publication from the Rollins family. In 2016, Paxton sold the newspaper to McClatchy, becoming a sister company to The News & Observer. Despite both newspapers experiencing large scale cuts, The Herald Sun still prints daily. Berry thinks print has never quite figured out its place in the digital era. It’s still trying to compete when it should be building a different identity. She has found that long-form work and print editions around local elections move well, a niche that the INDY has tapped into. Yet, readers still experience an attention span problem that Berry finds concerning. Researchers at the University of Florida and University College London found that only 16 percent of Americans aged 15 and over read for leisure every day in 2023, a 40% decline over the past 20 years. "Especially at a paper like INDY, where we do a lot of long-form stuff — it requires you to sit down and pay attention and read the page," Berry said. "And we just don't live in that kind of culture anymore." Napoli has started asking himself whether print should be thought of the way we think about vinyl records — not dead, but transformed into something smaller, more intentional, for people who want it badly enough to seek it out. Lifestyle magazines, Berry notes, are actually doing well. There's still something compelling about a physical object, a glossy cover, a story you hold in your hands. She has noticed a shift in how the news industry talks about itself. When she started, papers marketed themselves solely as bearers of news. People subscribed because that was how you found out what happened at city council, or because someone you knew was mentioned and you wanted to cut it out and frame it. Nobody invoked the idea that local news preserves democracy, an argument that dominates many local newsrooms’ subscription pitches today; nobody talked about the First Amendment. The paper was just the paper. "That wasn't a pitch that existed years ago," she says of the current ‘support local journalism’ appeals. "But that's very much a thread that I am seeing and hearing more and more across the board." But at the end of the day, neither Berry nor Cannaday frames what they are doing in terms of “keeping local journalism alive.” They're doing what they've always done, for the people they've always done it with, for as long as someone still wants the product delivered. Cannaday still rolls pretty hard, but he takes weekends off now. At one point he was running routes for the Herald-Sun, Triangle Web and the Raleigh Offset Printing simultaneously, stacking contracts just to make sure the money was always there. "Money don't mean as much as it used to," he says. “I do well.” Berry continues to do it for the few like Cannaday who still distribute papers. “I've known those folks for a long time, and so I want to keep it going for them,” she says. As long as the owners of the INDY want to keep printing it, she says, she wants to help get it out. That's all it's ever been. Editor’s note: This story was submitted for a course in the DeWitt Wallace Center for Media and Democracy. The Chronicle provided further edits prior to publication. The Chronicle contracts with Triangle Web Printing and Cannaday.

title: Duke's annual powwow celebrates indigenous culture on campus
subtitle: The event, hosted by the Native American/Indigenous Student Alliance in collaboration with the Center for Multicultural Affairs, blended tradition with campus spirit, offering attendees an immersive experience and a firsthand opportunity to engage with indigenous culture.
author: Jackson Kitts
text: Penn Pavilion was alive with dancers in tribal regalia, the pulse of drums and the buzz of vendors at Saturday's powwow, celebrating the enduring strength and vitality of native culture at Duke. The annual event, hosted by the Native American/Indigenous Student Alliance (NAISA) in collaboration with the Center for Multicultural Affairs, blended tradition with campus spirit, offering attendees an immersive experience and a firsthand opportunity to engage with indigenous culture. This is Duke's 25th year hosting the event. From toddlers to elders, dancers of all ages embraced the celebration. Vendors offered everything from beadwork and clothing to traditional medicines, while the beat of drums echoed throughout the space. Associate Professor of Hydrology Ryan Emanuel, Trinity ‘99 and a member of the Lumbee Tribe, ran a table at the event representing the Duke Native American and Indigenous Alumni affinity group. Their goal is to raise awareness about Native American visibility on campus, something that Duke lacked during Emanuel’s time as a student. “To have a powwow at Duke is amazing because it shows that we really do have a presence here at the university, and that the university values our community and wants to promote our culture [and] presence here,” Emanuel said. NAISA was founded in 1992 and held its first powwow in 2001. Over the years, powwow has grown to include more tribes and participants, showing an expanding network of Native students and allies. Vice President of NAISA Amber White, a Navajo senior, said that powwow used to be held on Abele Quad with less attendance. “I feel like now we have a structured way of hosting,” White said. For many, the powwow is an opportunity for Native Americans to celebrate their culture at Duke. Emma Smith, a junior of Chickasaw and Choctaw descent and one of the co-presidents of NAISA, highlighted how powwow introduces cultural appreciation to the University community. “I really hope that people take away that Native students are at Duke,” Smith said. “We're still here.
