She explained that although Trump’s FY27 budget request proposes a $5 billion cut to the NIH, approximately a 12% reduction in its overall budget, she expects Congress will not agree to the proposal. Last year, after Trump requested around a 40% cut to the NIH, Congress actually increased funding by about 1%, according to Liao. Liao also said that last week, the Trump administration gave up its legal fight to impose a 15% cap on indirect cost reimbursement rates for research funding from the NIH. The policy was blocked in court in January, but the Department of Justice did not appeal to the Supreme Court by the necessary deadline. The University had previously cited this proposed cap — which would limit the amount of money the University receives for research costs related to facilities or administrative matters — as a significant threat to University funding. “We’re certainly happy that the court ruling is final and that our costs are current and safe as they are,” Liao said. Amid the rosier federal outlook, Heather Hamby, executive vice dean of administration, said that the school would continue to evaluate its costs to be able to engage in “strategic investments.” Last summer, the school announced plans to cut $125 million from its budget to prepare for less federal funding, but Hamby did not provide updates as to the school’s progress toward that goal. However, Hamby did say that there is no hiring freeze in effect. Instead, the school engages with hiring on an “evaluation of request” basis, where a committee assesses the necessity of the hire and whether it is a replacement or new position. “We are not on a freeze, but I do think we've been very cautious, given our economic situation,” Hamby said. She still cautioned researchers to be responsible with how they use their research funding, despite the more positive funding landscape. “We are wanting each of you to plan carefully with your leadership on the best use and the most prioritized use of funds,” Hamby said. “ ... That's kind of our new world.” As the University seeks to embed AI across campus, the School of Medicine is working on its own approach to the technology. “I want to take a moment to encourage our entire community to thoughtfully embrace AI — not as an abstract concept, but as a tool that can help make our work more efficient and allow us to focus more fully on our impact,” Klotman said. To that end, Klotman recently announced the appointment of Christopher Lindsell, former director of data science and biostatistics for the Duke Clinical Research Institute, to serve in a new position as vice dean for data science and AI starting this month. In the two-year role, Lindsell will be tasked with coordinating all efforts on AI across the school by developing “durable practices and investments” to help lead the nation on AI strategy. “Technology is moving quickly, expectations are rising and the pressure to both act and succeed is real,” Lindsell said. Lindsell said that AI has the capability to ease administrative burdens and help doctors engage in better clinical care and decisionmaking. Still, Klotman emphasized that the school does not intend for AI to replace the human interactions at the center of health care. “AI is a tool that should enhance our ability to teach, learn, discover and care for patients, but certainly not distance us from each other,” she said.

title: Jewish Life at Duke’s Host Your Own Seder program facilitates cultural exchange during Passover
subtitle: The Host Your Own Seder program, along with communal Seders hosted by Jewish Life at Duke and Chabad at Duke, helped students to observe the traditions of Passover in community with Jewish and non-Jewish peers.
author: Lila Cohen
text: From the Rubenstein Arts Center to East Campus residence halls, Seders — religious dinners traditionally hosted on the first and second nights of the Jewish holiday Passover — took place all over campus last week as part of Jewish Life at Duke’s Host Your Own Seder program. Jewish and non-Jewish Seder attendees reflected on their experiences observing Passover at Duke, sharing cultures and traditions. In addition to attending student-hosted Seders, community members also had the option to participate in communal Seders at the Freeman Center for Jewish Life or Chabad at Duke’s Fleishman House. First-year Eli Sobel hosted a Seder in the basement of Gilbert-Addoms residence hall — called “G.A. Down Under” or "GADU" — on April 1. Ahead of his Seder, Sobel created the haggadah, a book containing readings and prayers that explain the story of Passover and the rituals of the meal, by combining historical details with elements from Sobel and his co-host’s personal traditions. Of his 10 Seder attendees, only two were Jewish. “They were all pretty enthusiastic,” he said, describing the non-Jewish attendees. Sobel incorporated a small play about the story of Passover into the Seder, asking all attendees to participate. He served as the play’s narrator and described the play as “the most successful part” of the meal. One attendee, who does not typically celebrate the holiday, decided to try keeping kosher for Passover after attending the Seder. “Opportunities like this don't come around very often, and when they do, it's great to really take advantage,” Sobel said, adding that his friends are “all very big on showing off [their] own individual cultures and religions to each other.” Although JLD’s Host Your Own Seder program typically occurs on the first night of Passover, Hoof ‘n Horn had scheduled a show of its spring production Ragtime for the holiday’s second night, and senior Lily Egol wanted to ensure she could observe the holiday with fellow Jewish peers in the musical theater group. Egol, co-music director and pianist for Ragtime, reached out to JLD about hosting a Seder on April 2. After receiving approval, she organized a shortened Seder before Hoof ‘n Horn’s evening show, encouraging all to participate. “I’ve always wanted to host a Seder,” Egol said. “It was really cool to take some of my family's traditions and bring them to a new group of people and celebrate with friends.” Egol also added that she hopes that Hoof ‘n Horn will continue to host Seders after she graduates this spring. First-year Arielle Misra, who had never been to a Seder before, described it as “really interesting.” Misra learned about the event through Hoof ‘n Horn and decided to attend. “I think people are a little bit scared to go outside of their comfort zone ... It's good that there are a lot of smaller Seders for people to feel more comfortable learning about [Passover],” she said. Egol appreciated the opportunity to share Jewish traditions with a diverse group, noting that a Seder includes explanations of the rituals and history behind the holiday, which naturally makes it more accessible to newcomers. The holiday of Passover began April 1 at sundown and ended April 9 at sundown. Chabad at Duke hosted students for all eight days and announced that 500 people participated in its Seders at the Fleishman House. Students were able to find kosher-for-Passover food at the Freeman Center for Jewish Life for breakfast, lunch and dinner. Other campus locations also offered kosher-for-Passover meals, including at Trinity Cafe, Devil’s Krafthouse, Gothic Grill and Beyu Blue.

title: Here’s how to participate in SHAPE Week on campus
subtitle: Through a series of events and support drives, SHAPE week aims to honor survivors, raise awareness about sexual violence and promote support and resources that are available on campus.
author: Max Tendler
text: Duke Sexual Harassment & Assault Prevention & Education is holding its annual SHAPE Week from Monday through Thursday as part of visibility efforts during Sexual Assault Awareness Month, which takes place every April. Through a series of events and support drives, the week aims to honor survivors, raise awareness about sexual violence and promote support and resources that are available on campus. Sophomore Ava Claar, director of outreach for SHAPE, wrote that the week’s events aim to demonstrate the need for “ongoing education, advocacy and collective effort to make Duke’s campus a safer and more supportive place, especially for having difficult but necessary conversations.” In spring 2020, Jacob Jeffries and Eden Schumer, both Trinity ‘22 and former Duke Student Government senators, helped organize the first SHAPE Week in collaboration with over 20 student groups. Notably, it featured a speech by Nadia Murad, recipient of the 2018 Nobel Peace Prize. Since then, Claar said that SHAPE Week has worked to continue “creating spaces for important conversations.” Take Back the Night Walk, the concluding and most visible event of SHAPE Week, began in 2022 and has been held annually since then. At the walk, students march across campus, standing in solidarity with survivors of sexual violence. Here’s how you can participate in the march and other events happening this year. SHAPE events this week From 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. Monday on Bryan Center Plaza, SHAPE is partnering with the Durham Crisis Response Center to discuss the DCRC’s domestic violence shelters and volunteer opportunities. They will also run a “necessities drive,” collecting household and hygiene items for victims. On Tuesday and Wednesday, SHAPE will continue its DCRC donation drive while hosting a poster-making session from 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. on B.C. Plaza to prepare for the annual march. Markers and poster boards will be available on B.C. Plaza for those interested in making signs. SHAPE Week culminates in a pair of events Thursday, beginning with a guest lecture on sexual assault adjudication and ending with the Take Back the Night March. The conversation will take place at 5:30 p.m. in Sanford 004 and feature guest speaker Tamara Lave, Title IX expert and professor of law at the University of Miami, who will discuss how colleges address sexual assault cases on their campuses. Shortly thereafter, at 6:45 p.m., students will march from the Sanford building to East Campus to honor previous sexual assault prevention advocates, support survivors and increase visibility on campus.

title: Roy Cooper champions himself as affordability candidate at Durham rally
subtitle: Cooper, the Democratic nominee for U.S. Senate and former governor, spoke to a cheering crowd on Thursday alongside other Democratic elected officials, calling to “make stuff cost less” and criticizing his opponent as a Washington insider.
author: Max Tendler
text: Former North Carolina Gov. Roy Cooper headlined a rally in Durham on Thursday, promoting his run for U.S. Senate alongside other Democratic elected officials. Cooper, who is running for the Senate seat being vacated by retiring Republican Sen. Thom Tillis, spoke after two hours of speeches all emphasizing the same three ideas: Cooper is for affordability, health care and combating corruption. “North Carolina can't afford any more excuses. We need to make stuff cost less,” Cooper said, alluding to his campaign’s core mantra. Cooper emphasized his disdain for the “chaos” in Washington, and positioned himself as a man of the people, in contrast to the politicians who he believes are letting “working people [be] left behind.” “Going to Washington, D.C. just wasn’t in my plans,” Cooper said, highlighting his long career of public service in North Carolina. “... [But] I couldn’t watch the people of my state be treated this way. Not without a fight.” Cooper announced his run for Senate in July, and resoundingly won the Democratic primary last month after facing minimal opposition. He is a familiar face across North Carolina, having held statewide office for nearly 25 years. Cooper served as state attorney general for four terms from 2001 until 2017, and then as governor until 2025. Before that, Cooper began his journey in elected office in 1987, serving in the N.C. House of Representatives until 1991. Afterward, he was elevated to the N.C. Senate, where he remained for 10 years, becoming majority leader in 1997. During his governorship, Cooper notably worked with the N.C. General Assembly to expand Medicaid which has been credited with providing health insurance to around 720,000 North Carolinians. However, in general, his relationship with the Republican-controlled state legislature was contentious, as he issued over 100 vetoes across his tenure, a record number. He also oversaw the state’s response to numerous natural disasters, including Hurricane Helene in 2024. Throughout the rally, Cooper portrayed himself as an everyman, while framing Whatley as an opportunistic former oil lobbyist who is “out of touch” with North Carolinians. Whatley did serve as a partner at lobbying firm HBW Resources from 2007 to 2022, where he worked on issues related to oil and gas development. From 2008 to 2019, he was also executive vice president for the Consumer Energy Alliance, an advocacy group backed by multiple fossil fuel companies according to Politico . In government, Whatley has a shorter history, serving in the George W. Bush administration as principal deputy assistant secretary for the U.S. Department of Energy from 2001 to 2003 and then as chief of staff to former Republican Sen. Elizabeth Dole, Woman’s College ‘58. Whatley's most prominent role came when he was elected chairman of the Republican National Committee in March 2024, after having served as chairman of the N.C. Republican Party since 2019. He remained in the role until Aug. 2025, when he resigned following his announcement to run for U.S. Senate. Cooper’s speech touched on many of the winning issues in the Democratic Party’s current playbook. He brought Durham residents on stage to talk about how they were struggling to make ends meet, lamenting the high cost of groceries and health care. “And now, with the war in Iran, here comes higher gas prices,” Cooper said. Rallying behind Cooper were several Democratic elected officials, including state Sen. Natalie Murdock and U.S. Rep. Valerie Foushee, both of whom represent Durham. “Roy Cooper has been a trusted partner in the fight for equity and opportunity for all communities,” Foushee said. “... Roy Cooper is running for us. Michael Whatley is running just to help himself and his fellow D.C. insiders and lobbyists.” Also among the lineup was Anita Earls, N.C Supreme Court associate justice, who is running for re-election this November to maintain a Democratic seat on the Republican-dominated court. Cooper endorsed her specifically, stating that her “re-election matters for true justice.” Democratic Gov. Josh Stein vouched for Cooper’s character, describing his experience working under him as senior deputy attorney general. He’s a “good man and a good friend,” Stein said, emphasizing Cooper’s work going after predatory payday lenders and prosecuting criminals. In the four public polls taken since the primary, Cooper has held the lead, ranging from a margin of 3 to 14 percentage points, according to the New York Times.

title: Assemble the Georges: Duke George Society seeks to reshape campus
subtitle: A new society on campus has only one membership rule: you must be named George. The group hopes to reach a day where everyone is named George.
author: Jackson Kitts
text: A new society has popped up on campus, with a peculiar membership rule. To join, you must be named George. The Duke George Society was founded in February when senior George Nathaniel, and juniors George Donnelly and George Fenimore began hanging up flyers to assemble all the Georges across campus. Chatter spread among Duke’s student body and Fizz upvotes spiked. All of it traces back to the society. “The state of the world right now is probably related to the lack of George that we've seen,” Nathaniel said. “You can look at a graph, you see problems go up, George goes down ... We’re trying to change the world by bringing us together.” What actually occurs behind closed doors at the society’s meetings remains unclear. Members declined to share details. Yet, the society’s leadership insisted that their presence will soon be felt across campus and beyond in a larger way. Their Instagram account is public and has posted content highlighting members’ spring break trips and meetups on campus. “We exist in the public. People know that there is a George society, but what we're up to, don't worry,” Nathaniel said. “We'll make ourselves known to you in due time.” The society hopes that going forward, they will continue to bring together more Georges and reach a day where everyone is named George. “We aim to create a sort of legacy of George,” Fenimore said. “It started with us, but boy, it's not going to end with us.”

title: Duke admissions outlines post-affirmative action strategy, AI limits and transfer push
subtitle: Members of Duke's admissions team discussed the their approach to diversity metrics and artificial intelligence use, as well as considering students’ academic pursuits and transfer status in their enrollment targets.
author: Ella Moore
text: Duke’s Office of Undergraduate Admissions, along with the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences transfer team, outlined a slew of admissions priorities in a presentation to Arts & Sciences Council Thursday, just nine days after the office admitted its Class of 2030. Kathy Phillips, interim dean of undergraduate admissions, told council attendees that the office looks to “support the faculty,” and keep the “goal of the University in mind” through the students they admit. She discussed the admissions team’s approach to diversity metrics and artificial intelligence use, as well as considering students’ academic pursuits and transfer status in their enrollment targets. Phillips summarized the many changes her team has had to contend with in the past two years, including navigating the admissions landscape following the 2023 Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard Supreme Court decision, which deemed the consideration of race and ethnicity in college admissions unconstitutional. Phillips said Duke unequivocally does not consider race or ethnicity — nor “proxies” to these characteristics — in its admissions decisions. Other demographic identities have significance for University priorities, though. She pointed to continued partnerships with QuestBridge, which matches low-income students with limited financial support to elite higher education institutions, and the STARS College Network , which supports small-town and rural students, as key examples of Duke’s work to ensure socioeconomic and geographical diversity in its student body. While the University has been “geographically consistent” in the last few admissions cycles, admitting more students from the Carolinas also remains a priority, according to Phillips. Duke has substantially increased its investment in undergraduates from North and South Carolina in recent years, specifically through its Initiative for Students from the Carolinas . Currently, the University provides full-tuition grants for admitted students from the region with family incomes of $150,000 or less. Phillips said she did not expect the “impending demographic cliff,” an expected decline in high school graduates that has become a point of concern for many colleges and universities, to be a challenge for Duke. She predicted Duke would remain a desirable and competitive choice for future applicants. Duke has seen steady increases in applicants over the last few admissions cycles, including for the Class of 2030, which saw a record-high 55,776 applications in the Regular Decision round. On legacy applicants, Phillips said the admissions office works with the Office of Alumni Engagement and Development to identify priority applicants — more specifically, children of Duke alumni who remain involved in the University through financial contributions or other investments. She emphasized these applicants make up a small number of spots, estimating they fill about 100 spaces — roughly 6% of the first-year class. Phillips described the recent classes of admitted students as steadily more credentialed and outcome-focused but less socially adept. She posited that these characteristics have arisen for a variety of reasons, including that many current students spent some of their time in high school amid a global pandemic and their unique identity as the “technology generation.” On the outcome-focused point, David Malone, professor of the practice of education, observed that students are now coming into his “The University: Why Are We Here?” FOCUS course with a set idea of roughly six professions that will provide a “return on investment” of attending university, which mostly revolved around STEM- and economics-based fields. Phillips explained in response that many students, especially those with low-income, first-generation or international backgrounds, may consider studies in the arts and humanities as “luxury goods” that they or their family may not want to prioritize ahead of a financially safer career path. She described the emphasis on more technical fields as a problem commonly faced by research universities, rather than an issue unique to Duke. While economics and STEM degrees consistently rank among Duke’s most popular majors, Phillips said admitting students with a genuine, demonstrated interest in arts and the humanities remains a priority. She also noted that students with interests in climate and sustainability, as well as civic and environmental engineering, have become a point of focus for the admissions office. Phillips said Duke does not use artificial intelligence for any step of the admissions process outside of organizing applications. She has previously expressed the University’s continued commitment to human review in admissions but qualified that the admissions office is not entirely opposed to its use in the future. “Most universities — Duke included — are eager to implement AI to assist with the objective parts of application evaluation, like reading transcripts to note course selection and grades,” Phillips wrote in a Jan. 14 email to The Chronicle. “The resources for doing so are just now being introduced to the marketplace.” During Thursday’s council meeting, she mentioned she would love to see a tool that helps the office standardize transcripts, as most high schools do not have unified standards for how they calculate grade point averages and implement class rank. Phillips added that while other institutions, such as the nearby University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, use artificial intelligence to assess students’ writing style and grammar proficiency through their admissions essays, Duke rarely sees applicants who do not submit competent essays and thus currently does not feel the need to use AI to analyze or rate essays. Duke stopped assigning numerical scores to the essays submitted by applicants in 2024. In the most recent admissions cycle, Duke revised one of its optional supplemental essay prompts to ask applicants to reflect on their own use of AI, inviting them to describe a situation in which they would or would not choose to use the technology and explain why. Attracting transfers has become an increased priority for Duke, an initiative that Claire Siburt, academic dean for Trinity transfer students, described as a “whole University effort.” Jenny Wood Crowley, associate vice provost for strategic enrollment management, said that transfers provide an additional population for Duke to draw from who can live on West Campus, whose capacity outstrips East Campus by a factor of four, she said. She also cited the mass exodus of juniors who study abroad each fall semester as empty seats that the University can fill with transfers. Duke has made changes to how it operates transfer admissions in recent years. Last admissions cycle, Duke transitioned from Common App to a personalized transfer application, which led to a brief drop in applicants, according to Anne Sjostrom, senior associate dean of undergraduate admissions. Before the COVID-19 pandemic, Duke admitted roughly 20 to 30 students per transfer cycle. The number rose to 90 last cycle, and Phillips said the University hopes to enroll another 90 in the current one. According to Siburt, around 28% of transfers are athletes, while the rest come from a variety of institutions across the country. These students typically have less financial need and pursue a wide swath of academic interests, though Duke targeted students interested in the arts and humanities in the most recent admissions cycle.

title: Duke halts Black Student Alliance Invitational, students warn of impact on enrollment
subtitle: Under Student Affairs, Duke's Identity and Cultural Center teams and student organizations will instead run a new "Blue Devil Nights" that is open to all admitted students.
author: Ella Moore
text: For the first time since the 1980s , Duke’s Office of Undergraduate Admissions will not host the Black Student Alliance Invitational. Current students say the decision was made without their input and worry it could hurt Black enrollment. BSAI introduced prospective first-years to Duke from a Black perspective. Students spent two nights in the East and West Campus dorms, where they were paired with upperclassmen mentors, attended networking events and watched student cultural performances, including an annual stroll show , organized by the historically Black Divine Nine organizations . Under Student Affairs, Duke's Identity and Cultural Center teams and student organizations will instead run a new "Blue Devil Nights" on April 16 and 17 open to all admitted students. Unlike BSAI, “Blue Devil Nights” has no overnight component and no programming specific to Black students. Jenny Wood Crowley, associate vice provost for strategic enrollment management, told The Chronicle in a Thursday email that the decision to pause BSAI was made after a regular review of all University programming to “ensure they align with evolving legal guidance and institutional priorities.” The pause comes at a time of low Black student enrollment. Just 7% of the incoming Class of 2029 identified as Black or African American, according to Duke’s class profile . Current Black students fear that the number will fall further without BSAI. For sophomore Clark Easley, BSAI was “one of the best weeks” he had at Duke. The invitational eased some of the trepidation he felt about attending a university with an “elitist” reputation like Duke and made it feel more like a home than a school. Many of his core friends went through the program as well. “I cannot undercount the impact BSAI has for recruiting and retaining Black students,” Easley said. Nigel Cooper, a first-year student, said BSAI was instrumental in his decision to choose Duke over other elite institutions he was accepted into, including the University of Pennsylvania and Stanford. The upperclassmen support system built through the multi-day BSAI experience was a key perk of attendance. “I only committed to Duke because of BSAI,” Cooper said. Wood Crowley told The Chronicle in her email that Student Affairs “partnered closely” with students to shape this year’s approach to Blue Devil Nights and the broader Blue Devil Days experience. However, student leaders have said that communication on changes to admitted students' events has been limited. Other students expressed widespread frustration with the lack of communication on BSAI’s fate for the recent admission cycle. The Black Student Alliance, the principal organization overseeing BSAI, did not have firsthand information from Duke administrators that the program would not take place this year. “We haven’t had the opportunity to give direct input into the changes that the administration has made,” junior Demetrist Booker said. Students familiar with administrative communications on BSAI, who asked not to be named after being told not to comment publicly, said that until around January, Duke seemed poised to run the invitational as normal. Applications were sent out for BSAI organizers, and students were appointed to the positions. But around late February and early March, students preparing to plan the April event were told BSAI would be changed and their positions as BSAI co-chairs would be renamed to “Impact Ambassadors.” Some students have speculated that the shift may have been in reaction to the Trump administration’s “Compact for Academic Excellence in Higher Education,” which included multiple tenets on preventing discrimination in all university operations, including admissions. The change is also reflective of Duke’s reaction to a broader push from the federal government for higher education institutions to roll back DEI programming. Wood Crowley touted Blue Devil Days, along with the new Blue Devil Nights, as experiences that together “reflect a more comprehensive and student-informed approach to community-building.” Despite these efforts, Booker worries that removing the overnight component and merging programming with Blue Devil Days won’t add the same depth of experience for students. “BSAI has been a mechanism for the last 50 years to encourage not just Black students, but all students ... by not having, or by significantly changing the programming, students are less inclined to come to the school,” Booker said.

title: Who is John Sauer? The Duke alum arguing for ending birthright citizenship at Supreme Court
subtitle: Sauer, Pratt ‘97, serves as U.S. solicitor general, a job that brought him to the Supreme Court last week to argue President Donald Trump's case against birthright citizenship.
author: Lila Cohen, Seyun Park
text: A senior government official and Duke alumnus is leading the president’s charge against birthright citizenship — and it’s not Stephen Miller. Dean John Sauer, Pratt ‘97, serves as the solicitor general of the United States. Last week, Sauer’s job brought him to the Supreme Court, where he argued on behalf of President Donald Trump concerning his executive order attempting to end complete birthright citizenship. “Unrestricted birthright citizenship contradicts the practice of the overwhelming majority of modern nations,” Sauer said at the court’s oral arguments for Trump v. Barbara. “It demeans the priceless and profound gift of American citizenship.” One particularly memorable interaction between Sauer and Chief Justice John Roberts went viral online, with Sauer arguing that the U.S. is in a “new world” when it comes to immigration and claiming that “8 billion people are one plane ride away from having a child who's a U.S. citizen.” “It’s a new world. It’s the same Constitution,” Roberts responded. Legal experts generally expect the nine-member court to rule against the president, with conservative justices signaling skepticism through their questions in oral arguments. North Carolina was one of 22 states to sue the president soon after the order was issued in January 2025, and Duke scholars have expressed skepticism of the policy’s legal standing and concern over its impact on American identity, should the ban be upheld. A ruling on the case will likely come toward the end of the court's term in June. The Chronicle took a look at Sauer’s Duke history and his connections to Trump over the years. Sauer did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment through the Department of Justice’s press office. At Duke, Sauer was an accomplished scholar and a student-athlete. He earned degrees in both philosophy and electrical engineering, was inducted into the Phi Beta Kappa academic honors society and graduated summa cum laude. He was also an Angier B. Duke scholar, Duke’s oldest merit scholarship that covers the University’s full cost of attendance and a summer at Oxford University. Sauer was involved with multiple campus organizations at Duke, including the Catholic Student Center and Duke Students for Life. He was also a walk-on member of the wrestling team, eventually serving as team captain. Outside of Duke, he spent summers in his hometown of St. Louis, where he worked with prisoners and taught inner-city children. He appeared in a February 1994 edition of The Chronicle, co-authoring a letter to the editor alongside 17 other members of Duke Students for Life in objection to an editorial that praised then-President Bill Clinton’s health plan, which included funding for abortions. Sauer wrote that given abortion’s alleged unpopularity in the country — he cited a New York Times poll — it would be “tragic and immoral” to force citizens to pay for it. In his senior year, Sauer was named a Rhodes Scholar, becoming the third student-athlete from Duke to earn the prestigious award. His adviser, Ed Mahoney, then-director of undergraduate studies in the philosophy department, praised Sauer at the time for his modesty. “I'm definitely no wiser or more virtuous than anyone else," Sauer said in a 1996 Chronicle article on the achievement. "I'm just a regular guy.” After graduating, Sauer took the aforementioned Rhodes Scholarship to pursue a second bachelor’s degree in theology at Oxford. He later earned a master’s degree in philosophy from the University of Notre Dame and a Juris Doctor from Harvard Law School, where he served as articles editor of the Harvard Law Review and graduated magna cum laude. Sauer went on to clerk for Judge J. Michael Luttig in the Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals, then for Justice Antonin Scalia in the Supreme Court. Both judges were noted conservatives during their terms. Following his clerkships, Sauer served as an assistant U.S. attorney before transitioning to private practice, founding his own law firm in 2015. Between 2017 and 2023, he served as the solicitor general in his home state of Missouri. Sauer is no stranger to the Supreme Court — or arguing for Trump. The solicitor general is the fourth-highest ranking member of the Department of Justice behind the attorney general, deputy attorney general and associate attorney general. The position’s main responsibility is to represent the government in cases before the Supreme Court. So far in Trump’s second term, Sauer has argued in seven of the administration’s biggest cases, ranging from the president’s tariffs, challenged in Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump , to his attempt to fire Federal Reserve chair Lisa Cook, challenged in Trump v. Cook . But Sauer was arguing for Trump in the nation’s highest court even before he was on government payroll. In 2024, when he was a private practicing attorney, Sauer represented Trump in the case Trump v. United States, which revolved around whether the Department of Justice could prosecute Trump for the Jan. 6 insurrection and attempts to overturn the 2020 election. Sauer eventually won 6-3, securing a surprising and controversial verdict from the court, which granted wide latitudes of immunity from criminal prosecution to presidents for actions conducted in office. In 2020, when he was serving as solicitor general of Missouri, Sauer filed a “friend of the court” amicus brief with the Supreme Court, supporting Texas’ effort to overturn the results of the 2020 presidential election. The case was eventually dismissed by the court due to the state’s lack of standing to sue. Over the years, Sauer’s advocacy on Trump’s behalf has made him a personal favorite of the president. Trump praised Sauer, calling him a “deeply accomplished, masterful appellate attorney” in a statement announcing his nomination. Sauer went on to be approved 52-45 in the Senate. Sauer’s loyalty to the president may eventually pay off in a big way for the Blue Devil: Sauer’s name is reportedly in the mix for a coveted Supreme Court spot, should one open up. “He is certainly at the top of my short list for the Supreme Court,” Mike Davis, another one of Trump’s lawyers, recently told CNN.

title: Kaitlyn Williams, Belton Moore named Young Trustee nominees
subtitle: The two Young Trustees will start their terms on July 1 once confirmed by the board.
author: Ella Moore
text: Trinity senior Kaitlyn Williams and Belton Moore, a master’s in business administration and public policy candidate, were selected by the Young Trustee nominating committees and President Vincent Price as Duke’s next undergraduate and graduate/professional Young Trustees, respectively. The nominations will be brought to the Board of Trustees for election on May 9, wrote Margaret Epps, secretary to the Board of Trustees and Price’s chief of staff, in a Wednesday email to The Chronicle. The two Young Trustees will start their terms on July 1 once confirmed by the board. Young Trustees are students who serve two- or three-year terms as one of the 37 members of the Board of Trustees to provide the perspective of “today’s Duke.” Though they spend their first year as non-voting observers, Young Trustees are tasked with ensuring the “long-term health” of the University, rather than advocating for student issues. Williams, originally from Virginia, is a SPIRE fellow pursuing a Program II in inequality in education. She has been heavily involved on campus throughout her time at Duke and described receiving the nomination as a “dream.” Williams currently serves as President of the Duke University Union, the University’s largest student-led programming and media organization with 18 committees. Before assuming the presidency, she helped establish (DUU)rham , which is aimed at fostering relationships “between Duke students and Durham’s vendors, landmarks and rich history.” Williams is also president of Duke SPIRE Fellows , a program focused on “enhancing student success, retention, and engagement in STEM disciplines,” and works as a Green Devils Inter-Institutional Student Sustainability Coordinator, connecting Duke student sustainability leaders with other colleges and universities. “I applied to be a Young Trustee because I believe in the power of higher education and want to help shape its future, starting here at my alma mater,” Williams wrote in a Wednesday email to The Chronicle. “Throughout my time at Duke, I’ve had the privilege of interacting with so many aspects of the university, and I view my time as a Young Trustee as an opportunity to learn about the place I’ve called home for the last four years.” Moore, a native of North Carolina, told The Chronicle that Duke has been “transformative in his life.” As he searched for a way to give back, Young Trustees seemed like a “great opportunity.” Besides pursuing dual degrees in public policy and business as a Rubenstein Fellow, Moore works as an economics tutor at the Sanford School and a research assistant in the Fuqua School of Business. As an undergraduate at North Carolina State University, Moore served in the university’s student government as a senator and vice president of the Native American Student Association. “My parents instilled service as a value, specifically the value of institutions of higher education,” Moore said. Moore’s connection to Duke comes from unique historical ties. His father worked as a tobacco farmer for much of his life, during the two decades when the Duke family oversaw the dominant American Tobacco Company. He is particularly interested in the University’s engagement with North Carolina, especially with “exciting” new programs like the recently announced HomeGrown Initiative. “[Duke’s] investing more into the Triangle, so I’m really excited to plug in there and learn more and see how I can help,” Moore said. This year’s selection of Young Trustees differed from past rounds. Traditionally, finalists faced the student body in a public forum hosted by Board of Trustees Secretary Margaret Epps’ office, where they outlined their goals and qualifications directly to the student body. For 2026, the forum was replaced by private interviews with Epps, a current Young Trustee and the chair of the Board of Trustees' governance committee. Epps expressed confidence in the new process and praised the Young Trustees as “assets to the board,” through their perspectives reflecting the student body.

title: Meet the 2026 Commencement honorary degree recipients
subtitle: Dianne McIntyre, Cass Sunstein and Abby Wambach will each receive honorary degrees in recognition of their trailblazing careers on May 10.
author: Annie Eilers, Sarah Diaz
text: Duke University will honor a pioneering choreographer, a leading legal scholar and a world champion soccer player at its Class of 2026 Commencement ceremony on May 10. Dianne McIntyre, Cass Sunstein and Abby Wambach will each receive honorary degrees in recognition of their trailblazing careers. Dianne McIntyre is the founder of Sounds in Motion, a Harlem-based dance studio and touring company. Her ensemble of dancers and musicians have performed across the United States and Europe for over 20 years, including at major venues like The Joyce Theater, The Brooklyn Academy of Music and The Kennedy Center. Beyond her dance company, McIntyre has mentored and inspired generations of dancers for the past 50 years. Since 1972 , she has choreographed four Broadway shows, 30 regional theatre productions, two feature films and three television productions, among other dramatic pieces. Her work has been commissioned by notable institutions such as the Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater and the American Dance Festival. McIntyre was awarded with the Doris Duke Impact Award in 2016 and was welcomed by the Duke Dance Program for a development residency in February 2022. She has also received additional accolades such as three Bessie awards, a John S. Guggenheim Fellowship and a Kennedy Center Master of African American Choreography Medal. Cass Sunstein is the founder of the program on behavioral economics and public policy at Harvard Law School and a recipient of the prestigious Holberg Prize. He is a leading legal scholar with work spanning behavioral economics, environmental law and political administration. Sunstein currently serves as the Robert Walmsley University Professor at Harvard. This semester he is teaching courses on the intersection of behavioral economics, law and public policy as well as the politics of social change. His current projects focus on the regulatory state, administrative burdens, fake news and freedom of speech. Sunstein has authored New York Times bestselling books “Nudge: Improving Decisions about Health, Wealth, and Happiness” and “The World According to Star Wars.” He has also written hundreds of articles for law reviews and journals across the country. Sunstein worked under the Obama and Biden presidential administrations. From 2009 to 2012, Sunstein served as the administrator of the White House Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs, before serving on the President’s Review Board on Intelligence and Communications Technologies and on the Pentagon’s Defense Innovation Board. In 2024, Sunstein won the Distinguished Public Service medal for his work as the Senior Counselor to the Secretary of Homeland Security under the Biden administration. Sunstein previously served as Chair of the Technical Advisory Group on Behavioural Sciences for Better Health with the World Health Organization. He has also advised officials at the United Nations, European Commission and World Bank and served as an adviser to the Behavioural Insights Team. Abby Wambach is an internationally recognized soccer player, coach and World Cup champion. She made four career appearances in the World Cup, during a period when USA women's soccer began growing in popularity. Her veteran leadership helped Team USA win its third title in 2015, the first since 1999. Wambach and her team came in third place in 2003 and 2007, and were the runners ups in 2011. She also played for the U.S. women’s national soccer team in the Athens 2004 and London 2012 Summer Olympics, racking up gold medals in both. With 184 career goals, Wambach is the leading scorer of all time on the U.S. women’s team. She was inducted into the National Soccer Hall of Fame in 2019. Now retired from soccer, Wambach is an advocate for women’s rights and equality. During the 60th session of the UN Commission of the Status of Women, Wambach spoke against soccer’s gender pay gap. She shared that after winning the 2015 FIFA Women’s World Cup, her team received “17 to 18 times less” compensation than the men’s champions from the year prior. After Wambach — alongside with other players — spoke out for years about the need for wage equality, the U.S. women’s national soccer team reached a $24 million settlement in an equal pay lawsuit in 2022. In 2019, Wambach released her New York Times Best Selling novel “WOLFPACK: How to Come Together, Unleash Our Power, and Change the Game.” She is also the co-founder and partial owner of Angel City FC, the first-ever majority female owned soccer club.

title: Academic Council discusses Diversity Task Force following community forum
subtitle: Nearly a week after students raised concerns about inclusion at a community forum, the Academic Council discussed initiatives to improve faculty diversity Thursday.
author: Vir Patel
text: Nearly a week after students raised concerns about inclusion at a community forum , the Academic Council discussed initiatives to improve faculty diversity Thursday. In the council's third meeting of the academic year, Emily Klein, professor of earth sciences at the Nicholas School of the Environment, provided updates on the Diversity Task Force of the Academic Council and the establishment of the Faculty Diversity Task Force Implementation Committee—two initiatives aimed at improving inclusion and representation among Duke faculty. As part of the new committee, Provost Sally Kornbluth has begun the search for a vice provost for faculty advancement. Faculty questioned different aspects of the committee's goals, expressing concerns about the many considerations that go into hiring faculty and integrating faculty at an institutional level. “Respect, safety, inclusion and community are not present for everyone as evidenced by the conversation this past Friday in Page Auditorium,” Kornbluth said. “It is so critical that we create a welcoming and open community at Duke.” Nan Jokerst, council chair and J. A. Jones distinguished professor of electrical and computer engineering, explained that the committee was established as a result of an assessment of faculty diversity and inclusion by the DTF last May. “[The assessment] concluded that we would continue to focus on underrepresented minorities and female faculty but also expand to include a focus on LGBTQ faculty,” Klein said. “We consider and support the idea that a diverse faculty also includes other attributes and are willing to extend into the future, looking at ideological and political diversity, religious background and ethnic origin.” Klein said that one goal of the newly formed committee is to establish the position of vice provost for faculty advancement, a position she explained would provide intellectual leadership, guidance and oversight of University programs to enhance faculty excellence and promote faculty inclusion and diversity. Joel Meyer, associate professor of environmental toxicology at the Nicholas School for the Environment, asked Klein how the committee would be able to gauge the effectiveness of new initiatives led by the DTF, and also suggested involving students to show the University’s commitment to diversity. “Part of the vice provost for faculty advancement’s role is to make sure that plans are actually being executed,” Kornbluth said. “Obviously, there are reasons why a [faculty hiring search] fails or doesn’t work, but easy answers are not going to be acceptable.” Joshua Sosin, associate professor of classical studies, stressed that the committee’s goals should remain broad rather than limiting its efforts to faculty hiring. “I speak for a lot of people when I say that we need to be sure that this is on our minds in every aspect of what we do,” he said. "In designing and implementing the curriculum, in recruiting undergraduate and graduate students, in creating a community that is safe and inclusive for the students, in recruiting faculty...this has to be central to our thinking and not something that emerges at the special moments such as hiring.” In other business: Jokerst announced a revision to the Duke Faculty Handbook that gives faculty members a greater say in the search and review processes for the hiring of upper administrative positions. Kornbluth and Edward Balleisen, vice provost for interdisciplinary studies, proposed a more clear process for converting a University-wide interdisciplinary center or initiative to an institute. Faculty responded with concerns about the blurring of lines between departments and institutes. The Council approved a new international master’s degree in environmental policy hosted at Duke Kunshan University.

title: Duke tops ACC in athlete graduation rates once again
subtitle: Statistics released last week by the National Collegiate Athletic Association show that 98 percent of Duke’s student-athletes graduate within six years of entering the University, an increase from 97 percent last year.
author: Adam Beyer
text: Statistics released last week by the National Collegiate Athletic Association show that 98 percent of Duke’s student-athletes graduate within six years of entering the University, an increase from 97 percent last year. Duke’s rate ranks as the highest Graduation Success Rate in the Atlantic Coast Conference. Nationally, the GSR increased two points to 86 percent. The rate represents the highest ever for the NCAA and included improvements across several gender and racial minority categories. “It’s difficult to say with certainty why the rate has increased, however, the Division I members have adopted a number of academic reforms in that time period,” said Michelle Hosick, associate director of public and media relations for the NCAA. The GSR modifies the student data colleges report to the federal government by excluding data from students who leave a school in good academic standing without graduating. It is calculated from the total cohort entering college between 2005 and 2008. Duke’s Federal Graduation Rate for the 2008-2009 cohort, also released last week, is 89 percent. Hosick explained the high graduation rate was driven by several factors, including “enhanced academic criteria for incoming freshmen, strengthened progress-toward-degree requirements and the Academic Progress Rate, a real-time metric that tracks team success every term and has penalties associated with it for teams who don’t meet benchmarks.” Of Duke’s 26 varsity sports , 20 reported a perfect GSR of 100 percent: baseball, men’s and women’s basketball, men’s and women’s cross country and track, crew and rowing, men’s and women’s fencing, men’s golf, field hockey, men’s and women’s lacrosse, women’s soccer, men’s and women’s tennis, women’s swimming and diving and volleyball. Six programs’ rates were less than 100 percent: football at 94 percent, men’s soccer at 89 percent, men’s swimming and diving at 97 percent, wrestling at 96 percent and women’s golf at 80 percent. The NCAA measures graduation success using the GSR instead of the FGR in order to not “penalize colleges whose student-athletes transfer in good academic standing,” according to their website. The FGR, which includes student-athletes who left school early, is 56 percent for Duke men’s basketball. The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hil l posted a GSR of 85 percent, down a point from last year, and North Carolina State University jumped two points to 83 percent. UNC men’s and women’s basketball reported an 80 percent and 85 percent GSR, respectively, and NC State men’s and women’s basketball teams reported 67 percent and 85 percent, respectively. Their football team reported a GSR of 70 percent, and UNC’s posted a 62 percent rate, the lowest in the ACC.

title: Yik Yak ban infeasible, admins say
subtitle: Although students have pushed to ban the app Yik Yak from campus, administrators have refused to take action against the forum.
author: Adam Beyer
text: Although students have pushed to ban the app Yik Yak from campus, administrators have refused to take action against the forum. A number of students have expressed concern that Yik Yak has become a conduit for anonymous offensive messages. During the first community forum Nov. 13 to address recent racist and homophobic events on campus, President Richard Brodhead said he did not know if he had the ability to shut down the location-based anonymous forum on campus in response to student requests that he do so. At least half a dozen universities have blocked the app from their campus wireless networks, including Augustana College in Illinois, Saint Louis University, Utica College and Norwich University. Larry Moneta, vice president for student affairs, ruled out blocking the app at Duke and noted that doing so would not be effective. “Since it can always be accessed via cell signal, no institution could truly eliminate it from their campus,” he wrote in an email. “For any institution to seek to silence it plays right into [Yik Yak’s] hands...since what they seek is notoriety.” After several complaints, the College of Idaho asked Yik Yak earlier this month to place a geofence around its campus so that the app would be inaccessible to its students. The app declined the request, according to The Washington Post . The app prevents users who are under 18 from posting by placing geofences around middle and high school campuses but does not geofence college campuses, explained Olivia Borger, Trinity ‘13 and a communications associate for the company. She noted that the app is working to enhance protective measures. “Encouraging a positive, constructive, and supportive community environment on Yik Yak is a top area of focus for us!” she wrote in an email. “Today, we have a number of safeguards in place like filters, pop-up warnings, in-app reporting, moderation, and suspensions.” Yik Yak does cooperate with law enforcement agencies. When issued a warrant, court order, subpoena or in an emergency situation, the app can disclose the user’s IP address, GPS coordinates from where the message was posted, the time and date of the post and other data including phone numbers, according to the Yik Yak website. Moneta noted that the University only contacts Yik Yak when there is a legal reason to do so. Yik Yak is not the first form of anonymous communication to appear on college campuses. Matt Ivester, Trinity ‘05, launched JuicyCampus.com in 2007 as an online gossip forum with pages for colleges across the country. It quickly generated controversy and legal inquiries—including a letter from the Attorney General of Connecticut—for posts targeting individual students. Ivester shut down the website in 2009 due to the effect of “historically difficult economic times” on its advertising revenues, and wrote that if not for the lost revenue the website would be “thriving,” according to an article in The Chronicle from February 2009. Duke administrators did not move to restrict the popular forum, but were nonetheless happy to see it shut down. “Whatever short-term pains it has caused can be replaced by the next foolish look-alike,” Moneta said at the time. “In the long run, it’s meaningless—for each one that comes, one goes. But hopefully it’ll diminish the next.” Subsequently, two graduate students at Johns Hopkins University and Wesleyan University started CollegiateACB, promising a safer website with features that would ensure internet searches could not find posts discussing individual students. According to an article in The Chronicle from March 3, 2014, Duke was the second-most active forum on the website, with more than 900 threads and 7,000 comments. The University’s forum experienced a surge in posts after The Chronicle profiled a student adult film actress, in 2014. Administrators expressed disgust at the content of CollegiateACB, but struggled to find any possible recourse. In 2011, the Panhellenic Association petitioned to have Duke removed from the site, which featured many posts attempting to “rank” sororities and fraternities. “It has affected our chapters on a collective level,” then-Panhel President Jenny Ngo, Trinity ‘12, said in an April 2011 interview with The Chronicle . “It fuels a social hierarchy that leads to competitiveness between our sororities.” Duke Student Government tried addressing the problem in 2010 with an online forum called Blue Devils District that required a NetID to log in and was not anonymous. CollegiateACB’s founder Tim O’Shea sold the site in early 2014 due to the negative media attention and to pursue other ventures. The site can no longer be accessed. Sue Wasiolek, associate vice president for student affairs and dean of students, encouraged students affected by targeted posts on Yik Yak to seek help. “Many individuals, including RAs, RCs, and colleagues in DukeReach and CAPS and across Student Affairs, stand prepared to support students who find themselves troubled by posts they read on Yik Yak or on any other social media,” she wrote in an email.

title: Academic Council examines master's degree programs in Thursday meeting
subtitle: During its Thursday meeting, Academic Council considered changes to its master's degree program approval process put forth by the Academic Programs Committee.
author: Bre Bradham
text: During its Thursday meeting, Academic Council considered changes to its master's degree program approval process put forth by the Academic Programs Committee. The proposed resolution outlined metrics by which master’s programs can be approved and reviewed. The suggested changes follow a 51 percent growth in the total size of Duke's master's degree programs since 2004, noted Emily Bernhardt, chair of the committee and a professor of biology. The council also discussed its rules regarding faculty governance in the wake of last month’s Duke Kunshan University vote, before going into a private executive session to be briefed on cyber security updates for the University. “These are complicated issues, but I feel that we have made pretty good progress on them,” said Edward Balleisen, vice provost for interdisciplinary studies, on the proposal regarding master’s degrees. The master's program resolution noted that the Academic Council has raised questions in the past about the framework through which new proposals are evaluated, the process for evaluating the “quality and impact” of programs following their initial pilot phase and the impact of new degree programs on existing ones. The resolution also focused on the “hidden cost” of programs to University resources and related departments. Some of the proposal's recommendations pushed for degree program proposals to include specific metrics by which to evaluate their success—such as increasing diversity among faculty and students, growth of faculty and research activities and the financial health of the program and its department. “We want to see that there is a compelling vision at the beginning, and we want to see that people stay engaged and are enthusiastic about something as it proceeds,” Bernhardt said. “These are the sorts of things that suggest that it is worth peoples’ time to be engaged. These intangibles are a big part of our conversation when we review.” Jane Richardson, James B. Duke professor of medicine and biochemistry, raised a question about the possibility of keeping up with students after they graduate in order to more fully evaluate the success of the program. Included in the resolution is a recommendation that the degree programs should include information about the employment rates of graduates on their websites for the benefit of prospective students. Nan Jokerst, J. A. Jones distinguished professor of electrical and computer engineering and chair of the Academic Council, informed faculty that discussions would continue in the Spring. Members of the council also debated the future of faculty governance in light of the successful development and approval of an undergraduate degree program at Duke Kunshan University last month. Joshua Sosin, an associate professor of classical studies and member of the executive committee of Academic Council, facilitated the discussion, which ranged from mentorship of less-experienced council members to the representation of non-tenure track faculty on committees and governance bodies. “It is critically important that all of these venues of initiating communication, discussions and proposals remain active and engaged,” Jokerst said. “That is the point of our conversation today—how to be proactive as a council in identifying and pursuing our priorities, as well as partnering with the administration, all in the spirit of rowing in the same direction to move Duke forward.”

title: Sophomore Jack Donahue alleges medical leave of absence policy is discriminatory after being denied readmittance
subtitle: Sophomore petitions suspension under ‘expected’ two-semester medical leave policyWhen sophomore Jack Donahue voluntarily sought a leave of absence from Duke to seek treatment for depression and anxiety this October, he did not anticipate the difficulty he would face in securing readmission.Donahue’s appeal to be readmitted for Spring 2017 was denied this November under the Duke Office of Student Returns’ recently amended Medical Leave of Absence (MLOA) policy, which now states that the ‘expected leave time is two full terms’. Donahue is petitioning the university for a reconsideration of this outcome, describing the policy as ‘prejudicial, against the Americans with Disabilities Act, and cruel’. The petition, posted Friday night, has since garnered close to 1,200 signatures.Donahue violated Duke’s MLOA policy on campus visitation when he returned to Central Campus on his birthday weekend in November to see his ‘friends and support system’. During this incident, he requested to be EMS-ed from Central Campus after consuming several alcoholic drinks, which he believes interacted with his new medication.
author: Joyce Er
text: When sophomore Jack Donahue took a leave of absence to seek treatment for depression and anxiety in October, he did not anticipate the difficulty he would face returning to Duke. But Donahue claims his appeal to be readmitted for Spring 2017 semester was denied in November because of the Office of Student Returns’ medical leave of absence policy, which states that the "expected leave time is two full terms." Donahue is now petitioning the University to reconsider, describing the policy as "prejudicial, against the Americans with Disabilities Act and cruel." The petition, created Friday night, currently has more than 1,200 signatures. In November 2015 , Donahue was the target of a death threat when a homophobic slur was written on the first-floor corridor of East Residence Hall. He wrote in the petition that the threat contributed to his mental health issues. However, he noted that now he wants to return because he is "ready and [has] been cleared to return by all of my health professionals." “I discussed my sincere and ardent desire to only be on leave for Fall 2016 before I even signed,” wrote Donahue in an email to The Chronicle. “I was first requested to submit an additional recommendation and then was told I wasn’t up for consideration for a Spring 2017 return after my trip to Durham.” Donahue admitted in the petition that he violated Duke’s MLOA policy on campus visitation—which says you cannot be on campus during the leave of absence—when he returned to Central Campus for his birthday weekend in November to see his friends. During this incident, he requested to be EMS-ed from Central Campus after consuming several alcoholic drinks he believes interacted adversely with his new medication. Donahue claimed that the Office of Student Returns is using this incident to reject his application to return a semester early. On its website, the Office of Student Returns says that students are supposed to remain on leave for two semesters to ensure adequate treatment of health issues. "Students placed on a medical leave of absence are expected to remain on leave for two semesters beyond the semester in which the leave was granted," the OSR website reads. "This ensures that there is adequate time for transition, diagnosis, treatment, and stabilization so that the student can return to Duke from a position of strength and without additional (undesired) interruptions in their degree pursuit." In October 2015 , Duke updated the leave policy to explicitly state that medical leaves can be longer or shorter than two semesters, whereas in the past the policy stated that they were required to be at least two semesters. OSR's policy on requesting an early return from MLOA is that "an early request will be given a full review," according to its website. But Donahue alleges that the administration has refused to answer his questions, that his petition was denied without explanation and that communications with him since have been "poor and infrequent." “I do not believe that this violation justifies my being barred from school for an entire semester,” wrote Donahue in his petition. “The Office of Student Returns seeks to take this accidental and minor infraction and force me to unnecessarily stay at home for another seven months with little to no resources.” Michael Schoenfeld, vice president for public affairs and government relations, declined to comment on Donahue's case, citing privacy concerns. The Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act does not allow universities to disclose certain private information without written consent. “The University respects the privacy rights of all students and cannot comment on any individual’s academic or medical situation,” he wrote. Donahue wrote that he thinks that Duke’s policy violates the Americans with Disabilities Act, arguing that there have been students on MLOA for physical illnesses who have been allowed to return after one semester. "This policy explicitly discriminates against the mentally ill, as a student who left with a physical ailment and wanted to return a semester later with doctor’s approval would never face this treatment," he wrote in his petition. Arlie Petters, dean of academic affairs and associate vice provost for undergraduate education, could not be reached in time for comment. Many Duke students have rallied behind Donahue. “I don't understand why Duke University, which has an internationally renowned hospital and medical school, isn't listening to the recommendations of trained health professionals in Jack's case,” wrote sophomore Solomon Yirga, one of Donahue's friends. “At this point, Jack is prepared to return and use local mental health resources while also having the social support of his friends.” Senior Pooja Mehta, who has dealt with mental illness herself, criticized the MLOA policy as "too broad to be truly effective." She said that only the opinions of the student’s medical professionals and medical professionals at Counseling and Psychological Services should determine whether a student is capable of returning. “The administration does not have the expertise and information to truly determine if a student is able to return to Duke,” Mehta wrote. “And it is truly upsetting that administrators everywhere choose to stand by flawed policies rather than make accommodations to do what is best for the student.” Larry Moneta, vice president for student affairs, also said that the administration had no comment on the matter.

title: 'Why?': Journalists debate role of gender in 2016 election as students struggle with results
subtitle: The words “motivating," “alarming” and “cathartic” were thrown around the Pink Parlor Room of East Duke Monday as frustrated students, journalists and professors grappled with the results of the 2016 presidential election.
author: Bre Bradham
text: The words “motivating," “alarming” and “cathartic” were thrown around the Pink Parlor Room of East Duke Monday as frustrated students, journalists and professors grappled with the results of the 2016 presidential election. Students in the “Women in the Public Sphere: History, Theory and Practice” course gave a presentation on interviews they conducted with friends and peers after the election in an effort to understand how millennials felt. This was followed by a roundtable discussion with two journalists—Annie Linskey, a reporter for the Boston Globe’s D.C. bureau, and Rebecca Traister, a writer-at-large for New York Magazine—as well as Deondra Rose assistant professor in the Sanford School of Public Policy. “I took this election as something of a referendum on the American Dream,” Rose said. “So often times we are given this idea that you work hard, play by the rules, become qualified, learn how to do it well and then you will be successful. I think the rules were turned on their head in this case.” Monday night’s student presentation was the capstone group project for a course taught by Rachel Seidman, visiting assistant professor of gender, sexuality and feminist studies. Students were tasked with interviewing two individuals and presenting their findings to the audience. Maria Magdalena Berrios, a graduate student in the class, said her interviewees felt that “everything changed after the election," and that they were scared of being interviewed because of the possible conflicts and ramifications. Senior Chaz Hawkins said that he was cooking eggs when his housemate informed him that he was voting for Trump—prompting Hawkins to plate the eggs, go into his room and yell “why?!” through the locked door. Another member of the class, sophomore Vivian Luo, said talking to people with different political views allowed her to take a step back and listen to the reasoning behind decisions that were different from hers. “For me, I was interviewing my conservative friend over the phone, so it was easy for me to scream into my pillow while she kept talking,” Luo said. The panel consisting Rose, Traister, and Linskey primarily discussed why Hillary Clinton lost the election. “I love that she was counting on a coalition of voters who this country’s power structures were built on top of—of people of color and women,” Traister said. “She was also betting that after electing the first African American president, that we would elect a woman. That was a risky bet, as we now know.” Traister noted that she has not always been a fan of Hillary, citing her support of John Edwards in the 2008 primary and her early criticism of Clinton’s “centrist” policies. As for the 2016 election, Traister said that she initially did not want Clinton to run at all—because gender is “always” a factor. “I didn’t want her to run because I was worried that it would end badly—that she would lose — and that it would be catastrophic,” she said. “The first person, the trailblazer, in any category bears too much symbolic weight, and no one person can bear all that weight.” Linskey, who followed Clinton for two years as a member of the traveling press pool, was critical of assertions that sexism was the deciding factor in the election. “Her inability to deal with her flaws was just jaw-dropping for me,” she said. Linskey, who like Clinton is a graduate of the all-female Wellesley College, said that sexism in politics manifests itself in a different and more internalized way. She noted that Massachusetts senator Elizabeth Warren looked herself in the mirror in 2014 and decided that Clinton was more qualified to run for the presidency, so decided not to run. Men, she explained, did not do the same—citing Jim Webb and Lincoln Chaffee. Traister, who sat down with Clinton while working on an article about her, drew a stark contrast between the personality Clinton embodied in their one-on-one conversation and her public persona. Reacting overall to the election, Rose said that was still trying to process it. Linskey noted that it was Clinton's election to lose. “She had every single advantage—money, endorsements,” Linksey said. “I just think that the person who lost this race was Hillary Clinton, and seeing so many women hear it breaks my heart. I just hope that you’re not going to take away from this some sort of huge rejection of women, or that people hate women. She did a bad job.”

title: Tax plan, immigration, guns highlight roundtable debate between Duke Democrats, College Republicans
subtitle: In an increasingly polarized political climate with a 24-hours news cycle and where social media has become a platform for political discourse, the last thing people seem willing to do is listen to opposing viewpoints.
author: Bre Bradham
text: In an increasingly polarized political climate with a 24-hours news cycle and where social media has become a platform for political discourse, the last thing people seem willing to do is listen to opposing viewpoints. But on Tuesday night, that’s the first thing that seven students from Duke Democrats and Duke College Republicans did. In a Listen First Conversation moderated by Pearce Godwin, Trinity ’08, the students discussed topics ranging from school choice to terrorism. The Duke Democrats were represented by sophomores Faven Getahun, Allison McHorse and Leah Abrams, who is the club’s president and the editorial page managing editor for The Chronicle. The students who spoke for the Duke College Republicans were sophomores Audrey Kornkven and Jake Chasan, junior Michael Brunetti and senior Colin Duffy, who is the club’s president. Here are three of the topics that generated the most interesting discussion from the students. National debt and President Donald Trump’s tax plan One of the first questions posed by Godwin was on the federal deficit and what students think about the state of national debt. Chasan drew the analogy of repeatedly taking out 30-year mortgages on cars that you replace every five years and explained that “eventually you become underneath a mountain of debt and you can’t pay it off.” He said that it’s essential for the United States to figure out how much is too much debt, and what can effectively be paid off without the the country being under that mountain. Abrams responded to the analogy, agreeing that the national debt is “an incredible problem.” “But I do think that it has to be tempered with our obligation to provide certain services for folks who are in need of them,” she said. She referenced the current tax plan being considered by Congress, and said there are ways to lower the national debt without reneging on responsibilities to provide programs like social security and Medicaid/Medicare. Duffy pushed back on negative views of the tax plan by critiquing the Congressional Budget Office’s assumption that the economy will only grow at 1.9 percent. He said that when good tax reforms are enacted that minimize loopholes, it is possible to see the economy take off. He called out multi-national corporations and the University itself for being beneficiaries of those loopholes. “Duke is taking advantage of the current system, in the sense that even us, we have millions of dollars parked off-shore for our endowments that we don’t have to pay taxes on,” Duffy said. McHorse took particular issue with the effects that the current plan would have on graduate students’ taxation rate for school costs. Duffy challenged the proposition that graduate students would be hurt by the bill. “What do you think would be more important though—having the ability to write off that interest or having a job there to allow you to pay off those loans?” he asked. “I just don’t have faith that the cutting of the corporate taxes will directly help people that much,” McHorse responded. “We’ve seen it have limited success, and I think there is no reason to hurt graduate students trying to pay back their loans in order to cut those corporate taxes when you could help in other ways.” Brunetti took a step back and summed up the overall view of the bill. “It’s kind of sad that it seems no one is really enthusiastic about the plan on either side,” he said. Immigration The students' discussion of immigration ranged from how to handle refugees to how strongly the United States should enforce illegal immigration laws. Getahun explained that in regard to refugees, she believes that the United States has the responsibility to bring in refugees and that it is critical that the government also offer aid and financial support once the refugees reach the United States. Duffy agreed that the United States has a responsibility to help refugees, but noted that the best way to do so is not necessarily by bringing them into the country. He suggested an alternative option—integrating them into another Middle Eastern country like Saudi Arabia where they share a common religion and language. “How do we take the money we’ve already allocated towards this and use it best?” he asked. Brunetti addressed the other end of the immigration spectrum, saying that the United States should shape policy and enforce it in relation to legal and illegal immigration. He said that it is easy to say legal immigration is good, but things get trickier when it comes to illegal immigration. He said that the right response was somewhere in between the positive economic impact and the negative judicial extreme of illegal immigration being a crime. Gun control Abrams kicked off the discussion on gun legislation by saying that she does not know of any reason that a person should own a semi-automatic weapon, and pointed to Congress’ lack of progress on the issue. “The fact that there has been absolutely no action on gun reform from Congress is absolutely irresponsible, especially given the number of mass shootings we have seen in just my lifetime and the amount of people that have been killed,” she said. Kornkven offered an alternative view on how to curb the number of mass shootings and gun violence. She advocated for better enforcement of the current gun laws, and noted that there are non-regulatory ways of addressing the issue as well, like the media not using shooters’ names in order to not encourage copycat killers. Duffy concurred with Kornkven on increased enforcement of the current laws, noting in particular a lack of prosecuting felons who try to purchase guns. Brunetti raised the question of whether or not a successful ban on all semi-automatic guns would truly bring an end to mass killings. He said people intent on causing harm may just turn to using bombs or vehicles instead. “I’ve noticed that when people are really determined to do something heinous like that, they will try to find a way to pull it off,” he said. “People are, unfortunately, really creative.”

title: 'Narrowly self-interested and disgusting': Duke professor discusses N.C. gerrymandering
subtitle: In a state in which both sides claim the other has threatened the institution of democracy with gerrymandering, a Duke professor suggests that there’s a better way out.
author: Ben Leonard
text: In a state in which both sides claim the other has threatened the institution of democracy with gerrymandering, a Duke professor suggests that there’s a better way out. A federal court-appointed Stanford professor submitted “improved” election map proposals Dec. 1 to fix unconstitutional racial gerrymandering in North Carolina, drawing ire from Republican state legislators.
