title: Duke Community Standard updated to address AI, re-define disciplinary action using ‘educational framing’
subtitle: The updated Duke Community Standard now includes a section addressing artificial intelligence and cheating, along with additions to policies regarding hazing and disciplinary outcomes.
author: Sophie Endrud
text: From the prohibition of unrestrained animals to the addition of BORGS to common-source containers, the Duke Community Standard provides guidelines and regulations that apply to all aspects of student life, and this year, several changes have been made to the nearly-70 page document. The Office for Student Conduct and Community Standards, which investigates and resolves alleged violations of University policy, told The Chronicle the details behind the updates made to the Duke Community Standard, which fall under themes of artificial intelligence, personal freedom, respect and learning-focused punishment. “We review our policies every year to address emerging patterns and trends that we’ve seen on college campuses across the country,” the OSCCS wrote in an email to The Chronicle. A primary addition to the updated Community Standard is the regulation of AI. In the section on academic dishonesty, AI is included as a form of “unauthorized shared resources.” Additionally, the new Computing and Electric Communications section outlines how to make acceptable use of computing and electronic resources. “Once advanced AI software became accessible to the general public, as seen with large language models like ChatGPT, we knew that the updated Duke Community Standard should include guidance on how to approach this emerging technology, particularly where it concerns appropriate citations and academic integrity,” OSCCS wrote in the email. The DCS still allows for departments and professors to make their own decisions about AI in their classrooms, while maintaining University-wide policies. The DCS’s hazing policy has also been changed to include a more robust list of sample activities and situations. “In Student Affairs, we understand how powerful student groups can be in creating a sense of belonging for various Duke students ... We also understand that Duke, like every college campus, has students who may be vulnerable to hazing experiences,” OSCCS wrote. “Because of the University’s commitment to student wellbeing, campus safety, and hazing prevention, the DCS includes an entire section on hazing.” Reports of hazing shared with their office will be met with “appropriate follow-up and action” which could include investigations, conduct hearings, interim actions and restrictions. A new Advertisements section was added to the DCS, providing details about the use of banners, chalking, posters, announcements and bulletin boards. Banners are allowed on the exterior or interior of the Broadhead Center, East Union Building, Bryan Center, the Plaza and residence halls, so long as they are within 5-by-12 feet and without hanging objects or parts covering a room’s windows. Chalking is prohibited on all surfaces. In writing about the new sections, OSCCS referenced Duke’s history of “promoting academic freedom, self-authorship and self-determination.” Students and scholars have “the autonomy to define their beliefs, values, viewpoints and search for new knowledge in their respective disciplines.” However, it is up to the University to provide guidelines for the parameters on how “ideas can be shared and challenged,” OSCCS wrote. Similarly, the office wrote that policies on picketing and demonstrations describe appropriate means for students to engage in activism without causing significant disruption or infringing on the rights of others to express their ideas. If the standard is bent or broken, the OSCCS wants to discuss disciplinary action with “an educational framing.” To do so, the term “sanctions” has been rephrased as “outcomes,” which the OSCCS wrote “more appropriately captured the educational approach we use in conduct cases.” “The Conduct process at most universities is often misunderstood to be punitive spaces where students ‘get in trouble.’ However, one of the goals of the OSCCS is to continue to change that impression at Duke,” OSCCS wrote. New disciplinary outcomes have been added to the DCS. Now, a “reprimand” is a potential outcome for a DCS violation, and serves as formal warning, while an “admonition” serves as an informal warning. Admonitions and reprimands are not reportable disciplinary matters to external entities, including graduate schools, employers and study abroad programs.

title: The Chronicle’s guide to volunteering in Durham and at Duke
subtitle: The Chronicle compiled a quick guide on several volunteer organizations that are accessible for students both on- and off-campus.
author: Luis Pereira
text: Looking for volunteer opportunities? Don't miss out on the chance to make a positive impact in both Duke and Durham by exploring the various volunteer options offered. The Chronicle compiled a quick guide on several volunteer organizations that are accessible for students both on- and off-campus. An eight-minute walk from East Campus, located at 2050 Chapel Hill Road, The Scrap Exchange has made it its mission to provide creativity and community through reuse since 1991. From dressing dolls to measuring, cutting and pricing fabric for sale, students can bring back gently-used items to life for the community. With a plethora of hours to choose from, there are plenty of opportunities for interested craft-lovers. Events are held Wednesday through Sunday from 11 a.m. to 6 p.m. For students with a passion for social media and marketing, the Poof Center is a cohesive environment where creative minds come together to collaborate with teens and contribute their skills to help build a vibrant online community . Responsibilities include monitoring social media outlets, responding to inquiries and reporting any unusual behaviors online. Events are held Monday through Friday from 4 to 8 p.m., and volunteers should expect to commit two hours of their time for each event. Relive elementary and middle school by tutoring children in the Durham Area with LEAP , an organization that focuses on the success of education within the Latino community by providing high quality support to children. There is no requirement to speak Spanish, and volunteers have the option to work individually with children ranging from six to 12 years old or work in a live preschool setting with three to four-year-olds. Volunteers must complete a one-hour training alongside a background check and commit a minimum of two hours per week. On Oct. 18, Duke will host its 46th annual volunteer fair at Penn Pavilion from 11:30 a.m. to 2:30 p.m . This fair will be an opportunity for students to interact with local non-profit organizations, discover volunteer possibilities within the community and learn more about Durham. If none of these cater to your needs, the Office of Durham and Community Affairs has a website dedicated to matching community needs with Duke volunteers.

title: Efforts underway to improve Durham walkability through new pedestrian infrastructure, human-oriented design
subtitle: Safe facility planning, national collaboration and equitable community engagement are shaping Durham’s many transportation safety projects, according to Sean Egan, Durham’s director of transportation.
author: Michael Austin
text: Durham is imagining a brighter — or at least higher-visibility — future for pedestrians. Safe facility planning, national collaboration and equitable community engagement are shaping Durham’s many transportation safety projects, according to Sean Egan, Durham’s director of transportation. These projects are in response to a “troubling rise in fatalities and serious injuries for bicyclists and pedestrians.” For Egan, a measure of a great city is how easy it is to get around without owning a car. His priority is the safety of “residents, pedestrians, transit riders [and] bicyclists” and the “folks who walk and bike and roll through our city.” Durham joined the National Association of City Transportation Officials in early 2020 and takes guidance from their examples of high-quality pedestrian infrastructure. “We’ve joined this network of city transportation officials to make sure that we can take advantage of the knowledge and the examples that our peer cities have undertaken,” Egan said. One key project is high-visibility crosswalks, which draw the attention of motorists. Last year, these crosswalks were implemented on Erwin Road near Duke University Hospital, an area with high numbers of pedestrians. High visibility crosswalks include “zebra” designs which “improve yielding behavior." The city is also implementing “leading pedestrian intervals” at various intersections. When vehicles and pedestrians get a green light at the same time, turning vehicles will often “jump out” and block the crosswalk. A leading interval gives pedestrians a five-second head start to cross. The city is also trying to ensure that crossing times accommodate differences in the time it takes people to cross roadways. The Durham Rail Trail, a planned 1.8-mile trail on the west side of downtown extending to Avondale Drive, looks to expand Durham’s trail network. Egan said that the trail used a planning process that was inclusive of the “needs that were voiced by residents, particularly residents from communities typically excluded from the decision-making process.” During the process, Durham developed its Equitable Community Engagement Blueprint , in which the city gathers input from residents about “where they are, whether that’s at a grocery store or at Durham Station.” Buffered bike lanes and “traffic-calming” are included in Durham’s goal of getting to “zero fatalities and zero serious injuries” on its transportation network. This goal was affirmed in 2017 as part of Durham’s Vision Zero initiative. Vision Zero recognizes several traffic-calming techniques , which intend to “create safe and attractive streets” and “reduce the need for police enforcement.” Egan mentioned traffic-calming projects in places such as the Holloway Street and Fayetteville Street corridors, which aim to lower vehicle speeds to decrease the likelihood of fatalities when a pedestrian is hit by a vehicle. Egan also stressed the importance of setting aside funding for pedestrian network improvements with a focus on underserved communities. Residents also voiced an interest in curb ramps for people who use mobility devices such as wheelchairs and safe pathways to bus-stops, according to Egan. “We’ve heard a great deal from residents about their interest in walkability in our city,” he said. “What we found is that ... many of our traditionally underserved areas do not have a connected sidewalk network in the way that some of the more advantaged areas of our city do.” Junior Mikey Schwartz, who instructed the house course Urban Studies 101, Breaking the Duke Bubble, sees many benefits to walkability, naming mental health, aesthetics and affordability as just a few. “When you build [cities] for a car, everything is at a much larger scale,” Schwartz said. “But when you build for humans, you can condense everything in.” Schwartz views this scale as environmentally sustainable and better for connections as “social animals.” He also points to zoning and other transportation reforms as promising solutions. As a biker, junior Addie Geitner advocates a separation between bike lanes and parking areas to prevent “dooring,” which is when bikers are struck by the opened doors of parked vehicles. She also has concerns about narrow roads such as “one-way pairs.” “Durham has managed to do that on every single side of downtown, which I never quite understand,” Geitner said. Geitner believes that the reason many people love their college experience is because it’s their first time living in a walkable community. “I think Durham is the best part of Duke,” Geitner said, adding that she’d like to spend more time downtown. Geitner noted that the more “generally accessible” the city is, the more fun she could have with her friends.

title: Duke’s 100th year, Duke’s 100th incoming class
subtitle: As Duke continues to celebrate its Centennial, The Chronicle looked back on the University's inaugural first-year class and got to know members of the Class of 2028.
author: Zoe Kolenovsky, Abby Spiller
text: After officially transitioning from Trinity College to Duke University in December 1924, Duke’s inaugural first-year class stepped foot on campus in the fall of 1925. Now in 2024, another crop of first-years will make history as the University’s 100th incoming class. “The Class of 2028 has arrived at a very special moment in Duke’s history,” President Vincent Price wrote in an Aug. 19 email to The Chronicle. “As we celebrate our Centennial year, the arrival of Duke’s 100th entering class of undergraduates — and the exciting ideas, ambitions and perspectives they bring with them — are in many ways symbolic of the incredible opportunities ahead for the entire University.” When the Class of 1929 joined the Blue Devil community, The Chronicle commemorated their arrival in a piece titled “ The New Freshman .” “Especially does one have difficulty in discovering the typical freshman because of the heterogenuity [sic] of the members of this class,” the Sept. 23, 1925, article reads. “The trains bring these new men from climes that vary as greatly as sunny California and cold, bleak Alaska, from balsam-clad peaks of the Appalachians to the sandy beaches of Florida, from the rustic mountaineer homes of the Unakas to the palatial residences of Riverside Drive in New York; and in individual differences the freshmen vary as greatly as in environmental circumstances.” Though much has certainly changed in the intervening century, diversity remains a defining attribute of the Class of 2028. The 1,711 members of this year’s class hail from across the globe. They also represent an effort by University administration to increase the socioeconomic diversity of Duke’s student body, which Dean of Undergraduate Admissions Christoph Guttentag expressed in a March email to The Chronicle where he noted the University’s ongoing goal of “attracting and admitting a higher percentage of students eligible for Pell grants.” The Class of 2028 is the first to contend with the Supreme Court’s June 2023 decision to end race-based affirmative action in college admissions. The incoming class also represents the most selective in University history, with record low acceptance rates for both the Early Decision and Regular Decision application rounds at 12.9% and 4.1%, respectively. While a century has passed since Duke welcomed its first class, similarities remain between the first Blue Devils and the new first-years. “Even though they may dress differently, have different accents, cherish different ideals and be unlike in physical appearances, each is a dealer in futurity ... and each is a profound optimist,” the 1925 Chronicle article reads. First-year Caroline McSwain, who hails from a small school in Skaneateles, New York, shared her excitement to be part of an intellectually diverse community, as well as one filled with students passionate both inside and outside of the classroom. McSwain comes from a long line of Blue Devils, including both her parents and some cousins. But joining the Duke community in its 100th year is particularly special for her and her family for another reason — her great-grandfather, Wyatt McSwain, was a part of Duke’s inaugural first-year class in 1924, marking a centennial of Duke history within her own family. When speaking about the significance of this milestone, McSwain shared that her family “is super excited” and that “it’s a big deal in [their] house.” First-year Jada Ennine comes to Duke from Powder Springs, Georgia. Like McSwain, she is “looking forward to the community aspect of Duke,” including meeting new students through her orientation group Project Wellness and student organizations she hopes to later join. She expressed gratitude for “all the help” provided by upperclassman orientation leaders during move-in, which made her experience a “smooth process.” First-year Vassilios Ringas from Richmond, Virginia, shared his goal of pushing himself beyond his comfort zone and what he would “normally do” during his time at Duke. He said he chose to attend the University because of its balance between having strong academics and a socially engaged student body. Helping Ringas move in was his father, Evangelos Ringas, Pratt ‘96, who recounted his first-year move-in experience. He emphasized the diversity that he witnessed at Duke, “even in the early 90s.” “As a freshman, when I moved in, [I realized] I had a person next to me from Germany and a person a couple doors down who came overseas from China,” he said. “... I learned a lot from different cultures and having late-night dorm conversations.” Duke has seen significant change throughout the past 100 years. Over the course of 10 presidential administrations, the University greatly increased its enrollment, admitted its first students of color and merged the men’s and women’s colleges. And through it all, Duke students’ pursuit of excellence persists and is continually refined with each new class. “This is a moment of transformation for Duke, when we can see more clearly than ever before how we might lead in the century to come,” Price said of Duke’s Centennial . “It’s also a moment of extraordinary continuity, as the seeds of our current and future excellence that were planted and cultivated throughout our University’s first hundred years come into bloom.” Andrew Bae and Halle Vazquez contributed reporting.

title: Lilly Library is closed for renovation. Here’s how to access library resources on East Campus.
subtitle: Library services on East Campus have been temporarily relocated to the Bishop’s House until renovations are completed in 2026.
author: Michael Austin
text: Lilly Library is under renovation, a process that began in May and is slated for completion in 2026. The estimated $64 million overhaul — dubbed “The Lilly Project” — is causing an 18- to 24-month closure of Lilly Library, with staff and library services relocating to the Bishop’s House located behind Bassett dorm. Although certain resources were temporarily unavailable during the summer relocation process, the Bishop’s House opened Aug. 1 and began offering essential library services. Here’s what you should know about accessing library resources on East Campus this year. During the renovation, students can meet with Lilly librarians and staff in the Bishop’s House. As the temporary home for many of Lilly’s services, the Bishop’s House also enables students to print and scan, return books, pick up holds and reserve materials for classes. Until Aug. 26 — the first day of classes for the fall semester — Lilly at Bishop’s House will be open 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday through Friday. Starting Aug. 26, the schedule will shift to 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. Monday through Thursday and 9 a.m. to 6 p.m Friday. For visitors, the building has two 30-minute parking spaces available for use free of charge. According to a Feb. 28 post on The Lilly Project’s website, “as much of Lilly’s collection as possible” will remain accessible during the renovation. Students are encouraged to find the locations of items of interest in the online catalog and to take advantage of the “90 million books and other materials” available from peer institutions through the Interlibrary Requests program. The Lilly Library 3D printers — managed by the Innovation Co-Lab — will be relocated to Wilson dorm during the renovation. The Value Transfer Station, which enables students to load money into their DukeCard Flex account, will be relocated to the East Campus Union. The Duke Music Library , located beside Baldwin Auditorium, will remain open and continue to offer “broader library services." After Lilly’s closure on May 5, library staff began the process of relocating library resources to the historic Bishop’s House. Once the residence of former Trinity College President John Kilgo from 1911 to 1915, the Bishop’s House has also served as a dormitory, an infirmary, the Duke University Press headquarters and most recently the administrative office space for Duke Continuing Studies until the COVID-19 pandemic. Various features of the Bishop’s House, including its fireplaces and wrap-around porch, have remained since the building’s construction in 1911. The move to the Bishop’s House entailed efforts to carefully relocate tens of thousands of books, as well as films, journals and other collection materials. The process began with moving Lilly’s artwork to university storage facilities during spring break. The collection is currently divided among offsite storage facilities and the Perkins and Bostock libraries, where materials will remain for the duration of the renovation. Lilly was originally built in 1927 as Duke’s first library. However, the facility has not seen expansion or substantial renovation since it was first constructed. The Lilly renovation was initially announced in 2018 and set to begin in 2020, but plans were delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Two separate $10 million grants from the Duke Endowment in 2021 and 2023 helped fund and relaunch the renovation. The upgrades to Lilly seek to “dramatically increase the building’s footprint,” expanding its square footage by 75% and adding increased seating, study spaces and “technology-equipped project rooms.” The expansion will include a new staffed writing studio, a testing center and a 75-seat assembly room. Improvements will be made to “behind the scenes” staff areas, Lilly’s reading rooms and the overall accessibility of the building. The renovation will also address “urgent facility needs,” including upgrades to the building’s infrastructure, heating and cooling systems, environmental controls and waterproofing. Additionally, a “cafe-like commons filled with natural light” will be added to the west side of the building, which seeks to connect Lilly with the “bustling residential ‘backyard’ of East Campus.” The extensive expansion is resulting in the closure — and eventual removal — of six East Campus tennis courts. The six courts, which are currently located behind Lilly, will be replaced with “a landscaped quad, featuring grass and trees.” The University plans to build six new tennis courts and six new pickleball courts in a different East Campus location by 2025. The University publishes renovation-related updates and announcements on the Lilly Project website.

title: Political theorist Yuval Levin discusses polarization, legislative deadlock in advance of election
subtitle: According to Levin, “the big difference about 21st-century American politics is not polarization — it is deadlock.”
author: Tanya Wan
text: Egan Visiting Professor Yuval Levin spoke of the increasingly divided culture of U.S. politics in a Tuesday conversation at the Sanford School of Public Policy. The event, titled “ Civil Discourse in an Age of Polarization ,” was hosted by the DeWitt Wallace Center for Media and Democracy, the Duke Program in American Grand Strategy and Polis: Center for Politics. Levin joined Duke Jan. 1 as the sixth Egan visiting professor in recognition of his work relating to the media and contemporary issues. In the position, he is delivering a series of lectures throughout the year and teaching a course this fall. Levin previously served as a domestic policy staff member in the White House under President George W. Bush. He is the founding editor of National Affairs magazine, director of social, cultural and constitutional studies at the American Enterprise Institute and a contributing opinion writer at The New York Times. After working on issues including the federal budget, welfare and domestic policy in the Bush cabinet, Levin observed that “politics looked more like a kind of partisan theater and less like the sorts of policy debates that [policy wonks] were trying to sustain and nourish.” He began to examine political divisions in America more closely in work that first emerged as a graduate-level dissertation on political fragmentation and later culminated in his books “The Fractured Republic” and “A Time to Build,” published in 2016 and 2020, respectively. According to Levin, the left-right ideological divide remains pressing but is no longer as significant as the “up-down divide,” which is indicative of the running tension between the public and its government leaders. He explained this as the result of political elites’ lack of competence, restraint and humility across party lines, which has led to a steep decline in the public’s trust in institutions. Levin claimed that “polarization is not exactly the issue” in American politics despite its salience in public discourse. “The big difference about 21st-century American politics is not polarization,” he said. “It is deadlock.” He described this deadlock — the consequence of “50-50 politics” — as the result of both political parties being minority parties that focus on retaining an existing base as opposed to building coalitions to engage new supporters. Levin noted that, in the last two years, the margin of difference that determined the majority in the House of Representatives was under five votes. “The incentives ... have all turned against the kind of cross-partisan accommodation and bargaining that is the work of Congress,” said Levin. “... Congress’ dysfunction is the biggest problem in our system.” In light of such political deadlocks, Levin emphasized the need for parties to build coalitions and seek compromise in order to create progress that is admittedly “frustrating, ... slower and more cumbersome” but embedded in durable legislation. “I think it's not likely that this coming election will be the most important of your lifetime,” Levin said of the presidential election this November. “... I don’t think democracy’s on the line.” He argued that because of the “50-50” political environment, “whoever wins this election is likely to have very narrow congressional majorities ... and [will] be unable to do very much that is very significant.” Levin added that “in a sense, the stakes are lower than usual,” given that “the most important promise that each [party] can make to its voters is not to be the other party.” In Levin’s view, concrete political change is unlikely to take place unless the elected president were willing to engage in continued negotiations with the opposition party. Levin suggested that presidents focus on legislative accomplishments in Congress instead of on “mak[ing] a lot of policy directly through regulation [and] administrative action,” since such sweeping changes tend to be unstable and are subject to reversal when a new administration takes power. Levin cited the Civil Rights Act and Great Society program as examples of substantial policymaking achieved by a president, both of which were promoted by President Lyndon Johnson but were ultimately legislative changes passed through Congress. “I think ... our system can only work if it has Congress at its center, if the big decisions are made by the representative branch of our government,” he said. “The presidency is not it.” Levin also warned of the dangers of public leaders like former President Donald Trump and President Joe Biden speaking “recklessly” about the constitutional system and the legitimacy of the courts. However, in his view, institutions are “fairly strong” and “tend to resist these kinds of pressures.” “Four years from now, you're probably going to be fine, and if you're not fine, it's probably not going to be because of the president,” he said. When asked about his predictions for the 2024 presidential election, Levin shrugged. “I have no idea,” he said. In light of the race’s close margins, he expressed concerns about Democratic nominee Kamala Harris’ impartiality in certifying the election’s results, a responsibility that falls to her as sitting vice president. However, he acknowledged that similar situations occurred in 1960 with then-Vice President Richard Nixon and in 2000 with then-Vice President Al Gore, saying that both politicians “went out of their way to assure the country that they did not think they had any role in deciding the result of the election.” “I think it is very important that Vice President Harris do the same, whatever happens,” he said. In discussing the significance of the Constitution in American politics, Levin stated that it was built on the premise that disagreement is inevitable “in a free society.” Levin believes the Constitution is crucial for facilitating cross-partisan compromise and preventing majority tyranny, as it seeks to answer the question, “How can we act together when we don’t think alike?” He cited the essay Federalist No. 10, in which Founding Father James Madison “insist[ed] that unity is possible because unity does not mean unanimity.” Responding to a question from an audience member on the role of educational institutions in addressing polarization, Levin said that “the politicization of campus life is a tremendous problem for American society” because of the critical part universities play in maintaining democracy. He emphasized the importance of “creating some spaces where there can be internal diversity on campus,” which would “allow real debate to happen [and] freedom of thought to exist.” Levin asserted that leaders of educational institutions like Duke have a responsibility to “model intellectual openness and diversity of thought.”

title: How the Class of 2028 spent their first year: Methods and summary statistics from The Chronicle’s survey data
subtitle: As the Class of 2029 enters as Duke’s newest undergraduates, our analysis of the Class of 2028's survey data aims to highlight key aspects of the first-year experience.
author: Zoe Kolenovsky
text: Editor's note: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2028 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in March and April 2025. Read the rest of our survey coverage here . The Chronicle is releasing survey data about the Class of 2028 this week. As the Class of 2029 enters as Duke’s newest undergraduates, our analysis of the Class of 2028's survey data aims to highlight key aspects of the first-year experience. For the eighth consecutive year, The Chronicle surveyed first-year students about their lifestyles, demographics, plans at Duke and more. Questions ranged from standardized test scores and religious beliefs to approximate family income and use of campus resources. The results of this survey will be published in stories on the Class of 2028’s paths to Duke, political beliefs and lifestyle, social life on campus and engagement in the classroom. The findings will be released throughout the coming week in a series of articles that give a deeper look at Duke’s most recent first-year experience. The survey was administered from March 24 to April 4. A total of 224 first-year students completed the survey in its entirety. Three $100 prizes were randomly awarded to participants who filled out the entire survey. There are 1,739 members of the Class of 2028 according to the class profile , marking a 13% completion rate. Last year’s survey was conducted in October and November 2023 and was completed by 16% of the Class of 2027. In total, this year’s survey asked 79 questions, compared to 87 questions last year. Participants saw different questions based on their responses to avoid redundancy. For example, students who were not from North or South Carolina were not asked whether the Carolina Initiative — which provides full-tuition grants to students from the Carolinas — impacted their decision to apply to or enroll at Duke. This year, we removed a question on high school GPAs, since these are reported differently across schools and respondent conversions can be unreliable. We also removed questions that have historically not yielded significant findings, such as whether respondents took a gap year or lived in a single or double dorm room. We added questions on whether respondents received a Pell Grant; had become more or less involved with their religious community since arriving at Duke; would support or oppose Duke adopting a policy of institutional neutrality (with a definition of such a policy provided); felt Duke was putting too much or too little emphasis on diversity, equity and inclusion; or had been involved with a student-run affinity group since arriving on campus. This year’s survey also had new questions asking respondents how many Duke undergraduates they knew prior to their acceptance to the University, how easy or difficult they felt it was to express their political opinions freely on campus and how often they used breakfast and dinner equivalency. Because we conducted the survey in the spring, not in the fall as in previous years, we were able to ask whether students had participated in additional campus activities and traditions. We added questions on whether respondents voted in the November 2024 election, rushed a sorority or fraternity, rushed a selective living group, tented for the Duke vs. North Carolina home men’s basketball game, or ran out of food points at the end of the fall semester. We analyzed only complete responses this year, after we began removing incomplete responses from our analysis last year. Jerry Reiter , professor of statistical science who previously served as department chair, told The Chronicle that partially completed responses could represent a different population than the fully completed cases, so combining them could misrepresent the survey results. Reiter previously told The Chronicle that students’ reasons for opting out of the survey, including lack of access to the survey or low enthusiasm for Duke, could make the results less representative of the class. He also noted that students may not have chosen to complete the questionnaire due to time constraints, which could result in the nonrespondents being systematically different than the respondents skewing the results of the survey. Below, we compare our survey data with the official Class of 2028 profile from the Office of Undergraduate Admissions to show how The Chronicle’s survey responses may differ from the overall class. Women were overrepresented in The Chronicle’s survey compared to the Class of 2028 profile. In our survey, which included more gender options than the class profile, 65.6% of respondents identified as women, 33.5% as men and 0.9% as nonbinary or genderqueer. The class profile has 53% of first-years as female or female-identifying and 47% as male or male-identifying. As in recent years, The Chronicle’s survey gathered race and ethnicity data differently than Duke’s admissions office, which reports race and ethnicity based on the categories used by the Common Application, Coalition Application and Questbridge application. The Chronicle included “other” and “Middle Eastern/North African” categories and separated American Indian or Alaska Native students from Native Hawaiian students, whereas Duke’s reporting system does not. Duke also groups Pacific Islanders with Asian students, but we grouped Pacific Islanders with Native Hawaiian students. Students are allowed to select more than one race and ethnicity in the data reported by Duke’s admissions office, meaning their breakdown percentages do not add up to 100. The intent was for respondents to be able to select multiple identities in our survey as well, but none did. Between 15% and 17.6% of respondents have selected multiple identities in recent iterations of The Chronicle’s first-year survey, making this year’s results an outlier. The form settings for our survey were set to allow multiple responses to this question, so it is likely that some aspect of the software experienced an error when collecting or reporting the data. As a result, multiple racial and ethnic groups appear underrepresented in our data because the ethnic percent breakdowns add up to 100. In our survey, 10.3% of respondents identified as Hispanic/Latinx; 11.2% identified as Black or African American; 33.0% identified as Asian; 40.6% identified as white; and 1.8% identified as Middle Eastern or North African. None identified as Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, or as American Indian or Alaska Native. About 3.1% identified as a race or ethnicity not listed. According to the official admissions profile, 14% of first-year students identify as Hispanic/Latinx; 1.3% identify as Native American, American Indian, Native Alaskan or Native Hawaiian; 13% identify as Black or African American; 29% identify as Asian, Asian American or Pacific Islander and 52% identify as white. About 11% of first-years did not specify a race or ethnicity, and 7% of students identified as more than one race. Our question addressing disability reflected the Americans with Disabilities Act’s definition of a disability. About 6.7% of respondents identified as having a physical impairment, and 7.1% identified as having a mental impairment. Students could identify as having multiple impairments. 25% of undergraduate students were registered with the Student Disability Access Office as of spring 2025, according to SDAO Director Leigh Millar, so the number of first-years with disabilities may be underrepresented in our survey. The official Class of 2028 profile states that 87% of students are from the United States and 13% are international students. Slightly more domestic students were represented in our survey at 91.1%, with 8.9% self-reporting as international students. About 15% of the Class of 2028 is from North Carolina compared to 22.8% of respondents in our survey. North Carolina was the most common home state for our respondents by far, followed by New York, Florida and Texas, with California and South Carolina tied for fifth. The Office of Undergraduate Admissions does not publish students’ financial information, but according to the most recent data from the U.S. Census Bureau, the median family income in the United States as of 2023 was $80,610. Seventy-one percent of survey respondents reported an annual family income over $80,000, showing that Duke’s Class of 2028 family incomes are not representative of national trends. According to Duke’s data , about 51% of undergraduates receive financial aid. 58% of respondents in The Chronicle’s survey receive financial aid, meaning our results overrepresent the proportion of students on financial aid. The official class profile stated that the percentage of students who went to public secondary school was about 60%, while 78.5% of respondents in The Chronicle’s survey reported attending public school. Our survey distinguished between charter and non-charter public schools — 8.0% and 70.5%, respectively — while Duke’s data does not. Duke also reports international students as a separate category. The Chronicle’s survey underrepresents how many students went to private schools. Our survey overrepresents the proportion of students who applied Early Decision. About 52.2% of the survey’s respondents were admitted in the Early Decision round, compared to 46.5% of the full Class of 2028, according to Kathy Phillips, interim dean of undergraduate admissions. The middle 50% of SAT scores for respondents in our survey is approximately 1510 to 1565, and the middle 50% of ACT scores is 34 to 35. This aligns closely with official data, which places the middle 50% range for SAT and ACT scores at 1510 to 1560 and 34 to 35, respectively. The Chronicle’s survey also found that 20% of respondents were first-generation college students compared to 12% of the official Class of 2028 profile, overrepresenting the figure by eight percentage points.

title: 2024-25 Chron15: Duke's icons
subtitle: Duke’s icons are the people everyone knows — for their kindness, passion, talent or intellect. They are the people who make your cross-campus commutes possible or who you see plastered across the television in Krafthouse.
author: Staff Reports
text: Duke’s icons are the people everyone knows — for their kindness, passion, talent or intellect. They are the people who make your cross-campus commutes possible or who you see plastered across the television in Krafthouse. The icons on this year’s Chron15 list are beloved by the University community for spreading joy, knowledge and Blue Devil pride. What can you say about Cooper Flagg that hasn’t already been said? Entering his freshman season at Duke, Flagg was hyped up as one of the greatest basketball prospects of his generation. Before he even logged a minute on the court in Durham, Flagg became the first NCAA men’s basketball player to sign an NIL deal with Gatorade. It goes without saying that the Newport, Maine, native lived up to the hype and then some. He became the ninth player in Blue Devil history to win multiple national player of the year honors. His play was nothing short of a tour de force, featuring powerful dunks, outstanding playmaking and lockdown defense. Flagg set the Duke and ACC freshman record for points in a game and led Duke in every major statistical category — points, rebounds, assists, steals and blocks. He led the No. 1-seed Blue Devils to the NCAA Final Four, where the season met its end against Houston despite a brilliant performance by Flagg. “The thing about [Flagg] that you love is he’s unselfish, so he’s always trying to make the right play,” head coach Jon Scheyer said. “I just trust his competitiveness and his spirit and his intuition during a game to know what it takes to win.” On April 21, Flagg declared for the NBA draft, where he is widely expected to be selected first overall. While the Blue Devils didn’t reach championship glory, Flagg’s one and only season in Durham will be remembered as one of the greatest in school history. He’ll be reminisced for years to come by the Blue Devils that witnessed his transcendent talent. – Tyler Walley, vol.120 associate sports editor Luis Alonso Juárez is the beloved driver of the C1 “party bus” — students across campus know of the loud pop melodies that deliver them from West to East and back. Juárez has driven the C1 for six years, becoming a familiar face on campus through his mood-boosting bus rides. However, his life in the United States began 30 years ago, when he immigrated from Honduras. He began working for a construction company, but then transitioned to Duke housekeeping and eventually to bus driving after obtaining his commercial drivers license. Juárez plays a range of music genres on the bus to connect him to his passengers, and he says that he alternates between playing genres he thinks they’ll enjoy and songs that may be more unfamiliar during his daily rides between East and West Campus. For Juárez, driving the C1 offers an outlet for creativity. He has made it his mission to create a fun environment for students, putting music on because, in his own words, “students enjoy themselves, and they’re young, and they like the music.” With long shifts being a regular part of the job, the music also helps Juárez keep his energy up on the road. Now, he is pursuing his GED through evening classes offered at Duke and teaching English to other English language-learners — but he’s still brightening all of our days on the party bus. – Chronicle Staff Reports Few performances from the fall semester were more unforgettable than Ed Sheeran’s headlining of the Centennial Founders’ Day Celebration and Concert on Sept. 26. Sheeran brought his personal, stripped-down style to the stage at Wallace Wade, performing with just a guitar and a Loop Station. He crafted all of his backing tracks live, including for hits like “Castle on the Hill” and “Shape of You,” layering guitar riffs and vocals to create a one-of-a-kind concert experience. More than just a concert, Sheeran’s presence transformed the rainy night in Durham into a magical centennial moment. From a surprise proposal in section 24 of the crowd during “Perfect” to an electrifying encore of “Bad Habits,” the rainy night was a core memory for Duke students, alumni and faculty alike. His presence elevated the entire weekend, which included a win over UNC football, fostering joy, celebration, and unity on campus. It was absolutely, as Ed would say, perfect. – Chronicle Staff Reports When news broke of Mike Elko’s midnight flight to College Station, it would’ve been easy to imagine him taking the magic out of Wallace Wade, but that’s exactly what Nina King didn’t do. Enter Manny Diaz. Diaz arrived with a chip on his shoulder after unceremonious departures from Texas in 2013 and Miami in 2021, eager to prove that he was capable of leading a Power 4 team to success. The task wasn't easy, as Diaz faced a depleted roster due to transfers and graduations. Yet, in the biggest portal haul in school history, Diaz showed his recruiting wizardry bringing in 22 players , including star Texas QB Maalik Murphy, who would go on to break Duke records. He instilled a sense of determination and grit into the team that produced some of the most iconic moments of the year. But perhaps the most iconic moment came against UNC, when Coach Diaz gave the team a rallying half-time speech that gave them the confidence to pull off one of the most stunning comebacks of the college football season, coming back from down 20-0 in just under 20 minutes — Duke’s first win against the Tar Heels since 2018. What made the win so much sweeter was Diaz finally defeating Mack Brown, the man who fired him all those years ago at Texas. Diaz continued this momentum, leading Duke to its biggest bowl game in over a decade and capping an incredible season. Beyond the field, Diaz immersed himself in Duke’s community, attending more Duke sporting events than even the most dedicated students. His shouts resonated from Cameron Indoor to San Antonio and the Final Four. Long gone are the days of an empty Wallace Wade Stadium — Manny Diaz truly bleeds blue. – Arya Korrapati, Trinity ‘27 Senior resident Dr. Rebekah Boyd is a blend of surgical proficiency, global humanitarianism and academic leadership. She graduated from Columbia University’s medical school and is a former emergency department and Cardiothoracic Intensive Care Unit nurse. At Duke, Dr. Boyd leads a Global Health Bass Connections project. Her project unites students from across many disciplines and personal backgrounds to solve the world disparities of pediatric cardiac care. Under her leadership, the team is working on the logistical, political, and ethical aspects of providing greater access to congenital heart disease procedures to children from conflict zones. Currently, she is leading Bass Connections research titled “The Impact of Conflict on Gazan Children with Heart Disease.” Her commitment to service transcends the classroom and hospital. Driven by a genuine passion for global health, she has participated in surgical missions abroad, including performing pediatric heart surgeries for underserved communities in Guyana. While these experiences have a priceless impact on lives abroad, they also carry lessons that Dr. Boyd passes on to students as they prepare to take on the world themselves — and find their own place in it. Dr. Boyd currently serves as Director of Research for the Global Thoracic Surgery Residents’ Association. Her awards include the Academic Achievement Award for outstanding performance on in-training exams, and she has co-authored research comparing global disparities in surgical education. Dr. Boyd represents the very best of Duke's mission. – Fiza Khan, Trinity ‘27

title: Duke’s new inclusive excellence working group releases draft ‘overarching principles,’ requests community feedback
subtitle: The draft includes 10 principles that emphasize Duke's commitment to inclusivity, but stresses that all operations must be "consistent with the law." If the draft is implemented, it would be the first concrete change Duke has made in response to President Donald Trump's January executive orders demanding federally funded education institutions terminate DEI programs.
author: Zoe Kolenovsky, Claire Cranford
text: Duke administrators released a draft set of principles to guide the University’s approach to “inclusive excellence” and requested feedback from community members in a Monday email to students, faculty and staff. The draft includes 10 principles that emphasize Duke’s commitment to inclusivity — noting that “inclusion” is one of the University’s five core values — but stresses that all operations must be “consistent with the law.” The administrators noted that some of the principles “reflect existing practices and affirm ongoing approaches, while others present clarified guidance” amid a “rapidly evolving” higher education landscape. The draft constitutes the first concrete change Duke has made in response to President Donald Trump’s January executive orders demanding federally funded education institutions terminate diversity, equity and inclusion programs. The email came from 11 administrators from across the University and Health System who are members of Duke’s new inclusive excellence working group. First announced in a May 7 webinar , the working group was established in early April to guide the University’s approach to DEI efforts in response to new federal priorities. The draft appears to establish the first policy preventing University groups and programming from restricting access based on protected characteristics under the law, which include race, color, religion, sex, nationality, age, disability status and genetic information. But it maintains that “the mission of a program can focus on supporting a particular community” as long as access remains open to those outside that community. Should a group choose to restrict access, Duke “will not formally recognize nor provide tangible resources or support” to that group under the proposed principles. But the University “will not restrict” community members from “gathering informally around shared interests and identities.” The “open to all” policy would extend to admissions and the conferral of benefits, such as employment or financial aid. It would also prevent Duke resources from being used to support or promote external organizations that limit access or give preferences based on protected characteristics, except in cases where doing so is “permissible by law.” Other principles include a requirement that internal and external communications related to “inclusivity, diversity, eligibility or specific populations must affirm Duke’s commitment to open participation and inclusivity” and clarification that University departments may continue to collect the demographic information of their members, as long as such information is not used to “influence specific decisions” about conferring benefits, such as in hiring or admissions. The principles would also affirm faculty members’ right to determine what they teach in their classes — “including sensitive or controversial topics” — and would require that they encourage “an inclusive environment, consistent with [University] values.” The full list of principles is visible on the NetID-protected University Updates website, and community members can submit comments on the draft to the working group via a form through June 18. After the comment period closes, the administrators wrote that “refined principles” will be shared with the Duke community. Since January, the second Trump administration has continued attacks on higher education and DEI efforts. A February Dear Colleague letter gave universities a two-week deadline to eliminate all DEI programs or lose federal funds; billions of federal research grant money has been slashed, including millions at Duke ; and the Department of Education has launched investigations into schools — including Duke — it alleges engaged in race-exclusionary admissions practices.

title: White House proposal could add political litmus test to research grants
subtitle: The University’s vice provost for research criticized the proposal as ineffective and noted that Yale is working to provide input to the government. Professors slammed the proposal as a threat to scientific independence and life-saving research.
author: Jolynda Wang, Aria Lynn-Skov
text: A recent proposal by the White House could add a political litmus test to the federal government’s evaluation of research proposals and allocation of grants. On May 29, the White House’s Office of Management and Budget proposed a rule to federal grant regulations that would require political appointees to review grant awards and ensure they “demonstrably advance the President’s policy priorities.” The sprawling proposal, if implemented, would prohibit federal funding for projects deemed to promote “anti-American values,” as well as diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives and “gender ideology” that denies that there are two immutable sexes within humans. As written, “the proposed changes risk undoing the highly-effective, merit-based, peer-reviewed systems that have allowed researchers to develop countless breakthroughs,” University Vice Provost for Research Michael Crair wrote in an email to the News, adding that both Yale and the federal government share a commitment to transparency and efficiency in their relationship. “Yale is actively contributing to conversations and advocating for policies that strengthen the U.S. research funding model that is admired and emulated around the world and has, for many decades, produced discoveries that Americans benefit from every day,” Crair also wrote. The federal government is currently holding a 45-day comment period during which the public can review the proposed rules and submit feedback. As of June 8, the proposed rule has received 11,135 comments . Crair wrote that the Association of Public and Land-Grant Universities, the Council on Governmental Relations and the American Association of Universities are “already working to provide feedback.” Yale President Maurie McInnis sits on the board of the American Association of Universities and said in a March interview that she has collaborated with the organization to advocate for a more transparent federal research funding model in recent lobbying efforts . Three Yale professors criticized the proposal in interviews with the News, describing it as a threat to scientific independence and warning that it may slow life-changing scientific progress. “If science can only ask politically comfortable questions, it stops serving the public,” Frederick Altice, a professor of medicine and epidemiology at the Yale School of Medicine, wrote in an email to the News. He noted that requiring approval from presidential appointees could delay funding decisions and “create uncertainty for trainees, staff, and community partners.” Thomas Pollard, a now-retired Sterling professor of molecular, cellular and developmental biology, said in a Zoom interview that he expected these changes, citing President Donald Trump’s cancellation of several grants last year. “I've always said that getting an NIH grant was about as competitive as making a team in the National Football League. The money is very hard to get, and this is going to make it even more difficult to get funds to do the work,” Pollard said. Pollard said the proposal is “horrible” and “immoral,” noting that the changes could push back funding for research on developing cures and treatments. “This is a damn shame because these funds have created endless advances in technology and endless advances in clinical medicine,” Pollard said. Molecular, cellular and developmental biology professor Valerie Horsley also wrote in an email to the News that inserting political review into merit-based funding could “undermine scientific independence and slow research progress.” Horsley added that she has been funded by the NIH since she was a graduate student and that she thinks the proposal could complicate faculty recruitment and jeopardize support for graduate students. “These changes would make the process more unreliable and reduce confidence in the system,” Horsley wrote. “It would also make it harder for Universities to support research because the funding would be unpredictable.” Scott Delaney, a former research scientist at Harvard and co-creator of Grant Witness, a website that tracks federal research grant disruptions, described the proposal as an unprecedented expansion of executive control over the research funding process. He added that the changes could make it easier for future administrations to delay, deny or terminate grants to universities like Yale. “This is the biggest attack on the American scientific enterprise in my lifetime,” Delaney said in a Zoom interview. “We cannot underestimate the impact, the transformative impact that this regulation could have if it goes into effect as it’s proposed.” Continued hurdles, ‘worrisome’ delays Last month’s proposal follows a turbulent period for federal research funding at Yale under Trump’s second term. Approximately 120 grants tied to Yale researchers were affected by federal actions in 2025, including around 50 awards that were terminated and later reinstated. In March, some researchers still receiving federal funding told the News that they were facing complications due to recent policy changes, such as longer application and approval processes for grants. On April 9, the United States National Science Foundation’s Office of Award Management placed a “Future Awards to Organization on Hold” designation on Yale, Harvard, Princeton and Duke, impacting 33 research proposals associated with the universities, according to reporting by the scientific journal Nature . The day after the publication of Nature’s reporting, on May 28, the foundation removed the hold from its database for Yale, Harvard and Duke, with several grants released to Harvard and Duke following the change, Nature stated in an update to the original article. The University has not yet received formal notice from the federal government indicating that new NSF grants were placed on hold, according to Crair. However, he noted that Yale has continued to observe delays in grants being awarded. “What we have observed, and continue to monitor, is that some new grants recommended for funding through standard NSF processes, including peer review, have not been funded in the timeline we would typically expect,” he wrote in his June 4 email. “However, I am glad to report that Yale has received a number of new NSF grants in recent days.” “Allowing agencies to withhold funds, or to pick winners and losers outside of the process outlined in law, is a problematic erosion of the fundamental principle in our Constitution that Congress has the power of the purse,” Cerin Lindgrensavage ’06, a counsel for Protect Democracy, a nonpartisan nonprofit organization that advocates for stronger democracy in the United States, said in a Zoom interview. “The law is what should govern how these funds are used and not any single opinion of a bureaucrat at the OMB or NSF.” Altice wrote that the recent stalling of research proposals adds to his concern about political influence on research funding. The affected proposals have already passed independent peer review and agency-level reviews, he wrote, and they seem to have been delayed only at the award stage. “When funding is held at that stage, outside the normal scientific workflow, it raises serious questions about whether decisions are being influenced by factors other than scientific merit,” Altice wrote. “It is worrisome.” The OMB’s proposal will receive public comments on its website until July 13.

title: Proposal could bar loan borrowers from Yale music, visual arts programs
subtitle: The Education Department outlined new regulations that evaluate university programs based on alumni income, threatening federal loan eligibility for graduate programs nationwide — including at the Yale School of Music and the Yale School of Art.
author: Brett Penk, Laurentia Woo
text: A new proposal from the United States Department of Education would prohibit Yale’s graduate programs in music and visual arts from enrolling students using federal loans. Those programs fail a new test that measures the earnings of graduate degree holders four years post-commencement against the median salary of bachelor’s degree-holding working adults aged 25 to 34. Should the earnings of any degree program’s alumni fail to surpass this median twice within a three-year period, the proposed guidelines could prevent federal student loan borrowers from matriculating to that program. A summary of the proposal cites goals of shielding “students from low-earning outcomes” and protecting taxpayers. “This policy is poorly judged and inherently unfair,” James Bundy DRA ‘95, dean of the David Geffen School of Drama, wrote in a Thursday email to the News. “Cutting federal loans to graduate arts programs will disadvantage students from lower income backgrounds and favor those students with generational wealth.” According to the Department of Education’s calculations , 89.4 percent of Title IV students — those who qualify for federal financial aid programs — seeking master’s degrees in religion or religious studies would fail to meet the earnings threshold, along with 44.1 percent of those seeking fine or studio arts master’s degrees and 41.8 percent of those seeking music degrees. Laura Nasrallah, a professor of New Testament criticism and interpretation at the Yale Divinity School, suggested that monetary compensation should not define student success. “When we make a salary level or a dollar sign the only marker of value, we lose other criteria of value: satisfaction, knowing that you serve your community, joy, beauty, a deep sense of honor or fulfillment,” Nasrallah wrote in an email to the News. Bundy similarly emphasized the subjective nature of success, adding that professional paths in the arts unfold at “different rates: an actor coming out of school has very different earning prospects compared to a technical director.” He said that students “should not be penalized if they have chosen a career that may be less lucrative,” and that “uniformly excellent information” should be made available to those considering graduate study in the arts. “It would make much more sense to require graduate arts programs to publish the average indebtedness of their graduating class, and/or to require a universal standard of reporting information on living expenses in the region,” Bundy wrote. Nearly half of graduate arts programs nationwide are threatened by the new guidelines, the New York Times reported earlier this week. Those include the Juilliard School’s graduate program in music and Yale’s degree programs in music and visual arts. Yale boasts one of the nation’s leading professional music schools, recognized for the full tuition scholarships it offers to all students. The Yale School of Art has a global reputation for producing artists as influential as Chuck Close ’63 ART ’64 and Richard Serra ART ’64. While graduates from these programs often pursue careers driven by artistic and public service goals rather than high salaries, the proposed federal guidelines would evaluate them using the same criteria as every other academic discipline. Jairus Rhoades ’26 is planning to begin a master’s degree in music at Juilliard this fall after an unconventional academic journey. “My path to music — from entering Yale certain I’d be an MB&B premed, to switching into Computing and the Arts my junior year — was one of the most fulfilling reckonings of my life, even if it meant seeking significant financial support from institutions and my parents,” Rhoades wrote in a text message to the News. While Rhoades believes students considering similar career changes can still find ways to support their education, he said what “hurts” is “the knowledge that our government has actively chosen not to be one of those means.” Caroline Smoak MUS ’27 expressed worry that the policy could discourage talented artists and musicians from pursuing advanced education altogether. “As a Yale School of Music student, it deeply saddens me that only the monetary value of a specific field seems to matter,” Smoak wrote in a text message to the News. “Even in fields where the resulting income is much greater, such as in the medical field, the amount of student debt medical students acquire is outrageous, and it is insulting that the worth of any field of study is now diminished to a paycheck.” Nasrallah shared similar sentiments, acknowledging how, in addition to those in the arts, jobs in the humanities and public service are often undercompensated. “Does that mean these jobs are worth less? Is salary the only way to determine value? No,” Nasrallah wrote. The proposal has stirred much discourse around which criteria should be considered in evaluating the worth of a degree. During the 30-day period ending May 20 over which the Education Department solicited feedback on the earnings test proposal, nearly 9,000 comments were received, with many criticizing the policy. “I hope discussion about how this and the place that the arts have in American society prompts us to look at countries that treat artistic practice as a public good — not as a luxury, but as something with a genuine place in civic life,” Rhoades said. Yale has 14 graduate and professional schools.

title: Lincoln Center president Mariko Silver ’99 to join Yale Corporation
subtitle: Yale alumni elected the performing arts executive to replace Carlos Moreno ’70 as a trustee next month. Silver boasts a wide array of fundraising experience, having served at the helm of the Henry Luce Foundation and as president of Bennington College.
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Yale graduates elected performing arts executive Mariko Silver ’99 to the Yale Corporation, alumni board of governors chair Jennifer Ebisemiju Madar ’88 announced in a Monday email to voters. Silver will begin her six-year term as trustee on July 1. Silver, who has been president and CEO of the Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts since 2024, prevailed over Michael Singer ’95 GRD ’99 ’00 MED ’02 to replace outgoing trustee Carlos Moreno ’70. According to a Yale alumni fellow election brochure , Silver raised the largest annual funding total in the Lincoln Center’s history during her first year as its president. She was previously president and CEO of the Henry Luce Foundation, a grantmaking nonprofit, and was also president of Bennington College in Vermont. “We are in a moment when people are asking questions about the value of higher education and the value of publicly funded research, but I believe they’ve never been more important,” Silver said in a video included in the official election materials . The Yale Corporation, or board of trustees, includes 10 successor trustees — appointed by the current board — and six alumni fellows. Alumni fellows serve staggered six-year terms, and one is elected each spring. Moreno was elected to the role in 2020 over Maurie McInnis, who was later appointed to serve as a successor trustee from 2022 to 2024 before assuming her current role as University president. This year, eligible voters — including Yale College alumni who graduated at least five years ago and all other University graduates and honorary degree recipients — were able to vote to elect Singer or Silver until May 17. According to the elections brochure , candidates do not traditionally campaign for the alumni fellow role, standing for election “solely based on the information included in the official election materials.” Silver could not be reached for comment as of Monday evening. As president of Bennington College from 2013 to 2019, she brought in the most diverse class in the school’s history, expanded financial aid and tripled the school’s endowment to $51 million, according to Bennington’s website. In her alumni fellow candidacy video, Silver said that her interactions with faculty were core to her experience as a student, adding that the curiosity and dedication of those she encountered on campus as an undergraduate have carried her through her career and her life. “That sense of curiosity that leads to innovation, that leads to new research, that leads to new ways of thinking, and also that sense of stewardship — gathering what we know, marshalling it for good purposes and sharing it with others — all of those things are part and parcel of the Yale mission and it would be an honor to help to bring them forward,” she said. After graduating, Silver worked under the president of Arizona State University and as policy adviser to Arizona Governor Janet Napolitano. From 2009 to 2011, she served the Department of Homeland Security’s Office of International Affairs under the Obama administration. Candidates for the alumni fellow position are currently chosen by the Alumni Fellow Nominating Committee, which reviews candidates nominated by Yale graduates. Up until the 2021 election, candidates could put themselves on the ballot by gathering signatures from 3 percent of eligible voters. The decision to eliminate the petitioning process was unpopular among alumni, and Victor Ashe ’67 and Donald Glascoff Jr. ’67 filed a lawsuit challenging the legality of the change in 2022. The Connecticut Supreme Court declined to hear the lawsuit in March, after it was unsuccessfully argued in the Hartford district court and Connecticut Appellate Court. Singer, who was Silver’s opponent in this year’s alumni fellow election, is a physician-scientist and entrepreneur who serves on the Yale For Humanity Campaign Committee . In his candidacy video , Singer said the campaign serves as a reminder “that we’re a unified body in New Haven but also a global university ultimately looking to serve the world.” The For Humanity fundraising campaign — which describes itself as “guided by our mission to improve the world now and for future generations — launched in 2021 with the goal of raising $7 billion. It is set to conclude this month. In March, McInnis announced Derek Chang ’89 and Philippe Costeletos ’87 as Yale’s newest successor trustees. The two will begin in their roles alongside Silver in July. The Yale Corporation will meet next on June 6 for the final of five meetings this academic year, according to a University webpage .

title: In faculty group survey, professors report self-censorship under Trump
subtitle: A survey with 177 faculty respondents described decreased academic freedom since the beginning of President Donald Trump’s second term, according to a Tuesday report by Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors.
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Faculty members described an environment of decreasing academic freedom and instances of self-censorship under President Donald Trump’s second term in a recent report by Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. Yale AAUP released its 26-page report on Tuesday, three months after the group launched a campaign calling on Yale to add explicit guarantees of academic freedom to the faculty handbook. The Tuesday report’s recommendations additionally include increased protections for non-tenured faculty, the consideration of a University-wide faculty senate beyond advisory roles and a faculty-elected committee to hear and decide upon academic freedom complaints. “There’s been a lot of talk about the decline in public trust in higher education, but trust on campus is in jeopardy right now too,” Amy Kapczynski LAW ’03, a Yale Law School professor and member of the AAUP executive committee, said in a phone interview. “And without trust that we won’t be disciplined or harassed for our speech, we can’t offer the best education to our students or do our best research or best contribute to public debates.” The group’s survey received 177 faculty respondents from Yale College and “nearly every professional school.” According to the report, 68.4 percent of respondents indicated that their academic freedom has decreased either somewhat or a great deal since January 2025, when Trump began his second term. Yale has 5,842 faculty members, according to its website . When asked in a phone interview about the challenges of conducting the survey, Daniel HoSang, an American Studies professor and the president of Yale’s AAUP chapter, cited “apprehension among faculty” to share their experiences and a lack of access to a centralized list of faculty. 56.3 percent of respondents also reported being “extremely concerned” or “somewhat concerned” that “students might file anonymous complaints about them,” while 44.6 percent reported concerns about being “disciplined by the university for their public-facing speech and engagement.” The survey also asked respondents about actions — related to teaching, research and public engagement — they may have taken since January 2025 “in response to the Trump administration’s higher education directives and actions and in light of the general climate of attacks on higher education.” 32.2 percent of respondents said they “avoided controversial topics in class or lectures,” and 47.5 percent said they “stopped or avoided posting on social media about controversial topics,” per the report. “The conditions in higher ed have really shifted in the last couple of years, and this is important evidence about how faculty are reacting at one institution,” HoSang said of the report. Since Trump’s inauguration, Yale has avoided direct funding cuts from the federal government that peer institutions, including Harvard and Columbia, have faced. In meetings with professors last spring, Yale President Maurie McInnis indicated that she was refraining from public commentary to avoid the federal government’s targeting, according to professors who attended the meetings. McInnis never confirmed these discussions, citing the private nature of meetings. In October 2024, a month before Trump was elected to his second term, McInnis adopted the recommendations of the Committee on Institutional Voice that University leaders refrain from making public statements on significant issues, except in rare cases. Kapczynski said that the audience for the University’s “lay-low strategy” seems to be the Trump administration and those outside of Yale, adding that when universities neither comment publicly on what’s happening in their “broader environment” nor take action to clarify their “commitment to academic freedom,” it “speaks volumes.” “The reality is that action is important in this context because it’s what conveys the sincerity of our commitment to academic freedom,” she said. Tuesday’s report also reads that “While some reporting has taken the view that Yale has successfully defended the institution and its finances from the present administration, our data show that all is not well at Yale.” In March, the office of Provost Scott Strobel established the Committee on the Principles of Academic Freedom, which is set to release a statement that “articulates the principles that guide the academic freedom of Yale faculty,” according to the Provost’s website . Strobel did not respond to questions about whether the committee’s formation was a reaction to the AAUP chapter’s advocacy when asked by the News in March. When asked if the University plans to address the concerns raised and changes recommended in the report, Yale spokesperson Tina Posterli pointed to the existing language about academic freedom in the faculty handbook, but did not directly address the AAUP chapter’s report. “Academic freedom is a crucial, central element of Yale’s purpose and mission. This tenet is explicitly recognized in Yale’s Faculty Handbook, which states that ‘academic freedom and the spirit of free inquiry are fundamental to Yale’s mission,’ and includes procedural safeguards for faculty who believe their academic freedom has been improperly restrained,” Posterli wrote in an email to the News. She also emphasized the work of the Provost’s Committee on the Principles of Academic Freedom, adding that the “committee has begun its work, including distribution of a survey on academic freedom to all members of the faculty and others in teaching and research,” and that it will produce a report by the end of the fall semester. HoSang said that the faculty handbook is updated annually “at the Provost’s discretion” and that introducing additional language into the faculty handbook is costless, can be “easily done” over the summer by the Strobel and McInnis and would not override the authority of the Provost’s committee on academic freedom “because that’s focused on what the statement should be.” He also pointed to the speed at which Yale narrowed its mission statement following a recommendation by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education — two weeks after the committee released its report. “When President McInnis wanted to change the mission statement, we took note of how quickly it was done,” HoSang said. “She felt there was urgency, there wasn’t a big campus-wide review, there was a recommendation from one committee, but no other campus constituents were engaged, and it was done.” “We would hope that they could act on this with the same urgency that President McInnis acted on changing the mission statement at her discretion,” HoSang said. “We assume that they’ll take seriously that many dozens of their colleagues, their faculties as well, have reported that they don’t feel like they can teach and research freely, and that they’ll want to address that problem.” The AAUP sent McInnis and Strobel the report once it was released but has not yet met with members of the administration since the report’s release, according to HoSang. “We’re looking forward to their response, given the widespread endorsement of the campus,” HoSang said, referring to the student support the AAUP’s recent campaign for academic freedom has received. Last month, the Yale College Council Senate and the Graduate and Professional Student Senate passed a joint resolution backing Yale AAUP’s academic freedom demands. Following the Tuesday release of Yale AAUP’s report, leaders from both student organizations emphasized the importance of protecting academic freedom. “Academic freedom is fragile,” Sam Haddad LAW ’26, last year’s president of the Graduate and Professional School Student Senate, wrote in an email to the News. “The Federal Administration has proved that over the past few months. If our university’s focus is truly on the production and dissemination of knowledge, then Administrators must act on this report’s concerning findings.” YCC President Alex William Chen ’28 wrote in an email to the News that the report “emphasizes the unmistakable need for students and faculty to jointly advocate for our shared interests: students can’t learn if teachers don’t feel free to teach.” “The Yale education students deserve is achieved through, and only through, critically engaging with an unfettered range of teaching and thought --- academic self-censorship robs students of exactly that,” he wrote. Yale’s chapter of the AAUP was established in November 2024.

title: Yale fills four out of six open residential college dean positions
subtitle: Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis announced on Tuesday the new deans of Berkeley, Davenport, Silliman and Timothy Dwight Colleges. The appointments follow a string of departure announcements in the past semester.
author: Jolynda Wang, Sarah Rivas
text: Yale College announced new appointments to four out of six open residential college dean positions.
