While often associated with the ways in which nations wage wars or governments enact policies, the term can refer to any action which requires a strategy to be achieved: athletic endeavors, job opportunities, romantic prospects. The best definition I have devised is to equate it to observing a round hole and attempting to find the square peg that best fits it. The principles one applies when conjoining limited means with limitless aspirations will never be perfect, but some may be more useful than others. And strategically, it is often wiser to make something less bad than to strive — and fail — to make something more perfect. Too often, however, discussions of grand strategy fall short of their grand implications and deal exclusively with matters of minute importance. Nowhere is this more true than at Yale, where the pursuit of perfection — rather than improvement — defines so much of what constitutes strategic success. Many of us have fallen prey to the belief that excellence might be achieved through the acquisition of wealth, influence, honor and acclaim. We see it in pre-professional clubs, where lucrative careers prove more enticing than personal fulfillment; in social settings, where peers may judge each other based on employment status rather than content of character; even in classrooms, where the rigid hierarchy of intelligence often prevails over the beautiful struggle of education . During my time in Yale’s Brady-Johnson Program in Grand Strategy, such academic sentiments were on full display. I learned much from my professors and colleagues, acquiring countless square pegs I will use later in life to fill countless round holes. But our class sought only to uncover whether a strategy had succeeded or failed in achieving its defined, material ends. Questions of moral strategy — how one might formulate ethical goals and the means to attain them — remained underexamined. It may be appropriate to exclude moral discussions from class if the goal is strictly to establish causal relationships between actions and events. But what happens when a strategy demands moral compromise? What happens when the desire for good ends attempts to justify the use of nefarious means? What happens when one believes that power is the only thing necessary to achieve change? Jesus was, by all accounts, a grand strategist. He recognized both his limitless aspirations — man’s salvation — and his limited means — man’s proclivity to sin — by which he might attain them. Yet, in doing so, he did not commit to obey the laws of power. He did not need to read the Melian Dialogue and Machiavelli to implement his vision. His was the strategy of success through failure: a necessary death for a perpetual resurrection. His was the strategy of complete commitment to leading a moral existence. His was the strategy not of this life, but of the next. Every student of grand strategy struggles to comprehend strategic lessons from the figure of Jesus. This is likely due to the fact that, despite its implicit “grandness,” grand strategy is actually quite limited in its scope of application. So many strategists seek to meet temporal means with temporal ends — victory in an election, preservation of an empire — that, when confronted with a truly grand strategy like that of Jesus, they suddenly recognize the smallness of their aims. Many graduates may experience a similar belittling at commencement. Students work tirelessly for years at their grand strategies of success, only to discover themselves as one drop in a sea of hats and gowns. Things previously considered important appear inconsequential when observed at a distance. Success amounts to no more than a handshake, a sheet of paper and a photo-op. Thus the importance of reminding ourselves that the grandest strategy of all is not how to succeed in life, but how to succeed in death. Whatever our temporal ends may be, our ultimate end remains the soft reprieve of the tomb; every strategy, therefore, must lead us to where we will be when we die, and whether or not our means of arriving there were just. Jesus’ story, religious professions aside, serves as perhaps the most useful square peg to be placed in the round hole of life. It is certainly the one which I consider to be remembered the least, and needed the most, at our university. As I close my Yale career, my final advice for all can be summarized no better than to follow the prescription of W. H. Auden: “Read The New Yorker, trust in God; and take short views.” WILLIAM BARBEE is a senior in Silliman College studying History and a staff columnist for the News. Contact him at william.barbee@yale.edu .

title: Leaving our legacy
author: Andrew Lipka
text: Last year, President McInnis chartered the Committee on Trust in Higher Education. The committee’s capstone report has arrived, with accompanying headlines . Thirty-two pages with statements of principles, diagnoses and 20 recommendations contain much that needed to be said. One can hope that the best ideas will receive the attention they deserve and find their way into implementation. But here’s the thing: Media headlines about the report have avoided its best parts, and many instead screamed a consistent meme: Legacy admissions must go. Bloomberg led with, “Yale Report Backs Curbing Legacy Admissions,” The Washington Post blared, “Yale considers changes to admissions” and Fortune echoed, “Admissions Process Questioned.” To be sure, other topics came up — notably including tuition cost — but legacy admissions was at the front, again and again. This is doubly problematic. The committee’s worthy primary theme, the primacy of academic excellence, got buried. Meanwhile, the report perpetrates an unjustified, unreasoned and frankly contradictory attack on legacy preference. In fact, the report mentions legacy only twice, principally through its recommendation that “Yale reduce preferences for special classes of applicants.” Why? It “distorts the admissions process.” In an 18-page bibliography, only one source is cited for this proposition. I applaud the committee’s choice to champion academic excellence. They even brought this same ethic to admissions when they asserted, “At Yale, the primacy of academic criteria should be non-negotiable.” They continued: “If the university claims that academic excellence is its mission, then its admissions process must visibly reflect that claim.” But much of what the public reads is instead the committee’s call — made essentially in passing — to eliminate legacy preference. This is damaging. And it is wrong. The committee was careless in throwing legacy preference to the dogs. By drowning out its real message, it was self-defeating . It should have known that the media would latch onto this. Legacy preference has long been a sexy topic. The public grossly exaggerates its extent and its degree. It is an easy target; it smacks of self-dealing, corruption and unfairness. But the committee offered no evidence to make that case. Its members should have known that this would drown out the good work they were trying to do. The public misunderstanding is in part due to the frequent mischaracterization of legacy preference at Yale. In truth, it is infrequent, mild and decreasing. Only 11 percent of the class of 2029 were alumni children; over the past 20 years, this number has decreased consistently. It basically takes the form of a tiebreaker when applicants are otherwise equal. The substance of the recommendation to eliminate legacy preference flies in the face of the committee’s own standards — this is the heart of the matter. The committee stresses that Yale is first and foremost a school, so admissions processes should prioritize academics. Legacy preferences do just this; the mere presence of a higher acceptance rate among legacies is not proof of “distortion.” Consider these crucial facts, cited by a Yale luminary: “The pool of legacy applicants is substantially stronger [academically] than ... the rest of the pool ... [and admitted legacies’] grades and test scores are higher than the average of the rest of the admitted class.” Given this, it makes sense that a higher admit rate prevails among legacy applicants. Furthermore, it belies the committee’s conclusory statement that legacy preference “distorts the admissions process.” And crucially, “The legacies that matriculate achieve higher grades at Yale than non-legacy students with the same high school grades and test scores.” This remarkable fact affirms the utter consistency of legacy preferences with the committee’s academic emphasis. A legacy preference, used as a tiebreaker, results in better Yale academic performance. To not employ this preference would be to vote for poorer academic performance — the opposite of the committee’s priority. The source for these facts is impeccable: a 2004 interview with then-Yale President Richard Levin GRD ’72 ’74. While these are somewhat old statistics, Dean of Undergraduate Admissions Jeremiah Quinlan ’03 has in recent years reiterated most of these claims and contradicted none. The committee recognized that it can be difficult to assess academic promise. Still, it criticized Yale’s admissions process, specifically the 1967 letter from then-President Kingman Brewster Jr. ’41, which set forth admission policies, as imprecise. But there is nothing unclear about the legacy preference process the letter prescribed: “Tradition, loyalty, familiarity deserve to be given weight if — but only if — the candidate has survived initial comparison and appears to be as worthy of the opportunity as any of the others being finally considered.” Just right. And what of “tradition, loyalty, and familiarity?” Do these have no value? In ignoring the virtues of legacy preference, the committee diminishes the vital assets the preference — and alumni in general — bring to Yale: institutional character, continuity and community. I love Yale; I am a Yale patriot. I love Yale traditions, in part for their own sake, but largely because they remind me that I am part of an intergenerational project that is passed on through ritual, through archive, through practice — and through Yale families. If my Yale education is valuable beyond merely earning power, if I am strengthened in character and commitment, it will imbue crucial areas of my life, which surely include parenting. If my children merit admission and attend Yale, they will bring this Yale ethos to campus, enhancing the continuity of the deep, ineffable goodness of the place, through the intimate corners of those Yale years. I am privileged to lead a community of lifelong learners, now known as EverScholar, but previously termed “Yale for Life.” EverScholar programs reconvene the Yale academic experience for alumni through the seminar classroom and campus-like milieu. One EverScholar alumnus is John Boardman ’64, a ninth-generation Yale student. John’s life is one of service and memory. To hear him is to learn of the greatness — and the flaws — of Yale’s past from one raised on them, who lived by them. This is priceless. John is — for us — an “alumni child,” and we are better for John. How could one replace this? The committee’s report scarcely recognizes Yale alumni’s existence, much less their role in the place’s fabric. Little wonder, then, that it so easily discards legacy preference. But this breaks trust with alumni, not a trust that we are “owed” something but a promise that we will be visible, valued and considered fairly, not dismissively. We trust in what Yale stands for. Its motto says it stands for truth. Its mission says it stands for knowledge. The committee says it stands for academic excellence. These are all the same promise. The committee did many things to honor that promise, and in that sense, it has acted to preserve that trust. But this piece has shown that legacy preference promotes the very values the committee champions. More than this, however, the preference crucially recognizes — and facilitates — the integral nature of alumni in the fabric and continuity of the institution. It makes Yale better and stronger. In its modest but vital current form, it is ethical and effective. To wrongfully diminish it would be not only an act of pandering; it would deny the value of alumni and of Yale’s past. This would violate, rather than confirm, trust. Earning trust can take the form of admitting mistakes when they are made. I call upon the committee to correct this error and to thus be worthy of the trust they seek. No doubt many may disagree, including many of my alumni brethren. However, if I may speak of Yale for the 170,000 Yale alumni for a moment, in the words of Daniel Webster in the case of Dartmouth College: “It is, Sir, as I have said, a small college. And yet, there are those who love it.” Do not diminish it in this way. ANDREW LIPKA is the President of the nonprofit EverScholar. He co-hosts the weekly podcast “Amarica’s Constitution” with Akhil Amar ‘80 LAW ‘84 and is Director of the Yale Alumni Schools Committee in central New Jersey. He graduated from Yale College in 1978 and from the Albert Einstein College of Medicine in 1982. He is a fellow of Jonathan Edwards College. He can be reached at andrew.lipka@gmail.com .

title: Noble ends in need of proper means
author: Ari Shtein
text: A couple of weeks ago, I wrote that “ Yale is finally headed in the right direction .” I felt that our administration had truly — finally — recommitted itself to the politically neutral pursuit of truth. And I thought it was the best we could get, that I could be no happier. Then, just two days later, the Committee on Trust in Higher Education proved me wrong . Their report can be divided more or less neatly into three parts: diagnosis, aims and methods. I endorse wholeheartedly the first two of these. Yet I worry that the Trust Committee’s goals will be undermined by its too-gentle methods for achieving them. The report’s authors are right that Yale has become expensive, out-of-touch and — as one Trust Committee guest speaker , Musa al-Gharbi, put it — just plain weird. So what should we do? Refocus! Make Yale better at being Yale — a university that “creates, disseminates, and preserves knowledge through research and teaching,” not a taxpayer-funded nonprofit devoted to diversity, leadership and justice. Diversity, leadership and justice are good things, no doubt — but they’re not what tuition dollars, nor federal research dollars, nor property tax-exempt dollars are supposed to be paying for. To earn the public’s trust, the report’s authors correctly note that we must first have “clarity, not diffusion, of purpose.” They go on to list several ways that our clarified purpose can be better served, ranging from an admissions overhaul to a new civics requirement for first-year students. These are ambitious and concrete recommendations. If followed, they would make Yale a shining example of what the elite American university can be: a nimble fusion of the liberal arts and the sciences where students are prepared to produce cutting-edge research, enter top law schools, practice medicine, write books, run for office — all with an eye still to the traditions and literatures that form the foundation of our civilization. As far as intellectual diversity and academic freedom go, however, the authors’ recommendations are more reserved. They write that “each department and school should engage in a self-study examining the breadth of its intellectual and methodological commitments; the range of scholarly approaches represented on its faculty; the diversity of perspectives in its curriculum; and the openness of its hiring and admissions practices to dissenting or underrepresented traditions.” A mere self-study! Is that really all that’s warranted? The authors throw in some other soft suggestions. Perhaps professors could teach alongside visiting scholars with opposing views. Maybe the Yale Center for Civic Thought could take on a broader mandate, more like that of Princeton’s James Madison Program . But all this fails to take seriously the urgency, scale and persistence of the problem. For nearly a decade now, ideologically and racially motivated hiring practices have been the norm, both at Yale and throughout elite higher education. A December report in Compact magazine made clear that departmental hiring committees have explicitly biased themselves against white men and against academics who study anything “that’s white and European and male and dead.” At the University of California, Berkeley, a 55-page report was prepared instructing search committees on how they could legally subvert the meritocracy in pursuit of left-wing political priorities. And between 2015 and 2023, the share of white men hired to tenure-track jobs in the humanities at Berkeley fell from 53 percent to 22 percent. At Yale, since 2018, tenure-track humanities hires have been just 8 percent white and male. I am not advocating the establishment of quotas for white male representation. That would be bad. What I am saying instead is that we have strong evidence showing that faculty hiring committees make biased and politically motivated decisions constantly, decisions which result in the overwhelming ideological orthodoxy we see on campus. Further, given the structure of academic leadership — the administrators of today were professors 10, 20, 30 years ago — if these practices do not change immediately, all the work done to improve Yale by administrators now will be undone by administrators 10, 20, 30 years hence. The situation calls out not for “self-study,” but for external scrutiny or even formal investigation. If the aims of the Trust Committee’s report are to be achieved and maintained long-term, it will be necessary to increase departmental oversight in the short term. The Yale administration should critically review the hiring practices of each department and ensure they are unflinchingly meritocratic. Departments and roles that should not exist, that produce research without social value, that function primarily as A-farms for students and soapboxes for ideologues with doctorates, should be identified and cut. Just as an open society must refuse to tolerate intolerance or risk its eventual dissolution, a new academically free Yale cannot tolerate ideologically conformist departments. Biased hiring practices are a ticking time bomb which threatens to undo all that might be accomplished today. That bomb must be defused, or the Trust Committee will ultimately prove a failure. ARI SHTEIN is a first year in Saybrook College. His column mostly consists of the airing of grievances. He can be reached at ari.shtein@yale.edu .

title: Is teaching Yale’s top priority?
author: Daniel Martinez HoSang, Alessandro Gomez
text: The recent report by Yale’s Committee on Trust in Higher Education calls on the University to “re-center the classroom,” reminding us that colleges and universities may be among the last places where it is possible to slow down, think carefully, and cultivate sustained intellectual engagement. That is precisely why the wave of instructional faculty firings across the Faculty of Arts and Sciences is so troubling. Universities cannot credibly speak of rebuilding public trust while eroding trust within the classrooms that shape students’ educational experience. As recent reporting in the Yale Daily News has shown, several instructors have been informed that their appointments will not be renewed. Many of these faculty members teach well-enrolled courses that are central to the undergraduate curriculum. Yet they have been let go late in the year and with little transparency. Instructional faculty are, in the words of the 2023 report of the FAS Instructional Faculty Working Group, “indispensable to the achievement of Yale’s educational mission.” They lead small seminars, guide intensive writing projects and provide the detailed feedback and mentorship that are central to a Yale undergraduate education. This fall, incoming first-year students will have 18 fewer sections of Yale’s introductory writing seminars. Programs such as Film and Media Studies and performance studies are facing significant cuts. These are not marginal offerings. They are core components of how Yale students learn to think, write, and engage the world. The consequences of these decisions will be felt immediately by students in reduced course offerings, fewer mentors and increased class sizes. But the cuts may also impact the quality of teaching. In recent years, Yale has invested in high quality teaching through improving the job security of its instructional faculty by offering more stability, resources and flexible appointment structures. These cuts undermine this progress. Recent surveys by both the FAS-SEAS Senate and Yale’s American Association of University Professors chapter found that many instructional and research faculty feel unable to speak openly about these changes, fearing professional repercussions. This climate of uncertainty and silence is corrosive. When instructors are anxious about their livelihoods and excluded from decision-making, they have few incentives to encourage difficult conversations in the classroom. The quality of teaching and mentorship inevitably suffers. Our teaching conditions are our students’ learning conditions. University administrators have pointed to financial pressures, including the impending federal endowment tax increase, as justification for these measures. These are real challenges. Yale has already taken steps to address them, including a hiring pause, retirement incentives, and delays in capital projects. But what has been notably absent is any consideration of shared sacrifice from the top tiers of administration. Today, as universities across the country are facing fiscal challenges from attacks on higher education, leaders at Harvard , Brown and Northwestern have embraced the principle of shared sacrifice. These universities and Princeton have implemented senior faculty and administrator salary freezes alongside other budget cutting measures. These measures signal a collective commitment to protecting core academic functions and maintaining trust across the institution. Yale, by contrast, has not announced any measures to cut or freeze the salaries of upper administrators in the spirit of shared sacrifice. Thus, the burden of budget cuts appears to be falling disproportionately on instructional faculty, many of whom are among the lowest-paid members of the academic workforce. This approach is difficult to justify in light of the university’s self-reported financial position. Yale ended the last fiscal year with a $347 million operating surplus. Its endowment grew by nearly $3 billion, generating an estimated $150 million in additional annual payout capacity. It just concluded a successful $7 billion fundraising campaign “For Humanity.” The university also maintains billions in liquid assets and continues to invest heavily in new initiatives, including a $150 million multi-year commitment to artificial intelligence. What are Yale’s institutional priorities? It is hard to reconcile the call to “re-center the classroom” with decisions that reduce the number of instructors teaching the courses that anchor undergraduate education. The lack of clarity surrounding these non-renewals only deepens the concern, undermining the trust university leaders say they want to build. Administrators have suggested that the cuts respond to “ urgent teaching needs ,” yet have also indicated that course enrollment and student demand were not decisive factors. Vague and shifting criteria in contract decisions undermine confidence in the process and suggest a troubling indifference to both faculty expertise and student experience. The Committee on Trust warns that universities are often perceived as “self-regarding, insular, and unreceptive to legitimate criticism.” This moment of financial challenge offers an opportunity to prove otherwise. Yale’s leadership can still revisit these decisions. It can provide targeted resources to departments to sustain critical courses and the instructors who teach them. It can pursue strategies of shared sacrifice that align financial stewardship with institutional values. Most importantly, it can affirm that the classroom is not simply a rhetorical priority but a real one. If Yale is serious about rebuilding trust, it must value the people and practices that sustain the undergraduate education by which it distinguishes itself. DANIEL MARTINEZ HOSANG is professor of American studies and political science. He sits on the executive committee for Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. He can be reached at daniel.hosang@yale.edu . ALESSANDRO GOMEZ is a professor of engineering in the School of Engineering and Applied Science. He sits on the executive committee for Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. He can be reached at alessandro.gomez@yale.edu .

title: The classroom is not the problem
author: Brunella Tipismana
text: Last Wednesday — an hour before the Yale Committee on Trust in Higher Education released its 58-page report calling for sweeping reforms — my history class discussed an essay written in Berlin back in 1807, during an earlier crisis in higher education. At the time, the university seemed like a pitiful relic, threatened by political crisis and the perceived decadence of national elites. In response, the Prussian government asked public servants and intellectuals to come up with solutions to academia’s existential crisis. One response came from philosopher Johann Gottlieb Fichte, who submitted a proposal for a new university in Berlin — and, it turns out, a foundational manifesto for what became the first modern research university. This school would shed the academy’s medieval habits. It would raise scholars committed to the public sphere. It would also be a refuge. Insulated from an increasingly commercial world and uncompromised by crude professionalism, young people would cultivate a new art of learning. This could only happen under specific material conditions, Fichte wrote: The university needed “to cover [students’] current expenses and provide a guarantee of appropriate support in the future.” To do otherwise was to subordinate the pursuit of knowledge to the desire for profit — and, in the process, to ensure that students learned nothing. As we discussed these passages in class, we all burst out laughing. Yes, we acknowledged one after another: Preprofessional culture — pointless coffee chats, club meetings , evenings spent tinkering with Workday applications — cannibalizes the university’s academic core. But it also seems impossible to graduate into a semi-secure future without giving in to it. Indeed, I can only sit down to write this piece because I am no longer sinking 30 hours a week applying to internships and other resume-stuffers. To be clear, I didn’t spend my sophomore and junior years like this for fun. I would have much preferred to spend that time poring over essays or doing the optional readings. No: I did it because I am a low-income student, and I knew I’d have to care for myself and my family soon after graduation. And while this preprofessional emphasis did get me a job, it also seems obvious to me that it was the single most destructive force in my undergraduate education. So, as I read the committee’s report after class, I was surprised to see only a passing mention of this side of student life. It calls on Yale to focus on its austerely rewritten goal, “to preserve, create, and share knowledge,” through measures like a no-device policy — a good idea — and grade deflation — a more controversial one. But in its attempt to “re-center the classroom,” the committee misses what pushed it aside in the first place: not the students, but the conditions they were embedded in and the choices their university made in response. So I believe, as Fichte did, that nothing would bring classrooms back to the fore more effectively than a reimagining of what Yale owes its students. For current undergraduates, the Buckley Institute arranges internships at the National Review and the Manhattan Institute, and the Block Family Foundation funds highly competitive fellowships in local journalism. Yale should radically expand post-graduate support for similar public service initiatives. Freed from spending the years before graduation chasing the spring program that leads to the internship that leads to the job offer, students could “re-center the classroom” secure in the knowledge that a cushion of time explicitly devoted to kickstarting their careers awaits them after graduation. As the report rightly acknowledges, the administration must also steer students’ considerable privileges — inherited from their families or acquired here upon admission — toward braver pursuits than editing PowerPoints for health insurers. Consulting and finance have excelled at selling themselves as the natural next step for any competitive undergraduate; Yale must use its resources just as directly to raise the status of public service careers, at least within its own walls. My proposals might seem obvious, yet the University’s approach to its current budget shortfall suggests otherwise. In addition to shrinking summer activity funds and low-income student stipends , the administration has cut the contracts of contingent faculty, the instructors most directly tasked with supporting our education. For Yale to implement this report as it erodes the tangible foundations of intellectual life is like giving a blood transfusion to a patient whose wounds haven’t been stitched shut: You’ll end up with a corpse anyway. To be fair, the committee’s report does nod at expanding public service fellowships. And because responsibility for recent cuts lies with the Office of the Provost and the Board of Trustees, it also rightly calls for a stronger faculty voice in university governance. It must ask for more. To focus on academic excellence means putting pressure not just on students’ grades, but on an unaccountable administration often more concerned with cultivating donors than knowledge. It might also mean contending with the failure of the university when run as a corporation. To restore public trust, it isn’t enough to simply strike out the word “diversity” from our mission statement and make vague allusions to intellectual renewal. Let us remember that the choice between those faddish changes and the real material conditions needed for the cultivation of thought is not new. In fact, it’s the same choice Fichte wrote about in 1807: Today, the university “must justify itself, or perish.” BRUNELLA TIPISMANA is a senior in Saybrook College studying History. She can be reached at karinabrunella.tipismanaurbano@yale.edu

title: Why so scared to talk Black?
author: Jean-Claude Pierre III
text: It is frustrating that three years since the end of race-conscious admissions, one year after the first-ever election of an all-Black Yale College Council ticket and in the midst of an intense anti-DEI campaign against universities across the nation, there is little to no journalism that explicitly and substantively regards race on this campus. This is troublesome because the stakes of comprehensiveness in our journalism are high, especially in a democratic society. The dissemination of information vitalizes our perception of the world, in turn informing our actions within it. Race-blind journalism builds limited perceptions, which — often, unknowingly — motivate the construction of flawed realities. This is problematic in the context of any race, but I’d like to focus on the need for change specifically in relation to blackness at Yale. First and foremost, we need better representation of Black journalists within our publications. While it may seem at a glance that the Yale Daily News’ racial demographics are generally reflective of that of broader Yale, representation dwindles in more influential positions. In the past 20 years, the News has had 65 managing editors and editors in chief, including in the incoming board. Four have been Black; none of the four have been editor in chief. I wouldn’t be surprised if this trend is reflected in most of Yale journalism. Throughout American history, Black journalists have played an important role in intervening on prevailing narratives within American discourse. Ida B. Wells pioneered data-driven investigative journalism to disprove misleading portrayals of lynching. It was the Black-owned Jet magazine that spread the tragic photo of Emmett Till and lit an urgent fire within the Civil Rights Movement. Ta-Nehisi Coates has repeatedly countered comfortable distortions of America’s racial reality that white liberal journalists so easily fall into. The reason for this is simple: The uniqueness of the Black experience leverages Black journalists to have different perspectives on key issues. This is most true while directly reporting on race — white journalists have often been allowed to take for granted what have been matters of life and death for Black journalists, instantiating a strong bias — but it remains true for all topics of discussion, primarily because race is so fundamental to our society that it affects every realm of our existence. This is not to say that a Black food journalist needs to infuse their racial experience into their reportage on, say, the taste of a meal, but rather that their lived experience has been significantly affected by their race in one way or another, and that lived experience often will affect how they taste and interact with said meal. Where it often fails to be monolithic, the Black experience on Yale campus remains undeniably unique. Whether it be from The House’s Black oasis or knee-deep in the whiteness of Sigma Nu-turned-Knox Club, we as Black Yalies see this campus differently, and that matters — it is extremely consequential to the type of journalism we can and want to produce. But even more importantly, there is a need for what I call Black journalism. By this, I mean journalism — by journalists of all races — that concerns itself with the intricacies of Black life on this campus. This can be applied to Yale-wide topics: What were attitudes about race in regards to the YCC election of Andrew Boanoh ’27 and Jalen Bradley ’27? We love talking about Yale dating, so how is it affected by race? Or this can be focused within Yale’s Black community: Did the affirmative action ruling have an effect on the Black community that was not captured by the demographic percentage? How do Black people feel about the Black community here at Yale? While some pieces have discussed race , Yale publications have not approached these questions with the nuance and depth they deserve — to ask beyond the “what” of the matter, and inquire at length about “why” and “how” things are the way they are. These types of questions are not just a means to an end of more comprehensive journalism. Journalism that asks these questions has a pragmatic benefit to campus life. We are in desperate need of a more robust archive of Black life on this campus. There are repeated conversations of issues of all types we wish we could improve upon, but once we learn enough about Yale life to have any sort of solution, we are on our way out and too old, jaded, busy and tired to do much about it. Black journalism highlights and archives meaningful discussions about problems and solutions relevant to the Black experience here at Yale. Data collection, wide-ranging interviews and well thought-out reportage and analysis are far more helpful to present and future Yalies than the insular dialogue we currently rely on. Thus, Black journalists and Black journalism are both critically important to our Yale community. They contribute to our journalism by building new knowledge and new pathways to action. With better information, it becomes easier to rally, easier to make change — the type of change that makes Yale a better place. This isn’t new. It’s what my ancestors have been doing in America, in Haiti and around the world for centuries. And they’ve been able to do it because they and the world were aware of their race. JEAN-CLAUDE PIERRE III is a sophomore in Berkeley College studying American Studies. He can be reached at jean-claude.pierre@yale.edu .

title: Yale still doesn't get it
author: Jack Clawson
text: The Committee on Trust in High Education’s 58-page report is nothing if not thorough. It makes 20 recommendations and cites hundreds of sources. But the committee missed an obvious problem. Yale became a prominent university because of the success and strength of its alumni. Our public trust and legitimacy are directly correlated with what those alumni do with their Yale education. So I was astounded to find that in 58 pages on trust in higher education, alumni were only mentioned about a dozen times. Furthermore, alumni were almost always mentioned as donors or trustees, never as the actual output of the institution and therefore a critical source of legitimacy. While I respect the committee’s effort, academics often overcomplicate or outright ignore simple truths. Have we considered that the first step to rebuilding trust in Yale is making sure our alumni aren’t inflicting massive pain on our country? It is a fact that Yalies are fleecing America. The Wall Street Journal reported that nearly 30 percent of Yale graduates are funneled into finance or consulting. The report briefly acknowledges this, writing that selective universities reinforce this “culture by allowing recruitment from finance, law, and consulting firms as early as a student’s first year, before a single round of classes has been completed.” The conversation ends there. This brief remark is treated as a byproduct of declining classroom rigor rather than a central fact of our campus culture and a crisis of legitimacy. Americans have watched, decade after decade, elite schools like this one shepherd our nation’s best and brightest into industries that actively extract value from the public. Alumni of this school were architects of the Iraq War, the Vietnam War and the 2008 financial crisis . The American people are not confused when they distrust Yale. They are simply beginning to see Yale for what it is. Obviously, the University can’t police the careers and private lives of its alumni. So there is certainly no way this problem can be addressed, right? Wrong. Start with admissions. Each time a student admitted based on an interest in humanitarian work becomes a McKinsey consultant junior year, the admissions office has failed. To avoid this common pitfall, the admissions office could connect admitted students before they reach campus with clubs and communities on campus that match the moral aspirations they wrote about in their essays. We need to give students the resources to actually accomplish their lofty moral ambitions. Thirty percent of the class of 2030 probably did not answer “Why Yale?” by stating their desire to work 80-hour weeks in high finance. Next, examine our career infrastructure. The Office of Career Strategy should devote substantial time and energy into recruiting for careers like public service, teaching or journalism. Seemingly, first-year students are more likely to have opportunities to interview with consulting firms than local nonprofits. This ought to change. Finally, look at who Yale celebrates. When people think of Yalies, they think of a Silicon Valley tech bro creating AI solutions to nonexistent problems. They envision a private equity executive who bought his hometown’s only grocery store and overleveraged it within a year. They picture an establishment politician who has spent years dodging accountability, likely for his time spent on a certain island. Instead, let’s highlight the alumni that are doing meaningful work and leading change. Yale’s communications apparatus could prioritize the graduates doing work aligned with the mission of “improving the world today and for future generations,” rather than the ones who are simply rich, powerful and prestigious. None of this violates institutional neutrality. Yale does not have to denounce Vice President JD Vance LAW ’13. It does not have to denounce any particular alumnus or career. It only has to stop crafting and celebrating a pipeline that contradicts our mission to serve the public. Shockingly, the first step toward rebuilding trust may be actually doing good for society. The report writes that “trust is earned by doing what you say you’re going to do—and, ideally, doing it well.” Yale says it is educating future leaders who will serve the public. The public has looked at said leaders and, rightfully, drawn conclusions about the trust they place in our institution and higher education. Now we have to respond. JACK CLAWSON is a first year in Grace Hopper College studying Ethics, Politics and Economics. He can be reached at jack.clawson@yale.edu .

title: Letter: Women’s Center puts politics above women
author: Letters To The Editor
text: Re: “ Standing our ground at the Women’s Center ” A step inside the Women’s Center reveals it is only a safe space for some women. Surrounding the comfy couches and table with arts supplies are a plethora of posters that would seem more at home at a political protest. With slogans such as “Free Palestine” and “Books Not Bombs,” their relevance to women is dubious. Moreover, their role in an organization that historically receives both space and funding from Yale University should be scrutinized. I entered the Women’s Center for the first time this week. As I looked around the room, I considered the paradox quite literally written on the walls. Here was an organization created to protect all women from discrimination, with political rhetoric that blatantly excluded many of them. Yet, according to its Instagram link, the Women’s Center purports to be “working across identities towards collective liberation.” Which identities? And whose liberation? If any campus organization should be capable of recognizing that women are not a homogenous group, it is the Women’s Center. Yet, with its overt political leanings, the Women’s Center pushes away Yale students who otherwise may have sought its support. Recently, the Women’s Center faced the choice of whether to be absorbed into the Dean’s Office as a politically neutral cultural center or to become a student organization free to engage in political activities. The center chose to abandon the idea of neutrality — and any intentions to accommodate all women – reflecting its true nature as a political group. This is not to say that the Women’s Center should forgo discussions regarding women’s issues and politics — far from it. However, rather than tacitly agreeing on a singular correct narrative for today’s most controversial issues, the Women’s Center leadership should invite students with a range of beliefs on a subject, for instance abortion, to gather and debate it. There are ways to encourage political discussion without adopting a political stance. The center was created as an affinity space and to criticize traditional patterns of thought. Yet, in an ironic twist, the Women’s Center has made itself into an exclusive space for a select group of students who choose not to question the tenets of progressivism. In its efforts to challenge the status quo, the center has neglected to consider that the ideals it promotes as obvious can — and should — also be challenged. Hannah Rubin Morse College ‘27

title: In uncertain job market, Yale students turn to new career pipelines
subtitle: Organizations such as the Buckley Institute, the David Network and Dwight Hall are providing new professional opportunities as undergraduates confront a tough post-graduation landscape and Yale pulls back some resources.
author: Michael Ndubisi, Jane Park, Olivia Woo
text: When Will Barbee ’26 arrived at Yale, he didn't come for politics. He said he came for theater and music. His freshman fall, he rushed five a cappella groups, a dramatic society and some of the campus comedy troupes, navigating the high-stakes audition culture that serves as a brutal rite of passage for Yale first years. Like most students, he met rejection. Within two weeks, he filled out an application to become a fellow of the William F. Buckley Jr. Program, a campus organization founded to promote conservative thought and open debate, and was accepted. The application, he said, took about 15 minutes. “I was immediately accepted into the community,” Barbee said. What followed surprised him. There were speaker events open to anyone, followed by smaller, invitation-only dinners at New Haven restaurants. He said it was a level of intellectual access he found that Yale’s formal classrooms rarely matched, so he kept going back. By his junior year, he was president of the program. By his senior year, a Buckley mentor was writing his recommendations for journalism fellowships. Barbee’s path is not unique. As the class of 2026 navigates a job market that has rarely looked more uncertain, job postings have proliferated while actual offers have declined, producing a market that appears abundant from the outside but functions like a brick wall for most applicants. The Trump administration’s cuts to the federal workforce and military education have closed off categories of public interest employment that schools like Yale have long relied on as graduate destinations. And finance and consulting, long the reliable fallback for elite graduates, are now slowing hiring and announcing layoffs of their own. Over several months, the News spoke with more than a dozen students, alumni and program directors across the Buckley Program, the David Network and Dwight Hall. At Yale, perhaps the most durable professional pipelines now belong not to the University but to organized programs that have moved into the space it left open, and the students who are finding their footing there. Mixed signals Johns Hopkins sociologist Amy Binder finds that at elite universities, the path from credential to career is largely unstructured. At large public universities, career prep programs and employer pipelines are often built into the curriculum. At highly selective institutions like Yale, students navigate that path themselves. In a 2025 white paper, Binder described the process as less a formal pipeline than a gravitational pull from peer culture, on-campus recruitment and institutional signals that steer students, often without their full awareness, toward a narrow set of outcomes she calls “ career funneling .” Across Ivy-Plus schools — the Ivy League and several other top universities — roughly half of all work-bound graduates end up in finance, consulting or tech, according to a 2016 study published in the journal Sociology of Education. At Harvard, more than half of the class of 2025 ended up in those same three fields according to The Harvard Crimson’s annual senior survey. At Yale, finance and consulting alone accounted for more than a third of the class of 2025’s first destinations according to a University report on graduates’ first jobs. But a growing number of students in the class of 2026 are realizing there may be no light at the end of the funnel. The Office of Career Strategy has moved to meet record-breaking demand. According to its annual report for 2024–25, the office conducted 7,391 total advising interactions, a 9 percent increase over the previous year. The office’s deputy director, Robyn Acampora, wrote in an email to the News that 87 percent of the class of 2025 reported using the office’s resources. Even as students increasingly turn to those official channels for guidance, Yale administrators under budget pressure have narrowed some financial resources available to them. In the fall, Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis announced that the Summer Experience Award and the International Study Award — two non-competitive funding opportunities for financial aid recipients — would be subsumed into a single Summer Experience Grant beginning in 2027 , citing the upcoming increase in the federal tax on university endowment returns, which raised Yale’s rate on investment gains from 1.4 to 8 percent. Allie Ruiz ’28, a Dwight Hall Student Executive Committee coordinator, said students “might have limited opportunities to engage in public service because of the cuts to the ISA, SEA.” For students already navigating what Binder describes as an unstructured path from credential to career, the loss of summer funding shifts an already outsized burden further. For years, students have had to construct, through an architecture of clubs and social maneuvering, the kind of career infrastructure that many state schools provide as standard programming. Several student organizations have spent years building exactly that, increasingly becoming the places students believe can actually turn a credential into a career. Each has a different theory of what a Yale career can look like and different tools for getting there. A think tank in New Haven? Founded in 2010 by a handful of undergraduates who felt the campus had no serious home for conservative thought, the William F. Buckley Jr. Program today has over 800 student fellows — more than one in 10 Yale undergraduates — and a multimillion-dollar budget. Its summer internship program places students in Washington, D.C., and New York on a $5,000 stipend that is sponsored by the program and administered directly through the Office of Career Strategy. When asked publicly why Yale has avoided the targeted funding cuts that have hit most of its Ivy League peers, University President Maurie McInnis said there was “no obvious answer,” but cited the Buckley Institute — the professional, off-campus parent of the student-led program — and a broader campus tradition of “open debate” as potential reasons. No matter Buckley’s significance for the institution, Isaac Oberman ’26, who comes from Wisconsin, came to the program the same way Barbee did — almost by accident. After hearing from a friend that becoming a fellow was the easiest way to get priority tickets to a speaker event, he filled out the form. Before Buckley, his only internship had been at a dairy company back home. When asked if he had joined looking for professional direction, Oberman didn’t hesitate: “Definitely not.” He kept showing up anyway. He attended winter-break seminars and met people, including someone who had interned at the Calvin Coolidge Presidential Foundation, a small conservative organization based in Washington and Vermont, through a Buckley-sponsored internship. Oberman applied, went through several rounds of interviews and got it. That summer, he was in Washington transcribing presidential speeches, traveling to Vermont to run debate programs for high school students and writing book reviews for the foundation’s journal. “It was an experiment, really,” he said. “By taking this internship in a field I wasn’t used to, I discovered skills in writing and people-interaction that I wouldn’t have known I had otherwise.” The Buckley stipend had bought him something harder to quantify than a summer salary. Trevor MacKay ’25, a former Buckley program president now working at McKinsey in Washington, was measured in his assessment of the program’s preprofessional element. The 800 fellows are not all conservatives, he said, and internal surveys show an even ideological split. People join, he said, because they want to hear arguments the rest of campus treats as not worth having. “I’m not sure that people are joining Buckley because they’re thinking, ‘Oh, they’re going to get me a job,’” he said. “But it certainly doesn’t hurt.” What makes it work, he added, is that “it does create these connections organically. It’s not really artificial.” What makes any of it possible is a carefully engineered funding structure built over its 14-year history. The Buckley Institute works with partner organizations such as the Manhattan Institute, National Review and the Coolidge Presidential Foundation to offset the cost of bringing on an additional intern. That money flows from an interconnected ecosystem of conservative alumni and philanthropies, including the Bradley Foundation and DonorsTrust, which allows donors to give anonymously while guaranteeing their contributions reach only conservative causes. Many of the foundations in that network also fund the partner organizations where Buckley places its interns. The Institute brought in just over $2 million in 2023. In 2024, that figure surged to nearly $3.8 million according to Buckley Institute tax filings . “The institute model was implemented to accommodate the massive interest we had on campus,” Barbee told the News, “and the big ideas we wanted to accomplish. It’s allowed us to grow into something bordering on a think tank, right here in New Haven.” Dinner at Villa Lulu To Buckley’s political right sits an organization that operates under a different set of assumptions. The David Network was founded in 2020 and has since grown to 3,000 students and 4,000 alumni and professionals across 10 elite universities, including Yale. Where Buckley accepts almost anyone who fills out a form, the David Network explicitly vets for conservatives. “Ideological alignment is our first priority,” Annie Rogers, the organization’s director of development and external affairs, said in a phone interview. “That comes first, always.” The network’s primary vehicle for translating that vetting into professional access is the private dinner, Rodgers said, adding that the setting is deliberately far removed from the sterile anxiety of a campus career fair and designed to make the professional outcome feel like a byproduct of the conversation. “It’s not really about bypassing traditional application portals,” Rogers said. “It’s about working with students to help them build their networks.” But some of the roles the network helps fill, she said, are specialized enough that they would never appear on a public portal in the first place. At a recent David Network event at Villa Lulu, an upscale Italian restaurant just off campus, a senior executive from Palantir spent the evening dining with a small group of undergraduates, one student present told the News on the condition of anonymity, citing professional repercussions. Before the night was over, the executive had instructed the students at the table to send him their resumes so they could be “plugged in,” according to the student. Rogers confirmed that Palantir has a “unique recruitment and vetting system” and that the network works to accommodate it when the company comes to campus. But not everyone navigating this job market gets access to that kind of evening. Abby Assouad, Staff Photographer Playing catch-up For students considering public-interest careers — which include working in the government or at nonprofits — the alternatives to Buckley and the David Network look very different. Dwight Hall, founded in 1886 as a center for voluntary service at Yale, was never designed to be a career office. But it increasingly resembles one. Last Tuesday, roughly 60 students filed into Dwight Hall’s common room to hear the Dutch historian Rutger Bregman describe consulting, banking and corporate law as a “Bermuda Triangle” of wasted talent. Bregman’s core message to the room of undergraduates was a rejection of the standard prestige track in favor of what he calls “moral ambition,” combining the operational drive of the private sector with the idealism of an activist to tackle neglected problems. He was there representing the School for Moral Ambition — a nonprofit, expanding on his latest book by the same name, that pairs students with public-interest internships and stipends in a model designed to make a summer of service financially competitive with a corporate internship. Bregman closed his pitch with a bleak concession about the reality awaiting the student attendees after graduation. “Let’s be honest: Today, a lot of people get burnout while they do jobs they don’t really like all that much or that don’t really contribute all that much to the welfare of the world,” Bregman told the crowd. “So if we’re going to get a burnout anyway, we might as well do something useful.” Still, the Office of Career Strategy has maintained its own programming for those on the public-interest track. Through its Common Good and Creative Careers initiative, the office hosted over 85 employer events in the last academic year and funds up to 12 students annually through a competitive fellowship for nonprofit internships carrying a $5,500 stipend, according to Jorimel Zaldivar, the senior associate director for common good careers. But institutional scale is not the same as career advancement. “For people interested in public service careers like myself,” Ruiz, the Dwight Hall coordinator, who attended the event where Bregman spoke, “the resource gap is difficult to ignore.” The finance and consulting tracks include formal recruitment pipelines, employer relationships and alumni networks that begin working with students before they declare a major. Dwight Hall has partially closed that distance by paying students directly through its Urban Fellows and summer fellowship programs, so the nonprofits hosting them don’t have to absorb the cost. According to Ruiz, Dwight Hall is now in conversations with the School for Moral Ambition. The organization launched its first fellowship program based in the United States at Harvard , where 8 percent of the junior class applied for 12 spots, and has since established chapters at Brown, Stanford, Princeton, Vanderbilt and several universities in Europe. Yale does not yet have one. Laura Clancy, a senior program manager for the fellowship, wrote in an email to the News that the School for Moral Ambition was “actively evaluating a range of universities, including Yale, as potential sites for the future,” adding, “we know what an incredible source of talent Yale already is to high-impact nonprofits.” That interest arrives as Yale constrains its own support. Acampora, the deputy director of the Office of Career Services, wrote to the News that the combination of two summer grants into a single award will require students on financial aid to plan their summers “more intentionally” across their four years at Yale. The median post-graduation starting salary for the Yale College class of 2025 was $90,000, according to the Office of Career Strategy. Jane Park and Olivia Woo contributed reporting .

title: They cut ties with parents. How do they afford Yale on their own?
subtitle: To get an aid award that is not based on their parents’ financial information, students who are estranged from their parents, some due to domestic abuse, must petition Yale’s financial aid office for independent status.
author: Jane Park
text: Marie cut ties with her parents at 21. While many people enjoy their parents’ phone or health insurance plans through college and beyond, Marie left that behind halfway through her sophomore year. Marie — a current Yale senior who asked to be referred to by her middle name due to fear of retribution from her parents — had suffered a lifetime of domestic abuse at her parents’ hands, she said in an interview. Though she’d considered emancipation during high school, Marie knew that she still needed her parents’ financial support to attend Yale, an “education that would sustain my life,” she said. After starting college, she began distancing herself from her parents and visiting home less frequently. Marie said she planned to end contact with them entirely once she graduated from Yale. That moment came sooner than she’d anticipated. In the fall of 2024, during a gap year spent at home, Marie said the violence became unbearable. “I thought I was going to die,” she recalled. “So I left.” Picking up a second part-time job and draining her savings, Marie found a short-term lease in her hometown and lived with a roommate until returning to school in January 2025, she said. But she still needed to figure out one more thing: How would she pay for the cost of attendance at Yale now that she was estranged from her parents? Students like Marie, who choose to break ties with their parents, often must find a way to pay for their Yale tuition without any family members pitching in. Students’ federal or Yale financial aid packages are calculated according to their parents’ incomes and assets. This means that students who no longer want to be financially supported by their parents must apply for independent student status — a condition that allows students’ eligibility for aid to be determined by their financial information alone. The process of petitioning for independent status at Yale is shrouded by a lack of public information and requires students to convey and even prove their personal situations to financial aid officers, according to four students who have participated in the petitioning process. One of the students gave up before securing independent status. The four students interviewed by the News described the application process as inaccessible, and said that financial aid officers were ill-equipped or insensitive in their responses to learning about students’ traumatic experiences. The News reviewed copies of therapists’ letters, medical records and email correspondences that four students submitted to the financial aid office to corroborate their accounts of domestic violence and homelessness. Financial aid officers, in turn, must gather enough information to verify students’ claims and ensure that aid is given to students who truly need it, said Alex Muro, the senior associate director of the Yale Undergraduate Financial Aid Office. Muro and two other financial aid officers, Nicole Rothfuss and Rondell Butler, said that the process of granting independent status was necessarily opaque and rigorous to protect the interests of the financial aid office and other Yale students. What emerges is a tension between students who say they need financial independence to escape abusive circumstances and financial aid officers who decide who qualifies for this aid — and who doesn’t. Embarking on the path to independence Qualifying for independent status and receiving considerable financial aid can help students avoid taking out substantial student loans in the wake of their estrangement. Through federal financial aid, independent students can receive up to $7,395, the maximum federal Pell Grant award. If they successfully petition for independent status through Yale’s financial aid office — a process that is separate from the Free Application for Federal Student Aid, better known as FAFSA — some students can attend Yale on a full ride, according to Muro. Yet, a lack of publicly available information about the process of petitioning for independent status may prevent students from finding out about the option in the first place, some students said. When students in the United States apply to college, they typically fill out the FAFSA with their parents’ financial information to determine their federal aid eligibility. Most students are considered “dependent,” though students who are married, orphaned, emancipated or homeless would be considered independent. This means that their eligibility for aid is determined only by their financial information — not that of their parents — according to the Federal Student Aid website . Under federal law, only college financial aid administrators can change a student’s dependency status once the student is enrolled. Students thus need to petition for this status change through their institution’s financial aid office, citing domestic abuse or other extreme circumstances that justify them no longer being financially dependent on their parents. Financial aid officers grant students independence based on their own discretion, while consulting the Federal Student Aid Handbook , Muro said. Muro said that “probably a few dozen students” apply for independent status each year. He declined to share exactly how many independent students are currently enrolled at Yale. Three of the independent students interviewed by the News said they began the petitioning process by submitting an inquiry to the financial aid office’s website. According to Muro, the office follows up with students who submit such inquiries and asks some to submit a personal statement and at least one letter providing “neutral, professional, third-party documentation” of the circumstances that led students to pursue independent status. This documentation can include therapist letters, police reports or court orders. If students cannot provide any formal documentation, financial aid officers meet with them to discuss their reasons for applying for independence and sometimes waive the formal documentation process. According to Rothfuss, the financial aid office may also reach out to a “counselor, dean, or other campus professionals” to gather additional information about the petitioning student. “That said, it is always most important for us to hear directly from the student, in their words, regarding the circumstances prompting their request for independence,” Rothfuss wrote in an email to the News. When critical information is difficult to find Unable to find specific information about the process of applying for independent status on Yale webpages, Marie turned to college information websites and forums like Reddit. She said that she was “confused” about why there was so little information online — an experience that was shared by three other students interviewed by the News. The term “independent student status” only appears on the financial aid office’s “ Forms for Undergraduate Financial Aid ” and the “ Frequently Asked Questions ” pages. The former includes PDFs of a “ Verification Statement for Independent Students ” — a CollegeBoard form that is not used in Yale’s petitioning process. The “Frequently Asked Questions” page mentions “independent student status” when it explains that a parent’s refusal to pay is not a “sufficient” reason for students to petition for independence. Muro explained this philosophy by providing a hypothetical scenario: Two students live in the same town and come from families with the same financial resources. It would not be “fair,” Muro said, for one student to receive aid because his parents refuse to pay while the other’s parents pay their expected contribution. The office only considers students’ financial need separately from their parents’ financial ability in “rare cases,” based on a student’s “documented petition,” according to the webpage. But, the site doesn’t explain what a “documented petition” is or how students can access it. The website also doesn’t define the types of “exceptional situations” that would qualify the exclusion of parents’ financial information. According to Marie, students are unable to make an “informed decision” about disclosing sensitive information in the petitioning process because they don’t know what information to share, who they should be prepared to share it with and how their information will be used. “They take away some of your autonomy in your decision-making by not giving you that information,” she said. Marie decided to do something about the lack of readily-available information on Yale websites and took matters into her own hands. Starting in August 2024, Marie documented the steps she took during her petitioning process and created a guide to inform prospective independent students at Yale. This guide is now 20 pages long and includes sections unrelated to the petitioning process, such as ways to budget, sources of emergency financial aid and housing resources for students unable to return home for breaks. Marie said she is in the final stages of editing the document and hopes to begin distributing it soon. Some students said that the lack of information about the petitioning process — including what qualifies as necessary documentation, the factors determining eligibility for independent status and the timeline of the petitioning process — hindered the process of attaining independence. Four of them also expressed frustration about what has become a decentralized, informal spread of information between students. For some of the students interviewed, the Harbor Fellows program — a collective of students who lack parental support that is funded entirely by Yale faculty and anonymous benefactors — has provided them financial and social support they weren’t able to find elsewhere. Not all members of the Harbor Fellows program are independent students. Andre Thomas ’27 said that he first heard about petitioning for independent status through his friend, who was already an independent student and a member of the Harbor Fellows program. Gaining independent status was a way for Thomas to sever his financial dependence on his mother. He said that their relationship had become a form of “emotional, like psychological abuse.” Thomas successfully petitioned for independent status in September 2025. When Thomas applied the first time —a month earlier — his application was denied without explanation, he said. “I was in the dark pretty much the whole time.” “When we do not approve a student’s request to be independent, it can be difficult to explain why in a compassionate and respectful manner. Another challenge is the need to avoid leading questions so that we can clearly understand the student’s experience,” Butler, the financial aid office’s point person for independent students, wrote in an email to the News. A process that is opaque ‘by necessity’ According to Muro, the financial aid office has a reason for the lack of transparency surrounding the process of attaining independent status. Every student’s circumstances are different, Muro said, which means that the process for filing for independent status is “not straightforward.” Students often submit a personal statement and a letter of documentation from third-party professionals in their petition, but the financial aid office sometimes makes exceptions to those requirements. Butler recalled an instance in which the office could not obtain comment from a “neutral, third-party professional” and relied on the student’s sibling for corroboration instead. While financial aid officers use the Federal Student Aid Handbook as a guideline to assess cases, Muro said that the Yale financial aid office doesn’t have a formal procedure it follows when evaluating students’ petitions. Instead, it’s a collaborative, two-man job, Muro said. After a petition reaches Butler, Muro signs off on the decisions. Muro said he believes the process does not require formalized policies, nor would it make sense to establish procedures for what can be a complicated process. However, Muro said that he understands why some students may want more information about the petition process than is available on the public webpages. “I’m not dismissing that at all, but I think it’s impossible to ever eliminate that entirely,” Muro said. “I’m not going to make the claim to you that this is a transparent process. It isn’t. It is, by necessity, somewhat opaque.” Some schools, like Stanford and Princeton, do publicize information about their petitioning processes, as well as the circumstances that would make students eligible to petition for independent status. Princeton’s webpage says the school grants independent status in instances of abuse or abandonment by a student’s parents and directs students to the necessary forms for petitioning. Stanford’s website states that the office uses a “standard method” to review students’ financial circumstances and calculate an expected contribution. If students cannot meet that amount, the office awards or directs students to grants, scholarships and other resources. Financial aid officers at both Stanford and Princeton declined multiple requests for comment. The difficulty of proving abuse The nature of prospective independent students’ circumstances, many of which involve domestic violence, can complicate the petitioning process. Four students interviewed by the News said that there were moments when they felt as though their experiences were invalidated or not dealt with proper sensitivity. In one such instance, a sophomore, who requested anonymity to maintain privacy about her abuse, said that Anna Osman, then a counselor at Yale’s Sexual Harassment and Assault Response & Education Center — known as SHARE — emailed financial aid officer Nicole Rothfuss following a Zoom meeting that had upset the student. In an email to the sophomore, which the News obtained, Osman wrote that she had received a follow-up email from Rothfuss and “was able to explain to her again my concerns about your being made to share your story to initiate the dependency override request process.” Osman wrote that Rothfuss had spoken to her supervisor and was willing to speak with a “trusted third party” to “obtain the background they require.” The sophomore’s decision to pursue independent status came at the suggestion of Osman, to whom Yale Mental Health and Counseling referred the student after she told them about a traumatic event that occurred in her childhood. In response to the News’ request for comment on SHARE’s involvement with the financial aid office, director Jennifer Czincz wrote in an email that the office is not “permitted to do any interviews or meetings” due to its “highly confidential nature.” Osman, whose LinkedIn profile says she no longer works at Yale, did not respond to multiple requests for comment. The sophomore said that, prior to the meeting, Osman had informed the student that she didn’t need to explain more than the “bare minimum” details of her familial situation to Rothfuss. Despite this, the sophomore said that Rothfuss kept pushing for more information. “She kept asking for the full picture,” the student recounted. Rothfuss wrote to the News that she could not speak in detail about specific student experiences, citing “privacy and other concerns.” She said that the financial aid office seeks only the “minimum detail to make a financial decision.” The first question that the office asks students, according to Rothfuss, is, “Why, as in what is about your circumstances that suggest that we should make an exception to our general policy to require parents to submit their financial information to be considered in the determination of a student's financial aid eligibility?” For financial aid officers, asking detailed questions is a necessary part of their jobs, even if it comes across as “unnecessary” or “invasive” to students, Muro said. According to Muro, this process is not intended to subject students to a higher standard, nor should it imply that the financial aid office does not believe students. “We’re not asking unnecessary questions. We need to understand because it is our job to allocate the university’s resources, to spend money, to support students,” Muro said. “But we also need to make sure that the right students are getting the right support, and that we’re being fair and equitable.” Occasionally, the financial aid office’s fiduciary duty to the University can clash with the professional opinions of mental health counselors, Muro said. In some instances, counselors write letters that justify students’ decisions to cut ties with their parents, but the documentation fails to explain the facts of the circumstances that led to estrangement. “It’s well-intentioned, but the counselor is basically making the decision for us,” Muro said. “It’s like, ‘Whoa, we understand how you feel, but ultimately, it’s not your decision to make.’” Some of the students interviewed by the News said that it can be difficult to prove instances of domestic abuse. Muro acknowledged the difficulty of asking students to present what would be the most convincing documentation — such as court documents, restraining orders and police records. These documents typically only emerge from extreme circumstances, Muro said. It’s not uncommon for survivors of intimate family or partner abuse to not report their experiences to authorities. According to a 2018 U.S. Department of Justice survey on “criminal victimization,” only 47 percent of domestic violence cases are reported each year. “Proof is not always there,” Thomas said. “Sometimes, there’s not always going to be hard proof for someone to see that you’ve dealt with X, Y and Z, but it doesn’t mean that it doesn’t matter, that it doesn’t happen.” Muro said that financial aid officers do not receive formal sensitivity training, but he said “everyone” on the financial aid team knows what to do if a student approaches a staff member in distress. “I am an active and patient listener, who follows students to proceed through their process at their own pace and as they feel comfortable,” Rothfuss and Butler both wrote in their separate emails to the News. Marie, who said she was confused by the lack of online information, was doubtful of Muro’s claim. “I am not experiencing the sensitivity that I feel like there should be,” Marie said. Harbor Fellows provides community, support Despite being little known around campus, the Harbor Fellows program has provided its members with financial support and community that students are not able to find elsewhere, according to two students. From movie nights to advocacy events, the group brings together students who are estranged from their parents –– an experience that is largely invisible to the rest of the Yale community. Housed in Dwight Hall, the program provides semesterly stipends and ad hoc emergency funds to its members. The program currently supports two dozen independent students, according to Topher Allen ’27, the student coordinator of the program. Because it has limited funding, the program requires students to apply. Harbor Fellows is entirely funded by a small group of faculty and alumni who “prefer to remain anonymous,” Allen wrote in an email to the News. Although its website lists Dwight Hall, the Yale College Dean’s Office and other Yale-affiliated groups as sponsors, Allen wrote that the group receives no funding from Dwight Hall, Yale’s endowment or any other external organization. Other members of the Harbor Fellows advisory board declined to be interviewed for this article. The group has grown nearly four times its size since spring 2024, according to Allen, but he wrote that the advisory board has maintained an acceptance rate of “over 95%.” All accepted students receive funding that covers expenses that result from the “additional burden and uncertainty of not having a home to return to during breaks,” Allen wrote. According to students interviewed by the News, the financial support covers housing, transportation and even medication. Harbor Fellows who live off-campus receive an additional $2,000 annual stipend due to the “increase in New Haven rental costs,” Allen wrote. For Marie and Joseph Lee ’25, who was a Harbor Fellows member, the program is a way for students to share and advocate for their experiences in ways that they aren’t able to do otherwise. Lee joined Harbor Fellows as a senior, after hearing about the program from a friend already in the group. Being a first-generation, low-income student is already a difficult process, and not having traditional parental support amplifies an already lonely experience, he said. “I want Harbor Fellows to grow and flourish because, like, there’s something not just about the money support that’s important there, but like, having a community of people who get something that is sort of maybe unspeakable at first and hard to communicate,” Lee said.
