[embed width="700" height="600"]https://www.google.com/maps/d/u/1/embed?mid=1fyj2JSFfGODmFsgaKKd_40ZIbO3wAT4&ehbc=2E312F&noprof=1[/embed] Property transfers raise legal questions One reason for using subsidiaries is to limit a company’s liability. If an LLC wants to further diminish the risk associated with a particular property, it might transfer ownership to different subsidiaries to ensure that one subsidiary's legal challenges do not affect the others. In the event of a court case against one of the subsidiaries, this business structure protects the assets of the parent company as well as its other subsidiary companies. On their own, property transfers can be a standard business practice. However, such transfers can be deemed fraudulent if a company makes them with the intent to avoid paying debts. Under Section 52-552e of Connecticut’s Uniform Fraudulent Transfer Act , transfers are fraudulent if they are made with “ actual intent to hinder, delay or defraud any creditor of the debtor” and debtors “engage in a business or a transaction” that renders their remaining assets “unreasonably small in relation to the business or transaction.” In other words, if transfers are made with intent to defraud creditors, and debtors also sell off a large proportion of their assets by engaging in these transfers, these business practices are considered fraudulent. According to the act, a creditor is any person with a right to payment from the debtor. This includes “judgment” payments that are determined by the outcome of a court case, which could apply to tenants in housing court. In the case of property transfers between Ocean’s subsidiaries , large assets were transferred from one business entity to another. Additionally, if these property transfers were made as a purchase of assets , the new subsidiary would not assume pre-existing liability associated with the property. This would mean that if a tenant sued its landlord for a housing code violation, the original subsidiary would still be liable. Ultimately, this strategy creates a loophole that protects the assets of an LLC and may help a company avoid paying its debts, including fines incurred in housing court. Between June 2023 and January 2024, Ocean and its subsidiaries sold 71 properties. Sixty-four of these properties had been transferred through at least two different subsidiaries affiliated with Ocean prior to sale. For example, if Super Zen LLC had transferred a property to Naiman Michal Shlomit LLC as a purchase of assets, then Naiman Michal Shlomit LLC would not assume the liability associated with the property. This would mean that if a tenant living at the property sued its landlord for a housing code violation, the original subsidiary — Super Zen LLC — would be liable for these complaints. However, because the subsidiary has since transferred its assets, the tenant may not be able to recover money from the case. Gary Kaufman, a corporate attorney based in New York, explained that if a tenant were to sue a subsidiary company for a housing code violation, they would not be able to reach assets of the parent company or the other subsidiaries that the parent owns. “You can go downstream but you can’t go upstream, so to speak ... if you sue the parent, you can attack the subs[idiaries]. But if you sue the subs[idiaries], you can’t attack the parent,” Kaufman said. If such an intent were sufficiently proven, this could suggest that Ocean was conducting transfers to avoid paying fines in court cases where it was sued for housing code violations. You can go downstream but you can’t go upstream, so to speak ... if you sue the parent, you can attack the subs[idiaries]. But if you sue the subs[idiaries], you can’t attack the parent, Gary Kaufman Ocean’s history in court While transfers can be deemed fraudulent if they are deliberately made with the intent to avoid legal repercussions, intent is notoriously difficult to prove in court, and would likely require evidence from Ocean’s internal communications that the News could not access. However, the UFTA outlines factors that may be considered in determining intent, including whether the debtor had been sued before the transfer was made. T here have been several instances where Ocean transferred property ownership to another entity it controls right before a lawsuit was filed against the LLC. In recent court cases filed against Ocean, most of the involved properties have undergone ownership transfers between at least two Ocean-owned subsidiary companies. In one case that involved three properties, two had undergone transfers within a year of the court case reaching a judgment. A case that concluded on May 3, 2022 , charged Ocean $3,750 worth of fines after the company pleaded guilty to 15 different housing code violations related to neglect of the properties. Of the three Ocean-owned properties involved in the case — located at 133 Plymouth St., 267 James St. and 167 Scranton St. — each of the three properties were transferred between four different Ocean subsidiaries between 2015 and 2023. Two of these properties were transferred within one year of the court case concluding, with ownership at the Scranton Street property last being transferred on Dec. 20, 2021. Ownership of the James Street property was transferred on March 16, 2022 — less than two months before the court case ended. The property at James Street was then transferred to a fourth subsidiary on June 6, 2023. This trend is relatively consistent among other properties involved in major cases against Ocean. In an August 2022 case concerning two properties that culminated in $2,500 worth of court-ordered fines , a property at 191 Ferry St. underwent four transfers across different subsidiaries and the property at 87 Willis St. underwent three. The last of these transfers at both properties took place on Jan. 28, 2020, less than two years before the court case reached a judgment. Similar patterns occurred at the four properties involved in a June 2023 case . All of the properties underwent multiple property transfers between Ocean subsidiaries, and three of these transfers took place within three years of the court cases ending. This pattern could indicate that the transfers were made in expectation of an impending lawsuit, suggesting that Ocean might have made these transfers with the intention of avoiding legal repercussions for their violations of the housing code. There are alternative explanations for Ocean’s property transfers, such as potential tax benefits. Property transfers between affiliated corporations are exempt from paying real estate conveyance taxes, according to Code 11 in a Connecticut list of exemptions . Alternatively, these transfers could be a product of the company’s internal disorganization. Carol Lopez Horsford, the founder of Farnam Realty Group, said that Ocean Management has experienced organizational challenges in the past. Ocean hired Farnam to conduct its residential leasing in 2019, a partnership that provided Farnam with insight into Ocean’s behind-the-scenes operations until April 2022, when Farnam ended its work with Ocean on the grounds that their company values were no longer aligned. “[Ocean is a] small business that probably grew too fast and wasn't organized enough,” Lopez Horsford said. Nevertheless, Ocean’s property ownership transfers align with patterns that could indicate intent to circumvent paying debts incurred in court. While Ocean’s property ownership transfers do not indicate illegality on their own, these findings suggest that Ocean’s long history of housing code violations might be accompanied by fraudulent intent to minimize the company’s own liability. Ocean’s offices are located on the second floor of 101 Whitney Ave. Natasha Khazzam covers housing and homelessness for the News. Contact her at natasha.khazzam@yale.edu .

title: Katie Ledecky, ‘The Ethicist’ columnist among honorary degree awardees
subtitle: Seven individuals are set to receive an honorary degree, the University’s highest honor, during Yale’s commencement ceremony on Monday. They include a MacArthur fellow, an Olympic gold medalist, the columnist for “The Ethicist” and a former mayor of Baltimore.
author: Leo Nyberg, Aria Lynn-Skov
text: During Yale’s 325th Commencement on Monday, hundreds of aspiring doctors, political scientists and playwrights will receive their diplomas hours after individuals who have reached preeminence in those fields are awarded with honorary degrees. Since 1702, the Yale board of trustees has selected the recipients of the honorary degree, which is the highest honor awarded by the University. In past years, Yale has kept the names of the individuals receiving the degrees private before Commencement weekend, but seven of this year’s recipients were announced in a Friday press release . A reception for the honorary degree recipients has historically been hosted at the Yale Center for British Art, but this year’s was held instead at the Yale Peabody Museum on Sunday evening, Yale spokesperson Karen Peart confirmed. “The honorary degrees dinner has rotated among the homes of Yale’s extraordinary collections including the BAC, YUAG, the Beinecke and now this year the newly renovated Peabody Museum with its beautiful new atrium,” Peart wrote in an email to the News, referring to the Yale Center for British Art and the Yale University Art Gallery. This year’s recipients, announced in the Friday press release, are 14-time Olympic medalist swimmer Katie Ledecky, The New York Times Magazine columnist Kwame Anthony Appiah, Harvard and MIT anesthesiologist Emery Brown, playwright and Columbia professor Lynn Nottage DRA ’89, political scientist Robert Putnam GRD ’70, former Baltimore mayor and University of Baltimore President Kurt Schmoke ’71 and MIT medical researcher JoAnne Stubbe. Last year, Yale honored eight individuals with honorary degrees, including actress Debbie Allen and literary scholar Henry Louis Gates Jr. ’73. Putnam said in a phone interview that he was “deeply honored” when he learned “at least a year ago” that he would be receiving an honorary degree from Yale, adding that he had to keep the news private from everyone but his wife, children and an executive assistant. “Getting an honorary degree from my own university is a special one. It reminds me of how much I learned at Yale,” Putnam, who earned his doctorate at Yale in 1970, said. The political scientist is known for his writings regarding loneliness in American life, in particular his seminal 2000 work “Bowling Alone.” In the book, Putnam chronicles how Americans were increasingly isolated and withdrawn from civic life, laying blame on factors such as urban sprawl and digital media. Not including the forthcoming Yale degree, Putnam has 21 honorary degrees to his name. Appiah, who has written his popular column, “The Ethicist,” for over a decade, also found out he was to be awarded the degree sometime last year. Like Nottage and Putnam, the Yale honorary degree will not be Appiah’s first — indeed, it will be his twenty-first . “It’s a great honor to be honored by a great university if you’re an academic,” Appiah said, referring to honorary degrees. “The Yale one means a lot to me because Yale was my first home in America.” The Ghanaian-English philosopher was born in London, grew up in Ghana and attended Cambridge, where he earned his bachelor’s and doctorate degrees. Appiah’s first job as a professor was at Yale, where he taught philosophy and Afro-American studies. “Yale’s always meant a lot to me,” Appiah said. Nottage, who is the only woman to have won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama twice and is the recipient of a MacArthur Fellowship, said that her experience at Yale has shaped her career. “Yale has been so foundational in terms of who I am as a writer and as a teacher,” Nottage said in a phone interview. “I went to the Yale School of Drama, and then subsequently came back and taught at the drama school for 13 years.” Nottage characterized the honorary degree as a “wonderful recognition” that means the “energy you’re putting into your craft is still being seen and rewarded.” In 2025 , the Yale Dramatic Association’s spring mainstage production was Nottage’s play “Intimate Apparel.” A Yale webpage lists “illustrative” examples of honorary degree recipients, including Benjamin Franklin, Meryl Streep DRA ’75 and Hillary Clinton LAW ’73.

subtitle: Yale College's traditional Class Day Exercises took place on Sunday, May 17th, 2026, at 2:00 pm until 3:30 pm EST, recognizing the graduating class before Commencement for their academic and creative achievement.

title: Min Jin Lee recalls disillusioned Yale years, urges seniors to savor time
subtitle: The acclaimed writer recounted once feeling like she had “wasted” her undergraduate years and emphasized the value of “qualitative, opportune time.” Her critiques of diverse sociopolitical issues were met with an impassioned audience response.
author: Leo Nyberg
text: Novelist Min Jin Lee ’90 headlined the annual Class Day ceremony on Sunday, describing her tumultuous years as an undergraduate at Yale and sending a message to the class of 2026 to “choose the important over the urgent.” Lee spoke after Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis and was introduced by the student organizers of Class Day. Following Lee’s address — which lasted 58 minutes — student groups performed songs reflecting on their years at Yale and speeches rife with jokes about careers in consulting, Yale football’s four-year winning streak against Harvard and the revised University mission statement, which some seniors have criticized as directionless. Lee, who won multiple writing awards at Yale, said she received inspiration for her second and National Book Award-finalist novel, “Pachinko,” at a Trumbull College tea she attended as an undergraduate. Much of Lee’s speech focused on her time at Yale, where she led the Korean Studies Task Force and advocated for the introduction of a Korean language class and more courses on Korean history. Lee, who immigrated from South Korea to the United States when she was 7 years old, said she encountered racism from Yale faculty who assumed Asian students only studied hard sciences and spoke poor English. Lee said she felt “disillusioned” at her Class Day 36 years ago and thought then that she had “wasted” her college years. “I was not tapped by any societies or clubs. I said and wrote things which upset important people,” Lee said on Sunday, drawing applause. “And I was so dumb or naïve because I didn’t know who was important in the world, and it turns out all the important people knew each other.” She said she did not pursue graduate school or a doctoral degree in history, her major, because she did not have the grades or support of professors. “It can be reasonably argued that I did not use my time well in college and that I had missed my opportunities,” Lee said. It took her 12 years for her to publish her debut novel, she said, after she quit her law firm job to become a novelist. Her second novel took her another decade, she added. But Lee said quantitative efficiency is not the only value of time. Instead, she emphasized to the class of 2026 a different understanding of time — “qualitative, opportune time,” represented by the ancient Greek “kairos” — which she said “tells you what matters and when.” Lee, who said she had been thinking about Sunday’s speech since Yale President Maurie McInnis invited her to speak at Class Day last May, encouraged the graduating seniors to make the most out of their time — “Time is not your enemy; time is your friend,” she said — and to stand up for themselves. “Nothing, nothing was wasted about your time here if you let the brightest and the darkest moments teach you to struggle better with truth,” Lee said. Martin Peralta, Head Photography Editor While much of Lee’s speech recounted her life story, she also said she has been thinking about “what your generation has experienced and is experiencing.” “A pandemic, deadly invasions and wars, mass school shootings, wildfires, climate change, inflation, the destruction of voting rights, AI, clip economies, attention economies, the enshittification of tech platforms, rampant economic inequality, lack of affordable housing, the rise of Neoliberalism and authoritarianism,” Lee said to mounting applause. “And then there’s ICE,” Lee then said, which students immediately met with loud boos. Lee was referring to United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement, which, under President Donald Trump’s second term, has facilitated a mass deportation operation, including in Connecticut and New Haven . The boos continued as Lee mentioned more topics like “the loss of reproductive rights,” Trump’s Department of Government Efficiency and cuts to scientific research funding. While Yale has largely avoided targeted funding cuts like that at Harvard and Columbia, researchers on campus have described challenges with funding under Trump’s second term, and an impending federal endowment tax hike has led to budget cuts across the University. The 80-degree heat on a sun-scorched Old Campus did not deter students from shouting their residential college chants before the ceremony began and cheering loudly when their colleges were mentioned in Lee’s speech. Hannah Qin ’26, who, like Lee, is in Trumbull College, said she enjoyed Lee’s references to Trumbull. Each time Lee brought up a college — especially Trumbull — the section where the students were seated would launch into applause or bang matching noisemakers. “I was really moved by her speech and I resonated with what she said about finding her own path outside of Yale and thinking back on Yale with a new perspective,” Qin also said. Colin Kim ’26 said that he found it inspiring, especially as a fellow Korean, that Lee found success later in her life despite “not succeeding necessarily” at Yale. Last year, former New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern spoke at Class Day .

title: Now in Toronto, former Yale professors continue to counter Trump
subtitle: Professors Marci Shore and Jason Stanley, who both left Yale for the University of Toronto last spring, said Canada has provided a more open academic climate for them to resist what they described as an authoritarian rise at home.
author: Sasha Hurowitz
text: Last spring, three Yale professors — history professors Marci Shore and Timothy Snyder and philosophy professor Jason Stanley — left New Haven for the University of Toronto’s Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy. A year later, Shore and Stanley spoke to the News about the public-facing scholarship they have been working on in Toronto in the face of what they described as an authoritarian rise in the United States, as well as their motivations for leaving and the chilling effect across American academia. While the three professors departed after the same year, they described decisions made based on varied timelines and distinct philosophical rationales. Stanley said in a phone interview that his departure “didn’t have much to do with Yale.” Instead, he said, he executed his departure to send a deliberate political message to the world that the United States is in severe crisis. Stanley told The Guardian in March 2025 that his final decision to leave was catalyzed by watching Columbia University “completely capitulate” to political pressure. After the Trump administration announced the cancellation of $400 million in Columbia’s research funding, the university agreed to overhaul its protest policies and place its Middle Eastern, South Asian and African studies department and Center for Palestine Studies under an administrator’s purview. Stanley told The Guardian that he “just became very worried” when he “didn’t see a strong enough reaction in other universities to side with Columbia.” Over a year later, Stanley noted that he is still uncertain if leaving was the correct decision. “Whether or not it was the right decision depends on all kinds of factors,” Stanley said, explaining that his decision’s merit will ultimately depend on what he and his colleagues can build in Canada to support American democracy. Stanley said at the Munk School, he is working to establish a center that gathers global experts to combat authoritarianism and facism. By operating out of Toronto, he said that scholars can bypass what he refers to as the “anti-wokeness” funding restrictions spreading across American universities. He added that the freedom of speech in Canada allows him to organize and speak candidly about American politics without risking institutional backlash. Shore also wrote in an email to the News that the Munk School is designed precisely to support “interdisciplinary, engagé, both academic- and public-facing scholarship.” While she acknowledged the historical debate over scholars’ politically neutral, Shore asserted that she cannot maintain academic detachment. She wrote that her background as a historian of Eastern Europe allowed her to recognize “postmodern forms of fascism emerging” in American politics as early as 2015, comparing the climate to 1930s Europe. Combined with the ongoing slaughter of her friends and colleagues in Ukraine, Shore stated that neutrality is no longer a moral option. Shore added that her departure was a “very gradual, complex family decision” that originated long before the 2024 election. Shore emphasized that her choice was not exclusively political but that it was heavily influenced by anxieties about American gun violence. Pointing to New Haven’s proximity to the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting , which took place in Newtown, Conn., Shore noted her desire to raise her children in a country where per-capita gun ownership is lower. Drawing on her academic background, Shore wrote, “I’m a Slavicist, and I always have in mind what Anton Chekhov said about guns: once the gun is on the stage, it has to go off by the end of the last act. I fear that’s true in life as well as in theater.” In a video posted last June on Substack, the online newsletter platform, Snyder rejected public framings that he and his partner, Shore, were fleeing the Trump administration or acting out of cowardice. To his more than 400,000 subscribers, Snyder noted that his relocation was a family decision made during the Biden administration and argued that leaving an elite private institution for a public university like Toronto is a progressive action. “I can teach more diverse students, including students from more diverse economic backgrounds,” he said, adding that he hopes to be worthy of them. Addressing broader progressive strategies, Snyder argued on his Substack that international mobility and cross-border institutions have historically been crucial to democratization. He warned his audience against equating physical presence with political action, stating that “staying is not the same thing as fighting.” Addressing broader political strategies, Snyder cautioned those who consider themselves “progressive or being somehow in the resistance” against turning their energy inward to critique imperfect allies. “Dismissiveness is essential to Trumpism,” he said, warning that looking for excuses to dismiss others is a habit that draws people away from tangible political action. Snyder declined to comment for this article. A chilling effect on American academia, but sparks of hope from Yale Shore and Stnanley also pointed to a concerning shift in the climate of American higher education, observing an increasing atmosphere of fear and self-censorship among both faculty and students. These warnings arrive as the second Trump administration places unprecedented financial and ideological pressure on higher education. In July , Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which raised the top tax rate on private university endowments from 1.4 percent to 8 percent. Beyond financial penalties, the executive branch has leveraged federal funding to enforce ideological conformity. Last April, Trump signed an executive order overhauling the college accreditation system to explicitly eliminate diversity, equity and inclusion standards in favor of what he described as intellectual diversity . Shore told the News that her “apocalyptic nightmares” have begun playing out in the real world, noting that her American colleagues “no longer speak openly or directly about politics in public forums.” Shore pointed to an incident in January 2025 when Vice President JD Vance LAW ’13 tweeted that her husband, Snyder, was an “embarrassment to Yale.” Her fear, she noted, did not stem from Vance’s comments but from the fact that no Yale colleagues publicly defended him. Drawing on her expertise as a historian of 20th-century totalitarianism, Shore warned that in situations of encroaching tyranny, “people most often lower their heads and get in line.” Stanley said that American universities are facing immense pressure from donors and the federal government to implement “affirmative action for conservatives” and lean into “Trumpist anti-wokeness.” He further warned that this is a “voluntary” and “pointless effort” to placate what he described as an authoritarian regime. Stanley also expressed sympathy for students navigating an “atmosphere of fear,” arguing that mainstream publications — such as The New York Times and The Atlantic — have systematically vilified student protestors over the last decade. He noted that this media framing historically positioned leftist students as a primary threat to democracy, which helped fuel right-wing political backlashes. “It must seem, as a student at Yale, like the whole society is poised to attack you if you say anything political,” Stanley said. Despite their stark warnings regarding the state of American democracy, Shore and Stanley highlighted persistent sources of hope within university communities, including Yale’s. Stanley praised the ongoing resistance organized by American university faculty, specifically commending Yale’s chapter of the American Association of University Professors. He described the faculty organization efforts as “really admirable and impressive,” adding that Yale’s administration has generally shown an inclination to defend faculty free speech. Shore noted that she maintains close ties with Yale, returning to New Haven “from time to time” to meet with and advise students. She wrote that engaging with students — whether at Yale, in Toronto or elsewhere — remains her primary source of optimism. “I love seeing the sparks in their eyes when they put ideas together, start to ask new questions,” Shore wrote. “We’re all counting on this generation to save the world.” The Yale chapter of the American American Association of University Professors was revived in November 2024.

title: The class of 2026: By the numbers
subtitle: The News’ data desk surveyed graduating seniors to learn about the Class of 2026’s experiences at Yale — and discover what their futures may hold.
author: Adriana Petrizzo
text: Ahead of Commencement, the News’ data desk surveyed the Class of 2026 on their Yale experience and future plans, with questions spanning from academics and careers to love and nightlife. The questionnaire was sent to 1737 graduating seniors and received 172 total responses, though response rates vary by question. Dive in below to learn about the Class of 2026’s expected starting salaries, political views and thoughts on budget cuts at Yale. Demographics Of the respondents, 31 percent were offered admission to Yale in 2021 or earlier, while 68.4 percent were accepted in 2022 and 0.6 percent in 2023. 4.1 percent of respondents identify as nonbinary, 2.4 percent identify as transgender and 32.9 percent of respondents identify as bisexual, gay, lesbian, queer or asexual. 10.4 percent of respondents’ gender or sexual orientation has changed since they arrived at Yale. Of the respondents, 26.9 percent are first generation college students, while 22.2 percent are legacy students at Yale. The majority, 63.3 percent, attended a public high school, followed by 20.1 percent who attended a private, non-religious, non-boarding school. The majority of respondents, 57.8 percent, received financial aid. Of those receiving aid, the largest group, 43 percent, received 100 percent aid. Respondents pursued degrees across 52 different majors, with 26.9 percent picking up a double major. Graduating Yalies were split between enduring and new academic focuses, with 49.7 percent of respondents planning to graduate with a different major than they started with during their first year. The majority, 84.1 percent, finished their major requirements during senior year. The popularity of each area of study, organized by Yale’s disciplines , is represented below. History was the most popular major among respondents, totaling 14 percent of responses, followed by economics at 9.4 percent of responses. Experiences The Class of 2026 was active both on and off campus during their time at Yale. Respondents are hard workers, with 93 percent having held at least one student job and 81.7 percent having held at least one job or internship outside of Yale. Most respondents — 98.2 percent — were involved in at least one student group, with 60.8 percent reporting involvement in two to four groups. Most respondents, totalling 61.8 percent, have volunteered in New Haven at some point, and 21.3 percent currently do. Only 26.3 percent of respondents received at least eight hours of sleep on average, though 46.2 percent averaged seven hours of sleep. The majority of respondents, 54.2 percent, are graduating single, but 24.7 percent are in romantic relationships with other Yale students. 72.8 percent have had at least one significant other and 78.9 percent have had at least one sexual partner at Yale. Most respondents, or 53.3 percent, have used a dating app, with Hinge being the most popular. The Class of 2026’s drinking habits changed while in college. 90.6 percent of respondents have consumed alcohol, and 79.2 percent started drinking before turning 21, with 47.4 percent drinking before coming to Yale. Marijuana is less popular among respondents, with 51.7 percent never using it at Yale. Of the respondents who have used marijuana, 56 percent used it once a month or less, and 53.7 percent started before coming to Yale. Of the survey respondents, 67.9 percent never took part in Greek Life or independent social organizations, but of those who did, 72 percent have stayed with their group throughout their time at Yale. Societies are far more popular, with 71.6 percent joining one and only 10.2 percent dropping. The majority of respondents, 69.2 percent, also participated in Feb Club. Fizz was popular among the senior class, with 81.9 percent of respondents using the app while at Yale. Views The Class of 2026 expressed mostly positive feelings toward Yale. 91.7 percent of respondents would have chosen to attend Yale again, and two-thirds view New Haven favorably. 46.4 percent of respondents feel somewhat safe in New Haven, and 30.4 percent of respondents reported feeling very safe. Only 1.8 percent of respondents feel very unsafe. However, respondents harbor mostly negative views of Yale administration. 81.4 percent of respondents noticed a difference in administrative priorities over their four years at Yale, and 68.5 percent said they personally felt the impact of budget cuts in Yale College. When asked their views towards University President Maurie McInnis and Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis, 44.8 percent had an unfavorable view of McInnis and 40.5 percent had an unfavorable view of Lewis. The majority of respondents, 67.7 percent, are at least dissatisfied with this year’s budget cuts, and 30.4 percent are very dissatisfied. By contrast, respondents have fairly positive opinions on the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s recent proposals to improve public trust in the University. The majority had favorable views of the committee’s recommendations to make Yale more affordable, expand accessibility to Yale’s resources, broaden intellectual/political thought on campus and limit technology in classrooms — at 89 percent, 71.8 percent, 56.5 percent and 51.9 percent, respectively. However, 42.1 percent of respondents had an unfavorable view toward the committee’s proposal to confront grade inflation. Most graduating seniors who answered the survey said their political views have not changed significantly during their time at Yale. Overall, however, fewer respondents reported being very progressive now than before college, with 21.5 percent calling themselves very progressive now compared to 25.8 percent reporting that they were very progressive before Yale. Asked their opinion on President Donald Trump, 92 percent said their views were unfavorable. Future Destinations The future will take graduating seniors in a myriad of directions. Of the respondents, 64.5 percent will be entering the workforce after graduation, while 32.5 percent will be continuing their education. The most common expected starting salary, reported by 29.1 percent of respondents entering the workforce, is $45,000 to $75,000, followed by 22.7 percent reporting expected salaries under $45,000. Of those continuing their education, the largest group, comprising 42.6 percent, will be working toward a master’s degree, followed by 37 percent pursuing a doctorate. The majority of respondents, 80.4 percent, are at least satisfied with their postgraduate plans, and 38.4 percent are very satisfied. The most popular postgraduate destination is New York, where 29.7 percent of respondents are planning to move, followed by Europe with 16.6 percent and California with 13.8 percent. 90.2 percent of respondents are not graduating with student loans. The News wishes the Class of 2026 success in all of their future endeavors!

title: $7 billion campaign ‘For Humanity’ nears end amid new mission statement
subtitle: After Yale revised its mission statement to exclude “improving the world” and educating “aspiring leaders worldwide,” proponents of the“For Humanity” campaign said its priorities remain intact. The fundraising push is set to end next month.
author: Sara Agrawal
text: In 2021, Yale launched “For Humanity,” its largest capital campaign ever, with a goal of $7 billion and a “mission to improve the world now and for future generations.” Last month, Yale revised its mission statement to exclude “improving the world” and educating “aspiring leaders worldwide,” narrowing down to a mission “to create, disseminate, and preserve knowledge through research and teaching.” But proponents of the campaign said that its priorities — and their alignment with Yale’s broader mission — remain intact as it draws near its end date of June 30, 2026. “The university’s mission and the priorities of the campaign remain closely aligned,” Joan O’Neill, the University’s vice president for alumni affairs and development, wrote in an email to the News. “The campaign language highlights the benefits of investing in the university’s core mission for prospective donors.” University President Maurie McInnis’ end-of-year message to the Yale community last month included a link to, but did not address, the University’s newly revised mission statement. The changes in the statement follow suit of the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s recently released report , which recommended that Yale revise its mission statement to focus on knowledge. While O’Neill refrained from releasing details on the financial finals and distributions until the end of the campaign, the campaign had raised over $6.4 billion by the end of the 2025 fiscal year last September, according to its annual report . The campaign had raised $3.5 billion during its silent phase between 2018 and 2021, when University leaders solicited large gifts from a small number of wealthy donors. Randy Mastro ’78, a two-time deputy mayor of New York City and a donor to the For Humanity campaign, dismissed the change in mission statement’s impact on the campaign, reflecting that he found his own commitment to public service from his time at Yale. “Those of us who are alumni have to step up and do even more to make sure that Yale can continue to advance the values that have always been an essential part of the university, whether or not certain words are in a mission statement or not,” he said in a phone interview. Mastro said Yale’s values have remained its “cherished culture” for “many, many decades,” attributing the change in Yale’s mission statement to “the political reality of a federal administration that is hostile to the traditional mission of certain revered institutions like Yale.” The success of the campaign is “a reflection of the alumni rising to the occasion and supporting the institution at a time when so many of the world’s great universities that are here in the United States are under siege,” Mastro added. The For Humanity campaign began during Peter Salovey’s GRD ’83 ’84 ’86 tenure as Yale’s president. Since McInnis succeeded him in 2024, she has appeared at a series of events called “For Humanity Illuminated,” which have promoted the campaign in cities across the country. On its website, the campaign is organized into four umbrellas: “Arts & Humanities for Insight,” “Science for Breakthroughs,” “Collaborating for Impact” and “Leaders for a Better World.” The science priorities listed on the For Humanity website — data and computer science, quantum science, neuroscience, inflammation science and planetary solutions — align with the “top priority ideas” outlined in a 2018 report by the University Science Strategy Committee. The University’s particular focus on the sciences has also been reflected in the decade-long development process currently underway in Upper Science Hill , which was a response to the report by the University Science Strategy Committee. The umbrella entitled “Leaders for a Better World” includes financial aid. For Humanity has set a goal of raising $1.2 billion for financial aid in Yale College and in Yale’s graduate and professional schools. According to the campaign’s website, donors to the campaign can choose the school and initiative to which their donation will be allocated. Donors of $100,000 or more can create an endowed fund, according to the campaign’s gift policies . As of June 30, 2025, Yale’s endowment totaled $44.2 billion, according to the 2025 fiscal report .

title: GPA cutoffs for Latin honors inch even higher, setting new record
subtitle: The GPA cutoffs for Latin honors increased by 0.01 across the board from their record-high in 2024 and 2025, with the required GPA for summa cum laude — given to the top 5 percent of the graduating class — reaching 3.99.
author: Sarah Rivas, Aria Lynn-Skov, Leo Nyberg, Olivia Woo
text: Last year, the GPA cutoff for the summa cum laude distinction — which honors the top 5 percent of the graduating class — was 3.98, continuing a record high in Yale College’s history. This year, that number increased to 3.99. The cutoffs for all three Latin honors — summa cum laude, magna cum laude and cum laude — increased by 0.01 this year, reaching 3.99, 3.96 and 3.91, respectively, according to Paul McKinley DRA ’96, the senior associate dean of strategic initiatives and communications of Yale College. While students acknowledged that this year’s record-breaking cutoffs were the result of unprecedented grade inflation across the College, they stopped short of supporting grade deflation, which was suggested in a recent report by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education. “Summa is 3.99, which I think is insane,” Colin Kim ’26 said. “I think it just goes to show the grade inflation, first of all, here. But at the same time, I feel like, grade inflation or not, the fact that that’s the cutoff is a little insane, right?” Since 1988, Yale College guidelines have limited the summa cum laude distinction to the top 5 percent of the graduating class, the magna cum laude distinction to the next 10 percent and the cum laude distinction to the next 15 percent. The marginal increase in cutoffs for these honors follow a trend of grade inflation in Yale College. According to a 2023 report by economics professor Ray Fair, more than 58 percent of grades in the College were As, with nearly 79 percent of grades being the A- or A-minus range. Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis said in a March interview that these shares have remained “pretty consistent” throughout the past four years. Emmet Houghton ’26 said that the increase in GPA cutoffs is the result of having a large quantity of “really smart, driven people in the same place.” In its report last month, the Committee on Trust in Higher Education wrote that decades of inflation and compression have “rendered the college grading system almost meaningless as an academic measure” and recommended a “3.0 mean, or some other college-wide standard.” Lewis told the News a week after the report was released that University President Maurie McInnis had asked him to charge Yale College’s Committee on Teaching, Learning and Advising with “taking a look at that recommendation and, just in general, the question of grading.” “I’m sure they will take this report into consideration as well as all the other data that they’re gathering, so I expect to get recommendations from them next year,” Lewis said. Adam Tufts ’26 said that he thinks this year’s hike in cutoffs was “a pretty expected change” due to grade inflation, which he described as “definitely a problem” for the University. In an interview with the News, Gia Cabral ’26 said that while the effects of unequal distribution of grade inflation are frustrating as a STEM major, she did not view Latin honors as consequential for a graduate’s employment prospects. According to the 2023 report, seven out of the 10 majors with the lowest share of As and A-minuses were in STEM fields. Brunella Tipismana ’26 wrote in an email to the News that as a transfer student who has fewer credits at Yale than non-transfer students, a single A-minus has put summa cum laude out of reach for her. But Tipismana also wrote that “this relentless focus on the boogeyman of grade inflation is a superficial culture-war concern that draws a lot of attention in the moment but, in the long term, really does not matter to the real, existential questions the university faces today.” “The attempt to improve the public reputation of the university by deflating grades exclusively appeals to the sector of the professoriate (and the coastal elite at large) that lives and dies by op-ed’s in The Atlantic but that remains out of touch with the rest of the United States, which genuinely does not care if the average GPA at Yale is 3.8 or 3.7,” she wrote. Tipismana suggested that following record-high cutoffs for Latin honors, Yale should consider awarding Latin honors to “a larger share of the class like Harvard does” instead of making students “fight for crumbs.” Harvard awards a greater share of its graduates with Latin honors, with the top 5 percent of its graduates being honored with summa cum laude, the following 15 percent with magna cum laude and the following 30 percent with cum laude. Tufts also said that grade deflation could be “worse than the problem” of grade inflation itself, expressing concern about Yale potentially adopting Harvard’s recently proposed grade caps. Harvard’s faculty is currently voting on whether to implement a grade-cap system that would limit the distribution of A grades to 20 percent of enrolled students — with flexibility for an additional four A grades per class — in a weeklong email ballot set to close on Tuesday, according to the Harvard Crimson . “In terms of universality, grade caps isn’t something that every college is considering, so, a singular solution for Yale doesn't seem like the smartest thing,” Tufts said, adding that such a rigid system would make the College more competitive and hinder learning. Hannah Kim ’26 also said that what initially draws many students to Yale is its “very collaborative student culture” and that artificial grade deflation would be “counter-productive” and harm Yale’s future admissions. Jack Landau ’26, who is a first-year counselor, wrote in an email to the News that one of his first-year advisees expressed worries about their GPA and law school applications when deciding whether to take courses like introductory microeconomics, and he “hardly knew how to respond.” Landau wrote that he wonders whether the rising GPA cutoffs for Latin honors — as well as the “climbing median GPAs at top professional schools” — could deter students from taking harder courses, while he acknowledged that some challenging courses like Introduction to Ethics still have significant enrollment. According to enrollment data from Yale’s Office of Institutional Research , the class of 2026 has 1,628 students, placing roughly 81 students in the top 5 percent share. Aria Lynn-Skov , Leo Nyberg and Olivia Woo contributed reporting. Correction, May 17: Due to an editing error, a previous version of this article inaccurately paired the GPA cutoff numbers with each Latin honor. The cutoffs for summa cum laude, magna cum laude and cum laude are respectively 3.99, 3.96 and 3.91, not 3.91, 3.96 and 3.99.

title: As seniors skate off, a strong legacy on and off the ice
subtitle: The Yale men’s hockey class of 2026 leaves the ice after four strong seasons highlighted by memorable victories against rivals. One senior described the team spirit as a “brotherhood.”
author: Liza Kaufman
text: The six seniors of Yale men’s hockey have much to celebrate. They are the first in recent program history to defeat every ECAC opponent at least once during their four years. In the 2025–26 season alone, the team earned its first win over Quinnipiac University in seven years and first victory over Harvard since 2018. From their first season, the class of 2026 made an immediate impact on the ice. Captain David Chen ’26 played in all 32 games in the 2022–23 season, finishing with the second most goals on the team, while Bayard Hall ’26 and Kalen Szeto ’26 each scored their first two collegiate goals in back-to-back games. In the 2023–24 season, Jojo Tanaka-Campbell ’26 scored his first collegiate goal in a game-winning shot against Sacred Heart University, and Elan Bar-Lev-Wise ’26 potted his first, a shorthander, against Colgate University. The two senior defensemen excelled in their junior year. Hall blocked a season-high three shots against Quinnipiac, while Tucker Hartmann ’26 blocked four against Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute before netting his first collegiate goal the next night against Union College. This season, the team had its strongest start since 2010, upsetting Quinnipiac and Princeton, with Chen netting the game-winning goal in the War on Whitney. In his final career game on Senior Day, Bar-Lev-Wise scored. For Bar-Lev-Wise, belonging to the team extends beyond the ice — and beyond graduation. “We are more than just friends, we genuinely have a brotherhood,” Bar-Lev-Wise wrote in a text message to the News. “I truly believe they will all be my best friends for life.” The seniors’ team spirit, in turn, has proved to be inspiring for underclassmen. “Their work ethic and passion for the game pushes our team every day,” Hudson Miller ’29 wrote in a text message to the News. “They bring out the best in everyone and set the standard for what it means to play for Yale.”

title: Lone senior departs after setting example on and off the court
subtitle: Whitney Taylor ’26 is the only senior left on the Yale women’s squash team. After making a splash as a rookie, Taylor led her team through several constructive seasons, building a strong record both on the court and in the classroom.
author: Tanner Battle
text: Whitney Taylor ’26 is the only senior on the 10-player women’s squash team. As the only remaining member of her three-player recruiting class, Taylor has made steady contributions on and off the court, leading the Bulldogs through several constructive seasons. Taylor made an immediate impact when she arrived at Yale in 2022. In her rookie season, she competed at the No. 5 spot and boasted a 6–4 record in the regular season, while the team struggled with a 5–8 record. In her junior and senior campaigns, Taylor played at the No. 7 spot, recording a strong 9–5 record her junior season before closing out her collegiate career this winter. While the Elis went 4–9 in Taylor’s final season, they posted multiple wins against Ivy League opponents, Dartmouth (7–10, 1–5) and Columbia (4–11, 0–6). Taylor has also excelled academically throughout her time at Yale. During her final two seasons, Taylor earned the College Squash Association Scholar Athlete Award, which honored her outstanding commitment to both the Yale team and her studies.

title: After four flagging years under new coach, a lone graduating senior
subtitle: Two members of the class of 2026 left the Yale women’s basketball team throughout a four-year period marked by a new coach’s struggle to rally the team.
author: Azara Mason
text: Ahead of the 2022–23 season, three members of the class of 2026 — Kiley Capstraw ’26, Lilah Grubman ’26 and Lola Lesmond ’26 — joined the Yale women’s basketball team as rookies. Four years later, captain Capstraw is the lone graduating senior on the team. That year, the team also welcomed Dalila Eshe as head coach in 2022 following Allison Guth’s departure. In their first season together, Eshe and the Bulldogs finished 13–14. Both Grubman and Lesmond faced significant injuries during their first year, with Grubman sidelined for the entire season and Lesmond appearing in just one game. Meanwhile, Capstraw’s debut season featured three 20-plus point performances and five Ivy Rookie of the Week honors. During their sophomore and junior seasons, Capstraw started every game, leading the Bulldogs in assists in the 2024–25 season. Meanwhile, Lesmond returned from injury and made 35 appearances across the seasons. Yale notched eight and four wins, respectively. By the 2025–26 season, Capstraw was the only remaining Bulldog in her class. She continued to play a major role on both ends of the court, once again leading the team in assists while averaging 8.7 points and 6.5 rebounds per game. The Bulldogs finished the 2025–26 season with seven wins.

title: In a full-circle moment, seniors depart with a return to national stage
subtitle: After a 14-game win streak this year, the Yale women’s hockey team clinched the ECAC regular season title and reached the NCAA tournament for the first time since the 2022–23 season.
author: Audrey Kim
text: The eight seniors on the Yale women’s hockey team leave their program after cementing it as one of the top in the ECAC. The class of 2026 capped off their Yale career with a historic senior season, securing the program’s second ECAC regular season title ever and advancing to the quarterfinals of the NCAA tournament. The first time the program won the ECAC title was during the 2022–23 season, the seniors’ first year, which was also the year they last appeared in the NCAA playoffs. This year’s success gave the class of 2026 a full-circle moment on both the regional and national stages. “The senior class was crucial to our success this year,” Sophia Levering ’28 wrote in a text message to the News. “While some players played statistically large roles, the class as a whole set a culture rooted in dedication and commitment. They leave big shoes to fill but have also given us a great example of how to lead.” This year, the seniors led the team through a remarkable midseason turnaround. The Bulldogs struggled with early losses and fell in the national poll and conference standings. However, in December, the Elis began a 14-game win streak that helped clinch the ECAC regular season title. Yale’s national ranking secured the team an at-large bid as the eighth seed in the NCAA tournament. The Elis defeated ninth seed University of Minnesota Duluth before falling to first seed Ohio State University in the quarterfinals.

title: The class of 2026 raises the bar
subtitle: Led by captain Gigi Sabatini ’26, five seniors guided the gymnastics program through one of its most successful stretches in recent history, highlighted by record-setting team scores and four straight runner-up finishes at the GEC Championships.
author: Liza Kaufman
text: From their first time on the mats, the five members of the gymnastics team’s class of 2026 made an impact. In her debut collegiate meet, Hana Strause ’26 shined on floor and beam, while Gigi Sabatini ’26 posted the second-highest score among all vault competitors in her second meet. After Yale finished second at the 2023 GEC Championships, the team saw continued success. In 2024, Sabatini was one of Yale’s most reliable all-around competitors, regularly leading the Bulldogs on vault and beam, while Megan Brueck ’26, Meredith Donovan ’26, Lauren Holt ’26 and Strause consistently contributed across events. The class of 2026 started off their junior campaign strong. In the season’s opening meet, Holt emerged as Yale’s top performer on bars, Strause and Sabatini led Yale on beam and Brueck was the top finisher for Yale on floor. The team ended the season with its highest score at the Ivy League Championship. The most recent season proved no different as the five seniors continued to raise the bar. The team made history in its opening meet at Pennsylvania State University, finishing with 193.850 points — the program’s highest-ever overall score. Led by Sabatini, the squad continued to redefine success, closing out the season with a score of 194 points or more in nine meets. At the seniors’ final collegiate meet, Holt earned the competition’s third-highest score on beam to contribute to the program’s fourth consecutive runner-up finish at the GEC Championships.

title: Seniors depart after making historic splash
subtitle: In their final season with Yale swimming and diving, the class of 2026 led the men’s best Ivy finish in over five decades.
author: Walter Royal
text: Members of Yale swimming and diving’s class of 2026 enjoyed remarkable success over their college career, with record-setting races, NCAA tournament appearances and a historic Ivy finish. That success was highlighted by the men’s historic Ivy finish this spring — the team’s best in over 50 years. Having not won the Ivy title since 1972, the Bulldogs’ second-place mark was especially significant following a heartbreaking third-place finish at the Harvard-Yale-Princeton meet. At Ivies, a plethora of Bulldogs qualified for the NCAA tournament, including captain Alex Hazlett ’26 and Noah Millard ’26. Millard finished 10th nationally in the 500 yard freestyle, 16th in the mile and earned All-American honors to cap off his standout career. As a whole, the team finished 20th, becoming the sole midmajor program to finish within the top 25. On the women’s side, captain Jessey Li ’26 finished her Yale career with a gold in the 100-yard breaststroke at the Ivy League championship, while her team finished the season fifth in the conference. Caroline Riggs ’27 qualified for the NCAA Championship in the mile, where she finished 28th.

title: Seniors brought prowess and power to the court
subtitle: A trio of graduating seniors left an indelible mark on the men’s squash program, winning individually and contributing to team success all four years.
author: Tommy Gannon
text: The Yale men’s squash class of 2026 hit the ground running four years ago and has not looked back since. During their collegiate debuts in 2022, Tad Carney ’26 and Arav Bhagwati ’26 both swept top-ranked University of Virginia opponents, and Shreyas Mehta ’26 similarly cruised to victory in his first NCAA match a year later. As juniors, Carney and Bhagwati sat in the one and two spots, respectively, and helped power the team forward. The Bulldogs reached their first national title match since 2016 when they defeated Princeton in the College Squash Association semifinals in March 2025. This past season, Carney, who has earned CSA All-American First Team honors twice, captained the squad. Mehta and Bhagwati supplemented his leadership, and all three played critical roles both in maintaining the team’s winning culture and cultivating kinship off the field. “These three guys lie at the foundation of our team’s grit and dedication on the court, but they will be missed most for the friendship they brought to each and every member of the team,” Lachlan Sutton ’27, who will serve as next year’s captain, said.

title: ‘Take the note, but do it your own way’: Sarah Jessica Parker underscores individuality, curiosity during commencement address
author: Jack Baker and Taariq Ahmed
text: For those who couldn’t help but wonder what advice Sarah Jessica Parker might offer graduates during Northwestern’s 168th Commencement ceremony on Sunday, the actor and producer’s address at Chicago’s United Center focused on embracing individuality, curiosity and the golden rule. Before the “Sex and the City” star delivered these lessons, she highlighted the relationship she developed with the 7,805 expected graduates of NU’s Class of 2026 while crafting Sunday’s speech. “Sincerely graduates, even though we’ve just met, you have become, over these last few months, cohabitants of my life,” Parker said. “A great part of my waking thoughts. A constant distraction and a beautiful burden. In other words, you have become my children.” Parker was awarded an honorary degree at this year’s ceremony, along with energy researcher Michael Graetzel, award-winning actor and singer Heather Headley and president and CEO of the Association of American Medical Colleges David Skorton. Parker appeared onstage beside University administrators, including interim President Henry Bienen, who jokingly promised Sunday would mark his “last commencement” during an introductory speech. Introduced by School of Communication Dean E. Patrick Johnson, Parker shared wisdom with audience members, first conceding that advice is like experience’s “boring cousin” that “no one really wanted to invite.” Posing a series of what-if questions, Parker began her address by highlighting the difference between constructive advice and social pressure. She stressed the importance of maintaining self-identity throughout young adulthood. “As we say in acting, ‘Take the note, but do it your own way,’” Parker said. “If you can hold tight to your individuality, sustain a deep belief in who you are, what you want to say, I can assure you that it is a great thing to know how to belong to oneself.” Parker urged students to foster their curiosity, which she said is more powerful than the “beautiful prison” of comfort. She called the act of aspiring toward success “a gift,” referencing her upbringing as one of eight children in a family that typically struggled financially. “Despite the successes you are sure to achieve, material or otherwise, never stop wanting,” Parker said. “In wanting is energy and surprise and youth and motion, and not wanting is the resignation to complacency and inertia.” Criticizing those who view dreamers as lost in fictional worlds, Parker argued that “to dream is to have vision” and encouraged students to stick to their goals, regardless of any obstacles they may face along the way. She added that while she considers herself “a bit of a rulebreaker” and that some changes to societal expectations have been positive, students should always rely on the golden rule. “Do unto others as you would have them do unto you,” Parker said, eliciting an enthusiastic response from audience members. Finally, Parker delivered a what-if question for the world, centered on the difficult yet necessary struggle for change. She reflected on how she feels that “an illness born of fear and ignorance” has penetrated social and political spheres, negatively impacting people’s compassion and mutual respect. Nevertheless, Parker said she believes in NU graduates’ ability to work toward a better future. She cited the American Revolution as an example of positive change that affirmed the “rights of every human being are innate and universal, not granted as privileges by kings or courts or governments.” “You are a generation of remarkable, brilliant individuals who have experienced the world in a way in which no one else has,” Parker said. “I have one last what-if for you. What if together you broke free from the isolating tides that pull toward apathy and cynicism?” Lily Mitchell (Bienen ’26) said they appreciated Parker’s statement on prioritizing curiosity over comfort. They also enjoyed how Parker shared her behind-the-scenes experiences, detailing how she approached the assignment of crafting Sunday’s address. “I liked that she said that she had us in her mind ever since being asked,” Mitchell said. “I felt that made it seem like she really did care and wanted to be there, and this wasn’t just another event on her calendar.” Later in the ceremony, the first of two student speakers, Tram Nguyen (Kellogg ’26), recounted trips on the Intercampus Shuttle spent observing NU community members, including an elderly woman who participated in one of the University’s continuing education programs. Nguyen said watching the woman endure harsh Chicago winters just to “read books in community” every week demonstrated what a gift education can be. She highlighted her experience as a first-generation college student, adding she was the first in her family to complete high school, college and now graduate school. “So many people held a door open for me before I even knew I was walking through one,” she said. “My parents, the teachers who stayed after class, the friends who showed up, the strangers who were kind at the right moment.” Nguyen mirrored Parker’s message, suggesting that while some advice will help graduates grow, other messages will urge them “to shrink.” She added that “learning to tell them apart” will be essential as Sunday’s graduates navigate the next chapter of their lives. Mitchell said they appreciated the “relatability” of Nguyen’s speech, including when she joked about students’ common frustration with the University’s two-factor authentication system. They said her speech reiterated that as NU graduates move forward, it is important to recognize just how far they’ve come together. “I think the grad and the undergrad speaker did a really good job of bringing us together,” Mitchell said. “We’re all graduating in different things and going to different places, but we still all did have similar experiences throughout Northwestern.” Echoing Mitchell’s sentiment, María José Gómez (SESP M.S. ’26) said both student speakers “covered the whole Northwestern experience.” As an international student, Gómez’s family traveled from Colombia to attend the ceremony. Sunday marked more than just the conclusion of her time in Evanston. “It’s Father’s Day in Colombia today, and my dad is extremely excited,” Gómez said. “It’s a beautiful moment that culminates all this effort that I believe is not just my effort, but my family’s effort.” Undergraduate student speaker Sydney-Reese Harris (Weinberg ’26) recalled pleading to return home less than 24 hours after arriving at NU in 2022, crediting her mother’s “tough love” with giving her the motivation to stay. Despite her initial homesickness, Harris said she got involved on campus and eventually “met lifelong friends.” Together, they enjoyed unpacking “campus lore” at the now-closed Chili’s Evanston , an institution she called “gone but never forgotten,” garnering rapturous applause. Beyond those joyful moments, Harris said NU’s Class of 2026 has endured “one of the most tumultuous times in history,” calling the last four years particularly difficult for higher education. She said her support system was crucial in helping her overcome these challenges. “In those heavy moments, I leaned on the very Northwestern community I once doubted I’d ever find,” Harris said. “I urge each of you, no matter how the future of higher education unfolds or how you feel about Northwestern as an institution, to remember what you truly gained here. Not just a degree, but growth, resilience and a community that will last a lifetime.” Email: [email protected] Email: [email protected] X: @jdowb2005 Related Stories: — Q&A: ‘Sex and the City’ star Sarah Jessica Parker talks advocating for arts, upcoming commencement address — Class of 2026 marches back through The Arch, reflects on upcoming graduation — Schapiro Award winners reflect on teaching philosophies, influence on NU students

title: Reel Thoughts: ‘Scary Movie’ sells itself short, dilutes trademark humor
author: Miguel Tsang, Senior Staffer
text: This review contains spoilers. Horror movies are having a moment right now — no thanks to the Wayans brothers. Though scary movies may not be their forte, the “Scary Movie” series most definitely is. When I heard the 2000s parody franchise was coming back after a 13-year hiatus, I was excited to see how the comedic brothers would tackle the latest crop of horror movies in their new spoof. It seems like the Wayans forgot what made their comedic stylings so great, though. Almost as if I had wished for this movie with a “One Wish Willow,” it came with a pretty big caveat: the marketing. For whatever reason, the Wayans brothers decided the best way to sell a new “Scary Movie” would be to double down on its shock value. The movie’s tagline is “every line will be crossed.” Needless to say, a lot of lines were left uncrossed. The movie noticeably pulled its punches when it came to jokes that could offend its parent company, Paramount. Maybe now that the Warner Bros. merger has been approved, the next “Scary Movie” can be funnier. For all its faults, the movie opens strongly. A surprise cameo during the cold open kicks off the bits with a lot of energy, and once the horror parodies get going, they flow seamlessly. I appreciate how the writing managed to poke fun at anything remotely scary from “Sinners” to deep cuts like “Longlegs” and work in beloved cast members from the original films, especially mainstays Anna Faris and Regina Hall. However, most of the funny bits in this movie don’t get far without being followed up by scenes that made me wish I didn’t have eyes. A clever parody of the teacup hypnosis scene from “Get Out” gathered a lot of laughs and should have been an easy layup for a funny dream sequence. But what came next could only be described as a rejected Saturday Night Live skit. Shorty (Marlon Wayans) sings an autotuned riff on a song from “K-Pop Demon Hunters.” Just like the original song, the parody is unfortunately an earworm. It’s with no joy that I write that it’s still stuck in my head, and I’ve been sent more than a few Instagram reels about it. The original “Scary Movie” wasn’t a success because of its offensive humor or its references to broader pop culture. The movie was funny because it used horror movie tropes as a way to make fun of disconnects — both interpersonally and in the movie industry. There are glimpses of this satire in the latest movie. One standout scene shows an activist, woefully named Dei (Sydney Park), comparing the return of the killer Ghostface to last year’s botched remake of “I Know What You Did Last Summer.” In the scene, Dei’s friends stare with bemusement and have no clue what she’s talking about. Eventually, someone replies, “Are you sure you don’t mean ‘The Summer I Turned Pretty?’” It’s this type of humor that the movie was missing overall: using horror as a tool to poke fun at both Hollywood tropes and social divides, instead of compulsively trying to parody everything à la SNL. Horror and comedy have always gone hand in hand because both are all about tension and payoff. The comedy here has less payoff when it’s making fun of more than just horror. If a masked killer ever gives me a call and asks me for my favorite “Scary Movie,” I unfortunately couldn’t go with this one. title: Q&A: ‘Sex and the City’ star Sarah Jessica Parker talks advocating for arts, upcoming commencement address
author: Desiree Luo, Senior Staffer
text: Actor and producer Sarah Jessica Parker will deliver the commencement address to Northwestern’s Class of 2026 at the United Center on Sunday. Best known for her role as Carrie Bradshaw in the long-running television series “Sex and the City,” Parker has long been an advocate for the arts. The actor will receive an honorary Doctor of Arts degree at the ceremony. In addition to her acting and producing endeavors, Parker serves as a vice chair on the New York City Ballet ’ s Board of Directors and is the founder of the literary imprint SJP Lit. The Daily spoke with Parker ahead of the commencement about the importance of the arts, her young adult life and what students can expect from her address. This interview has been lightly edited for brevity and clarity. The Daily: What made you say yes to speaking at Northwestern this year? Parker: Fear. Challenge. Probably the most influential part in moving me to say yes is that I had a chance to visit the campus. I actually visited the campus when my son was visiting colleges, when he was in the application process, and we fell in love with Northwestern . He didn’t end up going to Northwestern, but it stayed in all of our minds. And then this past year I was graciously offered a visit by (School of Communication Dean E. Patrick Johnson). I visited again with my daughters, who are rising seniors in high school, and I was so touched to be considered as somebody deserving of time with Northwestern graduates. Like I said, I really did feel challenged and completely daunted by the offer. But I love the school, and I love the students I got to meet. They’ve been so hospitable to me and my family. So I just, I thought, you know, be terrified — that’s okay — try to summon words that are meaningful to students who have worked really, really hard and be worthy of their time. So I said yes. The Daily: What do you hope graduates take away from your speech? Parker: They could perhaps read into the very reason I gave for saying yes, if that makes sense, that there was so much about it that really terrified me, that it was an opportunity that I felt was hugely challenging and offered a lot of obstacles. It’s a hugely important day for all of those who are graduating, and I’m not a college graduate. I didn’t pursue higher education, so in many ways, I feel extraordinarily ill-equipped. But I think that’s a lot like stepping into your future as a graduate. You feel like you don’t yet have the experience, or something is really scary, or it’s unknown, or it’s something you never considered you might do. Some of the greatest triumphs and moments of satisfaction come from pursuing those things that are really intimidating, so I’m going to try to talk a little bit about that and then obviously some bigger themes. I mostly just want them to feel appreciated for everything that they’ve accomplished. The Daily: How do you think not attending college impacts your perspective on the day to day? Parker: For a long time, it kind of embarrassed me because I felt like there was a conventional kind of path to success, and I hadn’t followed it. I come from a very different generation where there was sort of a blueprint, and I was an actor from a very, very early age, so I just kept acting. I’ve been acting since I was 8 years old. But it has, I think for many years, really been a great provocation for me to be self-taught, to read all the time, to learn, to be really curious. I love conversations that are complicated because I keep learning, so there’s many ways in which I’ve tried to give myself the sort of well-rounded education I would have pursued had I gone to a great liberal arts college. It’s impacted me because it’s made me kind of work hard to fill in all the academic gaps that I feel were there for so long. They still exist on paper, but I’ve made it my business to be as educated as I can be and to keep learning. The Daily: What advice would you have for students who are hoping to pursue careers in the entertainment industry? Parker: My industry has changed radically over the last five years. We’ve gone from a lot of work in conventional broadcast television to streaming dictating the opportunities, but the business model around those is shape-shifting all the time to accommodate new technology. For me, it’s unknown, and for them, I would say find ways of connecting with your art. Meet with groups of people, read plays, create your own work as you pursue work by others who will hire you. Be ready in terms of the kind of preparation you can do in front of opportunities, and know that, as I speak a little bit about this in my remarks, it’s not linear. No career is anymore because it all keeps changing so quickly. But I want to stress because I believe this, and it’ll be hard to shake it for me, that the human contribution will remain the most important. The most profound contribution will come from the human. I know technology is racing ahead, but I believe — and this doesn’t just go to your communication graduates, this is to English graduates, philosophy graduates, history graduates, STEM graduates — books and conversation have to be honored because that’s where all the great ideas really come from. That’s where conflict resolution really exists and the most arresting, original ideas. I’m not going to be talking about technology in my remarks. I’m going to be talking about the human connection and how I believe in it. The Daily: What are your thoughts on this recent trend of commencement speakers mentioning artificial intelligence in their speeches? Parker: I’ve really tried to avoid listening to any commencement addresses so that I’m in no position of being ‘inspired,’ in quotes, by anybody else’s words because I feel like I owe the students my thoughts about life and pursuing a dream or an idea. I am not somebody who’s very conversant in technology outside of using my phone and understanding that there is this new way that is dominating a lot of conversations. I’m not conversant in that language, and I don’t come from the world of STEM. It’s very unfamiliar to me, but it’s hard to not understand that there has been a lot of chatter around AI being folded into these commencement addresses. I can only say it wouldn’t have occurred to me to talk about AI because it’s not something I’m relying on to do the kind of work I want to do, or not the ways in which I’m engaging in arts and culture and politics. I want to talk to the students about who they are and what they’ve done and how important they are as humans. Their interest can be in technology and can be in the digital world, can be in areas that I’m completely unfamiliar with, biology and physics, all the extraordinary things. But I want them to remember that it is their own contributions to themselves to getting to this day that are the most robust part of their accomplishment. It’s not a judgment call. It’s simply, I can only write about what I know. I can only talk about what has been a point of inspiration for me, and the things that have been most inspiring to me have been human beings, not the machines. The Daily: What book might you recommend for graduates? Parker: This is not why I’m recommending it, my hand up — there is a book that I published. It was the first book I published as a publisher. It’s called ‘A Place for Us.’ It’s a novel by Fatima Farheen Mirza. And it’s a book about an Indian American Muslim family in California, and all that pluralism, what that means. It’s an extraordinary book about a devotion to faith, honoring your family, trying to find your own place in the world. What can you leave behind? What tethers you to home and to people? Disappointment, class, economy. It’s an absolutely stunning debut novel. I always thought that it should be taught, that it should be part of an English syllabus, a literature syllabus. The Daily: You played Carrie Bradshaw for so long. A lot of viewers feel like you grew up with her and that they grew up with the character as well. Is there anything you think you learned from Carrie about adulthood or life? Parker: I probably learned about friendship from her in a lot of ways. She had the kind of time with friends that I have not had in my life, a sort of alternate universe of time where you can see your friends a lot and have a lot of brunches. It was the devotion she showed to friends. It was the stick-to-itiveness. It was the candor of their conversations, the way they took care of one another, the way they fell short and were told so by friendships. I love that, and I wanted that for myself. I couldn’t mimic it completely because my life just didn’t allow that, but I did. I loved playing somebody that had that kind of intensity in relationships that are so meaningful, and as she said, and the show said all the time, friends are the family that you make as young adults. The Daily: If you could get a do-over of the college age or that period of life, what might you do differently? Parker: This is the easiest answer I can tell you, and this is really true. I probably would have gone to museums more. I missed a lot of art. You make a choice. You don’t have a lot of money. In my case, I went to the theater a lot and the cinema a lot. I think I missed out on some pretty great art and exhibitions that just don’t exist anymore. I probably would have done that more. And I’m gonna give you a really superficial answer: I probably would have used sunscreen sooner.
